Attendees will gain insights on managing errors in their own work and others', fostering a climate of psychological safety where mistakes can be openly addressed and corrected within the SIOP community.

title: Understanding and Controlling AI-Assisted Faking - (Session ID 745)
speakers: Hilary Butera | Bradley Pitcher | Naidan Tu | Georgi Yankov
description: Authors: Yankov, G. P. (Chair) (2025). Understanding and Controlling AI-Assisted Faking [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Phillips, J., Robie, C., Bourdage, J., Christiansen, N., Dunlop, P., Risavy, R., & Speer A. (2025). Can ChatGPT Outperform Humans in Faking a Personality Assessment While Avoiding Detection? Tu, N., Nie, W., & Sherman, R. (2025). Detection of ChatGPT-Generated Responses in Personality Assessments: Techniques and Implications. Pitcher, B., & Yankov, G. (2025). Toward Detecting GenAI Faking in Assessment Center Written Exercises. Butera, H, & Baytalskaya, N. (2025). The Impact of Pre-Assessment Warning Statements on Generative AI use in Online Assessments. Dunlop, P., Holtrop, D., Oostrom, J., & Bourdage, J. (2025). Your Star Job Candidate Has Used Generative AI! Now What? Faking on preemployment assessments has been exacerbated by the rise of generative AI (GenAI) and freely available tools like ChatGPT. The 5 studies in this symposium explore the gravity of, detection methods/tools for, and reactions to AI-assisted faking. The first study reveals that AI can fake personality assessments. The next 3 studies propose effective methods, tools, and strategies to control AI-assisted faking. The final study explores reactions to AI-assisted faking and finds them to be mixed. The session is equally valuable for academics and assessment practitioners.

title: What You Need to Know Now: Hostility, Mental Health, and Vulnerable Populations - (Session ID 1234)
speakers: Lisa Baranik | Jennifer Dimoff | Keaton Fletcher | Michelle (Mikki) Hebl | Lisa Kath | Claire Smith | Rui Zhong
description: Authors: Baranik, L. (Co-Chair) & Kath, L. (Co-Chair) (2025). What you need to know now: Hostility, Mental Health, and Vulnerable Populations [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. SIOP members are showing increased interest in workplace hostility, mental health and vulnerable populations according to SIOP member surveys. This symposium provides reviews and updates on 5 major areas of I-O psychology: incivility, aggression, mental health, sleep, and vulnerable populations (i.e., minors, imprisoned people, and migrants). Presenters provide overviews of their topic, recent changes, suggestions for future research, and recommendations for practice.

title: All Aboard the Skills Train: Laying the Tracks for Skills-Based Transformations - (Session ID 1506)
speakers: Jennifer Acosta | Kristen Cooper | Paul Fursman | Britany Mills | Josh Rohlfs | Julia Walsh
description: Authors: Cooper, K. (Chair), Fursman, P., Walsh, J., Acosta, J., Rohlfs, J., & Mills, B. (2025). All Aboard the Skills Train: Laying the Tracks for Skills-based Transformations [Panel]. Society for Industrial Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. With organizations facing rapid advances in technology, shifting market demands, and frequent changes to candidate pools, many are transforming their talent strategies to focus on a skills-based approach. This panel brings together experts from diverse backgrounds to discuss the complex issues and strategies related to skills-based transformations. Drawing upon their personal experiences and expertise, the panelists will share insights into the tactical, organizational, and practical aspects of this transformation, providing valuable guidance for I-O psychologists and HR professionals.

title: Anticipating Destructive Leadership: Predictors, Correlates, and Related Factors - (Session ID 654)
speakers: Kristin Delgado | Elizabeth Grimaldi | Izz Aldin Hamdan | Amie Lawrence | Levi Sassaman | Joseph W. Stewart
description: Authors: Sassaman L. (Chair), & Grimaldi, E. (Discussant). (2024). Anticipating destructive leadership: Predictors, correlates, and related factors [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Lawrence, A.D., Guenole, N., & Sassaman, L. (2024). Implicit Measures of Toxic Leadership. Hamdan, I., Malter McLean, K., & West, K. (2024). Toxic Leadership: An Examination of Gender, Generations, and Hierarchy Levels. Stewart, J. W., Nandi, S., & Menendez, J. (2024). Leadership Gone Toxic: Relationships between Leader Personality, Versatility Behaviors and Team Outcomes Delgado, K.M., Stubenrauch, S., & Wehle, S. (2024). Leveraging NLP to Develop SJT Content for the Covert Measurement of Narcissism. Destructive leaders wield detrimental influence over individuals, teams, and organizations, with well-documented, adverse effects on attitudes and culture. To better understand how to mitigate the impact of these harmful leaders, it is critical to examine the conditions under which their behavior is most likely to occur. Collectively, this group of papers seeks to expand our understanding of destructive leadership by providing insight into the predictors, correlates, and related factors that influence destructive leader behavior.

title: Applicants With Disabilities and Interviews: How Can Interviews Be More Inclusive? - (Session ID 171)
speakers: Yumna Ahmed | Haya Bakour | Silvia Bonaccio | Avery Hughes | Deborah Powell | Jessica Wilkins
description: Authors: Bonaccio, S., Powell, D. M. & Dunlop, P.D. (Chairs). (2025). Applicants with Disabilities and Interviews: How can Interviews be more Inclusive? [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. This symposium presents 4 complementary papers investigating how applicants with disabilities experience interviews. Collectively, the authors focus on different interview types (e.g., face-to-face, virtual, and asynchronous video interviews) to understand how the features of interviews (e.g., presence of a live interviewer) facilitate or hinder inclusive experiences. The papers adopt varied methods (e.g., experiments, surveys, mixed methods qualitative thematic analysis). Finally, the authors consider diverse disabilities (e.g., autism, attention deficit, chronic pain, anxiety), highlighting the importance of parsing unique experiences within broader demographic groups.

title: Beyond the Model Minority: Challenges and Barriers Impacting Asian Workers’ Outcomes - (Session ID 894)
speakers: Duoc V. Nguyen, Ph.D. | Anmol Sachdeva | Winny Shen | Zhixu (Rick) Yang | Laura Yang
description: Authors: Yang, L. (Co-Chair), Yang, Z. (Co-Chair), & Shen, W. (Discussant). (2025). Beyond the model minority: Challenges and barriers impacting Asian workers’ outcomes [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. This symposium addresses the unique challenges faced by Asian workers in the U.S., focusing on stereotype-based treatments, including the model minority myth, which contributes to workplace marginalization. Authors share how these stereotypes lead to feelings of invisibility and pressure to conform, impacting professional and emotional well-being, as well as explore health and occupational outcomes, highlighting issues like dehumanization and burnout. Together, these studies advocate for tailored interventions and disaggregated data to enhance the work experiences of AAPI employees.

title: Deep Into That Darkness Peering: Dark Personality and Counterproductive Work Behavior - (Session ID 242)
speakers: Nathan Bowling | Reeshad Dalal | Tyler (Ty) Fezzey | Cody Harrell | Linda Muller | Chase Winterberg
description: Authors: Bowling, N. A. (Co-Chair), Winterberg, C. A. (Co-Chair), & Dalal, R. (Discussant). (2025). Deep into that darkness peering: Dark personality and Counterproductive Work Behavior. Counterproductive work behaviors (CWBs) have negative implications for organizations and members of organizations. Identifying predictors of CWBs thus supports theoretical and practical advances toward a better workplace. Much of the current literature has identified 5 factor model personality traits as predictors of CWB. Several emerging models of dark personality, however, may provide additional insight. The papers in this symposium extend the literature by dissecting the relationships among various models of dark personality, contextual factors, and CWBs.

title: Evaluating the Evaluators: Applied Research to Improve Human-Based Evaluations - (Session ID 633)
speakers: Jessica Blackburn | Gabi Danna | Kate LaPort | Georgi Yankov | Ashley Hanks
description: Authors: LaPort, K. (2025). Evaluating the evaluators: Applied research to improve human-based evaluations [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Danna, G. C., Johnson, L. N. W., Sinar, E., LaPort, K., Simonet, D., & Zhu, E. (2025). Evaluating interviewers: A job analysis and written feedback deep dive. Blackburn, J., Hallren, R., Huang, Z., Roberts, J., & Zhang, H. (2025). Feedback for the feedback providers: The development of a GenAI tool for improving feedback. Palmer, P. C., Himelright, B., & Hanks, A. (2025). Managing panel differences in public safety assessment centers: An applied case study. Yankov, G. P. (2025). Role with it: Leveraging LLMs to score executive assessment centers Human evaluators have the potential to introduce noise into the selection process. This symposium presents 4 applied projects assessing human evaluators in the selection process. Attendees will hear about an NLP-based deep dive into promotion document feedback writing, evaluation of interviewers and their feedback, strategies for accounting for panel differences in assessment centers, and LLMs’ potential to support roleplay exercise evaluators. Participants will share the context, research, and actionable recommendations from their work as well as answer questions from the audience.

title: From Silos to Synergy: Interdisciplinary Approaches to Strengthen I-O Psychology - (Session ID 579)
speakers: Charles Calderwood | Destiny Castro | Dev Dalal | Lori Foster | Hannah Perkins Stark | Elizabeth (Betsy) Shoenfelt | Rebecca Storey
description: Authors: Stark, H. P. (Co-Chair), Castro, D. M. (Co-Chair), Brossoit, R. M. (Co-Chair), Sasso, T., Storey, R., Calderwood, C., Shoenfelt, E. L., Dalal, D. K., & Foster, L. L. (2025). From silos to synergy: Interdisciplinary approaches to strengthen I-O Psychology [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Organizations face increasingly complex challenges that require insights beyond I-O. Presenters will discuss how I-O, in combination with sexuality and gender studies, learning science, exercise science, sport psychology, decision sciences, and other areas of applied psychology, can innovatively address issues with the greatest expected impact on the global workforce. This session aims to strengthen I-O research, practice, and impact by encouraging audience members to rethink their approaches to workplace problems through a broader interdisciplinary lens.

title: Insight to Impact: Translate Data, Insights to Impact Through Storytelling and Action - (Session ID 903)
speakers: Noa Besner | Shannon Bonner | Brittany Head | Jenni Knighten
description: Authors: Beechly, L., Besner, N., Bonner, S., Head, B., Knighten, J., (2025) Insight to Impact: How to Translate Data and Insights into Impact Through Storytelling and Actions [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Boston, MA, United States. In this 50-minute session, Perceptyx will present modules from “Analytics to Action,” a Master Tutorial highlighting strategies for translating data into impact. Experts Dr. Lauren Beechly and Dr. Brittany Head will guide attendees through 5 key learning objectives, including connecting data to business priorities, synthesizing key data themes, managing personal reactions to data, communicating results effectively, and creating sustainable implementation. Case studies from Coke Consolidated, NBCUniversal, and 3M showcase practical applications, followed by Q&A and discussion.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Nguyen, T. P. H. (2025). Award: Best of SIOP [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. This IGNITE style session features research conducted by 2024 SIOP grant award winners, including the Bray-Howard, Graen, Hebl, IRC, Zedeck-Jacobs, and Small Grant winners. Presenters will each discuss their award-winning research in 5 minutes, followed by an opportunity for audience questions and discussion.

title: Invited: COI - Strategic and Ethical Use of AI in Organizations - (Session ID 1689)
speakers: Jonathan Kochert | Pete Mclemore | Patrick Rolwes | Jacob Seybert | Ellie Tran
description: Authors: Kochert, J., Seybert, J., McLemore, P. B., Rolwes, P., & Tran, E., (2025). Community of Interest: Strategic & ethical use of AI in organizations. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Artificial intelligence (AI) has reached a pivotal moment, driven by recent advancements in user-friendly technologies, making it an increasingly valuable tool for organizations. As with all historical technological leaps, it's crucial to reflect on the strategic and ethical implications of AI use. This COI will present the current and future state of AI integration in organizations, as well as the ethical implications of AI use. Participants in the COI will share their experiences and research on AI's application and the ethical considerations, providing real world context.

title: Invited: COI - What's mine, what's yours? Ownership in Survey Follow-Through (Session ID 1681)
speakers: Roza Jankovic | Tim Amadore | Austin Seibert
description: Empowering leaders to understand what they can own and impact is vital for organizational change after an organizational survey. Industrial-Organizational Psychology practitioners—both internal and external—bring unique perspectives to help organizational leaders understand their roles in owning survey feedback and action follow-up. We’ll share how we guide organizations in identifying focus areas and taking action, clarifying the contributions of each practitioner type to support leaders in driving meaningful change.

title: Invited: Recognizing and Exploring the Working Class - (Session ID 1691)
speakers: Leah Bourque | Melissa Keith | Xin Wei Ong | Matthew Ng
description: Authors: Bourque, L. C. (co-chair), Ong, X.W. (co-chair), Keith, M.G., & Kish-Gephart, J.J. (2025). Invited: Recognizing and Exploring the Working-Class [Alternative Presentation]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Research in industrial-organizational psychology and related fields primarily studies "white-collar" jobs, neglecting blue collar, pink collar, retail, service, care, and gig work, which constitute 31% of the U.S. workforce (Gallup, 2024). This gap is problematic as working-class jobs are essential to the economy. There is limited research on improving job satisfaction and well-being in these sectors due to data collection barriers. Authors will hold rotating table discussions with experts and graduate students to address research directions and methodological issues regarding working-class jobs.

title: Mentorship for the WIN: Program Mentors, Mentees, and Organizers Share Lessons Learned - (Session ID 961)
speakers: Catalina Flores | Jensen Mecca | Ashley Stephens | Megan Stocks | Mia Tran | Aashna Waiwood
description: Authors: Stephens, A. (Chair), Flores, C. (Panelist), Mecca, J.(Panelist), Stocks, M.(Panelist), Tran, N.M.(Panelist), Waiwood, A., (Panelist). (2025). Mentorship for the WIN: Program Mentors, Mentees, and Organizers Share Lessons Learned [Mashup]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Effective mentorship plays a critical role in women’s career development. This interactive session focuses on the Women’s Inclusion Network (WIN) mentorship program, designed to create a small group environment for sharing expert advice, creating connections with SIOP members, and building a network of trusted professionals. This session brings together current and former WIN mentors, mentees, and organizers to discuss expectations, roadblocks, and how to make the most of a mentoring relationship. Participants will be encouraged to ask questions and share insights during this mashup session.

title: New Directions in Candidate Feedback: Collecting, Analyzing, and Actioning - (Session ID 568)
speakers: Hannah Siwik | Allison Besl | Christopher Cerasoli | Rachel King | Dan Simonet | McKenzie Specht | Dr. Stephanie Boone
description: Authors: Asbury, H., Besl, A. (Co-Chair), Cerasoli, C. P., King, R., Simonet, D. (Co-Chair), Specht, M., Swindler, S. (2025). New Directions in Candidate Feedback: Collecting, Analyzing, and Actioning [Panel Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. The intention of this panel is to explore new directions in candidate feedback. The experience of candidates who apply for a job has an impact on likeliness to accept, refer to a friend, and even subsequent success in-role. With the advent of new technologies and services, it’s time to revisit how I-Os assess the experience of candidates. Panelists will explore what has been done, what is new, and how I-Os should pivot to meet the evolving needs of organizations. The moderated session will engage the audience, leaving time for Q&A at the end.

title: Nudging Ahead: The Future of Personalized Organizational Interventions - (Session ID 825)
speakers: Kaytlynn Griswold | Rachel Omansky | Caribay Garcia Marquez | Jared Weintraub
description: Authors: Omansky, R. (Co-Chair), Weintraub, J. (Co-Chair), Foley, K., Griswold, K., Garcia, C. (2024). Nudging Ahead: The Future of Personalized Organizational Interventions [Panel]. Society for Industrial Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States This panel session will touch on what nudging is; ways to create and implement nudges to drive actions, tips, considerations; the role of technology and nudges in transforming the follow-up process; and new frontiers for nudging in organizations.

title: Optimizing AI Adoption: Measuring Sentiment and Productivity Across Industries - (Session ID 925)
speakers: Caitie Jacobson Mikulis | Kelsey Kline | Sami Nesnidol | Ketaki Sodhi | Jeremy Watson
description: Authors: Watson, J. (Chair), Sodhi, K. (Panelist), Kline, K. (Panelist), Nesnidol,S. (Panelist), & Jacobson, C. (Panelist) (2025). Optimizing ai adoption: Measuring sentiment and productivity across industries [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, Colorado, United States. As AI tools, particularly generative AI, become more common, companies face challenges in measuring their impact on employees, culture, and business outcomes. This panel brings together leaders from Target, Vaya Group, Intel, Dell Technologies, and Microsoft to discuss their approaches to measuring AI's influence on key metrics and employee experiences. Panelists will share best practices, early data insights, and offer participants a chance to explore specific areas, ask questions, and brainstorm solutions for their own organizations.

title: Pathways to Impact: Job Crafting Through Alternative Leadership Roles in Higher Ed - (Session ID 262)
speakers: Wendy Bedwell-Torres | Deborah DiazGranados | Lisa Finkelstein | Julia Fullick-Jagiela | Kiku Jones
description: Authors: Fullick-Jagiela, J. M. (Co-Chair), Bedwell-Torres, W. L. (Co-Chair), DiazGranados, D., Finkelstein, L., & Jones, K. (2025). Exploring pathways to impact: Job crafting and alternative leadership roles in higher ed [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. This panel explores job crafting through alternative higher education leadership roles, such as faculty senate, center directorship, student program development, employee resource group leadership, and executive education. It highlights diverse pathways for faculty to create meaningful work and positive impact in academia. Attendees will gain insights into job crafting, learn from leaders’ experiences, and discover strategies for implementing job crafting to enhance career satisfaction and meaningful impact.

title: Personality and Leading for Creativity: Looking Through the Reputation Lens - (Session ID 1074)
speakers: Robert Kaiser | Averie Linnell | Nadine Maliakkal | Kevin Mitchell
description: Authors: Mitchell, K. S. (Co-Chair), Maliakkal, N. T. (Co-Chair), & Hogan, R. (Discussant) (2025). Personality and Leading for Creativity: Looking Through the Reputation Lens. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Maliakkal, N. T. (2025). Creative leadership: A meta-analytic examination of leaders’ bright-side personality. Kaiser, R. B. (2025). Personalities of creative leaders and leaders of others’ creativity. Linnell, A. E., Reiter-Palmon, R., & Keller Hansbrough, T. (2025). Intimidating innovators: Follower perceptions of creative yet abusive leaders. Creativity is vital for businesses to survive, thrive, and compete. Though researchers and practitioners know the key role leaders play in attaining creative outcomes, several questions remain: What characteristics help a leader direct a creative enterprise? What characteristics help a leader facilitate others’ creativity? Do we expect these characteristics to differ? How impactful is a leader’s creative reputation? This symposium applies a reputation lens to unpack these questions regarding creative leadership, offering theoretical, practical, methodological, and future research implications.

title: Poster Sessions: Strategic HR/Utility/Changing Role of HR, Counterproductive Behavior/Workplace Deviance, and Teaching I-O Psychology/Student Issues/Professional Development
tracks: Strategic HR/Utility/Changing Role of HR | Counterproductive Behavior/Workplace Deviance | Teaching I-O Psychology/Student Affiliate Issues/Professional Development | Poster Sessions

title: Practical Solutions to Common Challenges in Impact Measurement - (Session ID 1154)
speakers: Heather Champion | Christina Fleck | Chia-Lin Ho | Jeff Kosovich
description: Authors: Ho, C.-L. (Chair), Champion. H., Fleck, C., & Kosovich. J. J. (2025). Practical solutions to common challenges in impact measurement [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Drawing on the collective knowledge and experience of practitioners and researchers in impact measurement, this session will explore common challenges in measuring the impact of learning and development initiatives; introduce three practical, under-valued solutions to help address those challenges; and demonstrate the application of the solutions with actual data and tools. Panelists will share insights to demonstrating the value of learning and development through impact measurement.

title: Show Us the Money: Need for Further Research and Educational Support in Compensation - (Session ID 784)
speakers: Lynn Beer | Krista Carter | Lisa England | Leah Klos | Sarina Maneotis | Julio Manso
description: Authors: Maneotis, S. M. (Chair), Beer, L., Carter, K., England L., Klos, L. S., & Manso, J., (2025). Show us the money: The need for further research and educational support in compensation [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Employee compensation is a critical talent management function and yet an area where research and education often leave practitioners underprepared. A panel of experts with a variety of degree types (e.g., MS, MBA, PhD) and experience (higher education, public and private sector industry) are assembled to discuss how they use research (or not) to inform compensation decisions within their work roles and how their graduate educations support (or did not) their ability to interface with compensation decisions and data. Implications for researchers, practitioners, and educators are discussed.

title: Surprising Research Insights & Practical Paths for Addressing The Social Capital Imperative
speakers: Kristin Cullen-Lester | Greg Pryor

title: The Impact of Nontraditional Work on Workers, Organizations, and Society - (Session ID 811)
speakers: Gabriela (Gabby) Burlacu | Russell Cropanzano | Steven Hunt | Amy Pytlovany
description: Authors: Burlacu, G., Cropanzano, R., Hunt, S., & Pytlovany, A. (2025). The impact of non-traditional work on workers, organizations, and society (an alternative session). Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Technological advancements, remote work models, and increasingly turbulent markets have created the possibility for more nontraditional talent models (i.e., gig labor, freelancing, and other modes of work that do not require a traditional 9-to-5 full time employment contract). This panel will bring together researchers and practitioners to discuss the current and potential impact of nontraditional work on people, organizations, and our workforce and society at large.

title: The Internship Equation: Intern and Supervisor Perspectives for a Successful Internship - (Session ID 628)
tracks: Teaching I-O Psychology/Student Affiliate Issues/Professional Development
speakers: Erica Barto | Ashley Biswell | Lindsay Bousman | Mary Chacko | Lesly Krome | Abigail Burt | Miguel Gonzalez
description: Authors: Chacko, M., Krome, L. R. (Chair), Cox, C. B., Bousman, L., Barto, E., Biswell, A., Gonzalez, M., & Burt, A. (2025). The Internship Equation: Intern and Supervisor Perspectives for a Successful Internship [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. The purpose of this panel is to share internship experience through the lens of the student-university-employer internship effectiveness model. Featuring pairs of interns and their supervisors, this panel will provide insights on the preparation, execution, and evaluation phases of the internship process. This session will present insights and recommendations tailored for 3 key groups: practitioners who lead interns, students in search of internship opportunities, and academics who assist students throughout their internship journey.

title: Understanding Neurodiversity at Work: Perceptions, Intersectionality, and Interventions - (Session ID 1477)
speakers: Debra Comer | Debora Gottardello | JeongJin Kim | Joie Magalona | Rebecca Mintz | Lida Ponce | Narda Quigley | Sarah Wittman
description: Authors: Ponce, L. P. (Co-Chair), Mintz, R. M. (Co-Chair), & Wittman, S. (Co-Chair). (2025). Understanding Neurodiversity at Work: Perceptions, Intersectionality, & Interventions [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO. United States. Recognition of neurodiverse individuals in the workplace has grown as part of recent efforts to improve inclusivity overall. However, stereotypes still abound. This symposium contains papers that provide updated perspectives on neurodiversity at work, expound upon the additional challenges faced by neurodiverse individuals with additional minority identification, and present interventions that may improve perceptions of neurodiverse individuals at work. The aim is to advance scholarship on the unique experiences of this group, with the hope of advancing workplace inclusion for neurodiverse individuals.

title: What a Difference an Election Makes: EEOC/OFCCP Practitioner Update - (Session ID 855)
speakers: Michael Aamodt | Joanna Colosimo | Laura Davis | Michelle Duncan | Dr. Katey E. Foster
description: Authors: Colosimo, J.L. (Chair), Aamodt, M.G., Davis, L., Duncan, M., Foster, K.E. (2025). What a Difference an Election Makes: EEOC/OFCCP Practitioner Update. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Organizations are under increased scrutiny from the EEOC, OFCCP, and private litigants concerning affirmative action, nondiscrimination, and DEI efforts. Federal agencies and contractors must adjust to shifts in political leadership and regulatory efforts at the science–law intersection. This panel will update the I-O community on EEO trends, current enforcement by agencies, DEI initiatives, pay equity, and AI regulation, with insights into potential impacts of the U.S. presidential election. Experts including legal professionals, practitioners, and employers will share their perspectives.

title: ¡Para la Cultura! Embracing and Leveraging Latine Culture in Industry and Academia - (Session ID 1136)
speakers: Aylime Bueno | Samantha Erskine | Jenn Feitosa | Bernardo Ferdman | Patricia Guerrero | Zytlaly Magaña | M. Gloria González-Morales
description: Authors: Guerrero, P. (Co-Chair), Bueno, A. (Co-Chair), Solis, L., Roberson, A., Delgado, P. (Panelist), Erskine, S. (Panelist), Feitosa, J. (Panelist), Ferdman, B. (Panelist), Gonzalez-Morales, M.G. (Panelist), & Magaña, Z. (Panelist) (2025). ¡Para la cultura! Embracing and leveraging Latine culture in industry and academia [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. The increasing number of Latine professionals in both industry and academia highlights the need for deeper exploration into their experiences navigating workplace dynamics influenced by cultural identity. Despite their growing presence, many face challenges such as cultural stereotypes and feelings of marginalization. These barriers often hinder their ability to fully express and embrace their cultural heritage while striving for professional advancement. This panel aims to offer insights into fostering inclusive, multicultural environments that support the identities of Latine professionals.

title: Opening Plenary
description: Kick off the conference at the in-person Opening Plenary featuring Scott Tannenbaum’s Presidential Address. description: Join us for an exclusive, invite-only Fellows Breakfast honoring one of the most distinguished groups within SIOP. This special gathering celebrates our newest Fellows while recognizing the contributions of this esteemed community. Enjoy a morning of meaningful conversation, connection, and engagement with fellow leaders and SIOP members in an intimate and welcoming setting.

title: General Conference Registration
AI-Assisted Applications: Rethinking How We Apply, Hire, and Connect (Session ID 199)
speakers: Kimberly Silva | April Cantwell Holland | Cat Yaris | Lauren Wegmeyer | Ngoc Duong
speaker_affiliations: Talogy | Harver | Capital One | PepsiCo
description: Silva, K., Cantwell, A., Duong, N.S., Wegmeyer, L., & Yaris, C. (2026, April 30 – May 2). AI-assisted applications: Rethinking how we apply, hire, and connect [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI is reshaping the way candidates search, apply, and polish application materials—and companies are struggling to keep pace. This session explores how and why job seekers turn to AI, the challenges it creates for employers, and how both sides can adapt. Panelists will share practical advice for candidates on using AI thoughtfully without losing authenticity, and for companies on evolving hiring practices to focus on skills, fairness, and human connection.

title: Blending Kirkpatrick and Psychometrics to Evaluate Leadership Development Impact (Session ID 577)
speakers: Stewart Desson
speaker_affiliations: Lumina Learning
description: Desson, S., & Cannon, J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Blending Kirkpatrick and Psychometrics to Evaluate Leadership Development Impact. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Evaluating leadership development needs more than ROI metrics. Two large-scale programs with Lumina Learning used an integrated framework combining psychometrics, experiential learning, and Kirkpatrick’s model. Among 72 leaders, results showed significant gains in reflective communication (+16%), resilience (+14%), and purposeful argumentation (+6%), alongside stronger inclusion and psychological safety. This multimethod, human-centered approach captures the true complexity of leadership impact beyond linear or financial measures.

title: Community of Interest: Bricolage in I-O: How to Do More With Less in a Changing World (Session ID 1432)
speakers: Jonathan Kochert | Matthew Christensen | Roza Jankovic | Kyle Benbow
speaker_affiliations: U.S. Army Research Institute for Behavioral and Social Sciences | Deloitte Consulting | Culture Amp | Army Research Institute
description: Benbow, K. L., Christensen, M., Jankovic, R., & Kochert, J. (2026, Month TBD, Day TBD) Community of Interest: Bricolage in IO: How to do more with less in a changing world. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Pressures from emerging trends are forcing organizations to do more with less. Are I-Os addressing these demands in a purposeful manner? The concept of bricolage provides a framework for addressing these trends. How can the systems and personnel characteristics that will lead to success in this environment be identified? How can the “do more with less mentality” be discarded and this as an opportunity to be adaptive, creative, and playful be embraced? This will be an audience participation session; presenters are eager to hear how attendees are/would respond to these trends.

title: Creative Micropractices: Cultivating Reflection, Connection, and Workplace Well-Being (Session ID1069)
speakers: Patricia Friberg
speaker_affiliations: Patricia Friberg Coaching
description: Friberg, Patricia (2026, April 30-May 2). Creative Micro-Practices: Cultivating Reflection, Connection, & Workplace Well-being {Master Tutoria} Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Relational well-being is recognized as essential for workplace well-being and overall human flourishing. However, many organizations lack practical methods to cultivate them. This interactive workshop introduces micro-arts practices, which involve short sessions, such as music, movement, drawing, or virtual museum visits, to promote reflection, empathy, perspective taking, and trust. Based on current research, this beginner-level, participatory session equips participants with actionable tools to integrate creative reflection into coaching, leadership, and team dynamics.

title: Decoding AI: Amplifier of Belonging or Architect of Inequity? (Session ID 989)
speakers: C. Malik Boykin | Christine Smith | Stephanie Tavarez | Taiwo Togun
speaker_affiliations: Brown University | The Culture Backbeat | University of Hartford | SeqHub AI Academy
description: Smith, C.R. (Co-Chair), Boykin, C.M. (Co-Chair), Monroe-White, T., Togun, T.A, & Tavarez, S. (2026, April 30-May 2). Decoding AI: Amplifier of Belonging or Architect of Inequity? [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI shapes how people are hired, developed, and heard at work, but are these technologies amplifying inclusion or quietly rewriting inequity into systems? This panel brings together academics and practitioners to “decode” how AI affects access, belonging, and trust in organizations. Panelists will examine the hidden assumptions within algorithms, discuss AI’s impacts on equity at work, and offer practical recommendations for creating inclusive and emancipatory systems. Attendees will leave with insights to help organizations align technological innovation with equitable culture.

title: Future-Proofing Selection Tools for Diverse Minds and Emerging AI (Session ID 649)
speakers: Ana Garcia | Viena Cao
speaker_affiliations: Cappfinity
description: Cao, V., Rowe, E.-L., Garcia, A., Mueller, S., Dovey, H., Deakin, P. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Future-Proofing Selection Tools for Diverse Minds and Emerging AI [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This intermediate-level session explores the dual challenge facing talent assessment today of ensuring fairness for neurodiverse candidates while maintaining assessment integrity in the age of GenAI. This tutorial explores how inclusive design and AI resilience can be addressed together, not in conflict. Featuring a real-world case study from Cappfinity, attendees will gain insights into reimagining traditional assessments such as situational judgement tests (SJTs) to be more secure, inclusive, and future-ready, without compromising psychometric rigor.

title: Generative AI Adoption: Competency Framework and Organizational Applications (Session ID 589)
speakers: Kate Bravery
speaker_affiliations: Mercer, A Marsh Business
description: Bravery, K., Srirangam, B., Agrwal, K., Golcha, S., Bailey R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Generative AI Adoption: Competency Framework and Organizational Applications [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Successful AI adoption requires not only technical skills but also behavioral and cognitive competencies. The tutorial will present the development and validation of an AI readiness framework measuring behavioral competencies (e.g. innovative mindset and openness to adopting new technologies), plus skills like proficiency in AI tools use, and cognitive abilities such as abstract reasoning. The tutorial will cover how organizations can adopt the Mercer AI Readiness Framework to support talent development and selection, fostering effective and equitable adoption of GenAI technologies.

title: Human-Centered Personas at Scale: Using LLMs and RAG to Build Personas Using Mixed Me (Session ID 1316)
speakers: Alice Choe | Diana Wolfe | Chelsea Homann
speaker_affiliations: University of Toronto | Avanade | Kyndryl | Open to Work
description: Wymer, C., Wolfe, D., & Choe, A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Human-Centered Personas at Scale: Building LLM-Based Personas Using Mixed Methods Data [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizations navigating disruption collect vast employee feedback, yet traditional qualitative methods struggle to scale. This advanced tutorial shows how large language models (LLMs) can be embedded in reproducible, mixed-method pipelines to transform employee voice into actionable personas. Participants will learn to integrate HRIS and text data via ETL and RAG workflows, apply grounded theory coding and clustering, and address ethical concerns through bias auditing and oversight. Materials and code will be shared to support adoption of scalable, transparent methods.

title: Interdisciplinary Collaboration Guided by a Case Study and Applied Exercise (Session ID1034)
speakers: Cristina Banks | Haley Cobb | Rebecca Brossoit | Ryszard Koziel
speaker_affiliations: University of California, Berkeley | Louisiana State University | Rice University | Saint Louis University School of Medicine
description: Thomas, C. L. (Chair). Banks, C. G., Cobb, H. R., Brossoit, R. Koziel, R. J. (2026). Interdisciplinary Collaboration Guided by a Case Study and Applied Exercise [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The landscape of the working world is increasingly complex. One way to adapt, make a positive impact, and thrive as a field is to embrace interdisciplinary approaches in work. This session uses a case study to provide researchers and practitioners an opportunity to actively learn about how to successfully implement interdisciplinary projects in organizations. Attendees will learn how to conduct interdisciplinary work by engaging in an exercise where audience members can compare their own interdisciplinary planning skills compared to a successful interdisciplinary case.

title: Leadership/Leader Development Posters
Moving Beyond Benchmarks: The Role of I-O Psychology Expertise in Total Rewards (Session ID 1271)
speakers: George Montgomery | Alec Munc | Nathan Bjornberg | Timothy Willis | Taylor Scotese
speaker_affiliations: Willis Towers Watson | Eli Lilly | Meta | Humana | WTW
description: Bjornberg, N., Montgomery, G., Munc, A., Myer, A., Scotese, T., Willis, T.. (2026). Moving Beyond Benchmarks: The Role of I-O Psychology Expertise in Total Rewards (Compensation and Benefits). Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will explore the significance of I-Os to the field of Total Rewards (TR). Panelists will share their applied and theoretical perspectives on best practices, challenges, and the unique positioning of I-Os in this field. Points will center around defining TR, the value I-Os can bring to these roles, the power of data-driven strategies, the impact of analytics technologies, and managing stakeholder resistance to change. The session aims to highlight the value of I-O psychology in enhancing TR strategies and opportunities for influence within this area.

title: On the Spectrum All My (Work) Life: Autistic Experiences Before and After Hire (Session ID 688)
tracks: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Cassidy Gaddie | Hudson Barthes | Mark Burnard | Jeffrey Olenick | Maira Ezerins | Sara Gutierrez | Tim Vogus
speaker_affiliations: American Psychological Association | University of Georgia | Marriott International | The University of Georgia | University of Central Florida | SHL | Vanderbilt University Owen Graduate School of Management
description: Barthes, H. (Co-Chair), Gaddie, C. (Co-Chair), Olenick, J. (Co-Chair), Burnard, M. (Panelist), Ezerins, M. (Panelist), Gutierrez, S. L. (Panelist), Vogus, T.J. (Panelist) (2026, April 30 – May 2). On the spectrum all my (work) life: Autistic experiences before and after hire [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session of academics and practitioners will present insights spanning autistic individuals’ employment experiences at critical stages of the work journey. Topics will include invisible disability disclosure, reactions to selection methods, the accommodation process, autistic burnout in the workplace, and how academic theories relate to lived experiences.

title: Pay Equity in Trump 2.0: Enforcement, Litigation, and Organizational Response (Session ID 539)
speakers: Lisa Harpe | Quenton Wright | Scott Kelly
speaker_affiliations: DCI Consulting Group, Inc. | Charles River Associates | Ogletree
description: Colosimo, J.L. (Co-Chair, Non-Speaking Contributor), Harpe, L. (Co-Chair), Kelly, T.S., Wright, Q. (2026). Pay Equity in Trump 2.0: Enforcement, Litigation, and Organizational Response. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores pay equity enforcement under the new administration, featuring insights from an economic consultant, labor and employment attorney, and I-O psychologist. Topics include federal rollbacks, state-level transparency laws, litigation trends, and organizational investments in audits and compliance. Panelists will share what employers are doing to manage employment-related risk. Attendees will gain practical strategies for navigating enforcement uncertainty and building defensible pay equity programs in 2026 and beyond.

title: Rethinking Generational Differences in the Age of AI (Session ID1086)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Use of expert judgment about minimal competence to distinguish between passing and failing performance is a bastion in I-O psychology. In the modern testing era, factors like expert time, test design, sample size, AI availability, and other factors influence how a test cut score is set and maintained over time. This fast-paced ignite session will feature speakers from internal and external I-O psychology roles who have set cut scores using a variety of methods. Though Angoff (1971) set the gold standard for cut score methodology, this practitioner focused session will feature methodologies for solution oriented professionals.

description: Global economies are conceptual economies—advanced by the quality of ideas. SMPY’s 50-year longitudinal study of thousands of intellectually prodigious participants (identified at age 12) shows how individual differences within the top 1% (one-third of the ability range) form contrasting constellations of specific abilities, interests, and values that predict consequential and creative outcomes in business, health care, the humanities, and STEM. The higher the abilities the more likely consequential outcomes emerge; interventions in adolescence provide a boost. In uncompensated ways, intellectually prodigious populations also contribute inordinately to the social good.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Dabdoub, A. & Gonzalez, M. (2025). Values and personality in context: Insights from healthcare workers and performance predictors in nurse aides. Loignon, K. (2025). How does education and experience impact leadership certification in nursing? Torres, K. (2025). Examining regional variation in nurse leader competencies. Meyer, K., McGonagle, A., Pindek, S., Reed, B., & Arvan, M. (2025). Understaffing, unreasonable tasks, and safety performance: A mixed-methods examination of within-person fluctuations. Four presenters will share research that bridges micro- and macrolevel perspectives to study performance in healthcare. Together, they examine who thrives through personality, how education and experience shape leadership certification success, whether regional contexts shift competency expectations, and when and why stressors undermine safety performance. An expert discussant will integrate these findings for an intermediate audience, offering actionable implications for advancing research and strengthening the healthcare workforce for practitioners.

title: Learning Together: Lessons From the Work Futures Initiative’s Sandbox Conversations (Session ID 1106)
speakers: Cristina Banks | Suzanne Tsacoumis | Tracy Kantrowitz
speaker_affiliations: University of California, Berkeley | HumRRO | PDRI Pearson
description: Banks, C. G. (Co-Chair). Thomas, C.L. (Co-chair), Loi, J.C., Gagnon, C., Kantrowitz, T., & Tsacoumis, S. Work Futures Initiative: Lessons Learned from the Sandboxes Discussions [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology 2026 Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. With the increasing complexity of the modern workplace, there is a strong need for interdisciplinary approaches to organizations. To do this, I-Os must first develop skills in interdisciplinary learning. In this session, authors provide research in the Work Futures Initiative as an example of how this can look and give space to collectively practice collaborative learning together. Lessons learned from 16 Sandbox discussions with professionals from diverse backgrounds will be shared and attendees are invited to try out a mini-Sandbox themselves.

title: More Than Salary: Snapshot and Historical Trends in SIOP Members’ Income and Employment (Session ID 1138)
speakers: Ahleah Miles | Caitie Jacobson Mikulis | David Tomczak | Fagana Samadova Stone | Nikola Fedorowicz | Shreya Cho
speaker_affiliations: Michigan State University | Insight222 | George Washington University | Atomic Research | Regeneron | San Diego State University
description: Miles, A. F. (Co-Chair), Jacobson Mikulis, C. (Co-Chair), Cho, S. (Facilitator), DeVita-Cochrane, C. (Facilitator), Fedorowicz, N. (Facilitator), Stone, F. (Facilitator), & Tomczak, D. (Facilitator). (2026). More Than Salary: Snapshot & Historical Trends in SIOP Members’ Income and Employment [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The Income and Employment Survey is one of the most valuable benefits to SIOP members. It provides snapshot estimates and historical trends of the employment situations of SIOP members, giving insight into shifting economic and employment contexts. This alternative session will include a brief presentation of 2026 results, including new special topics (e.g., RTO, AI, workforce management). Participants in the session will break into groups to discuss/request additional analyses/reports and shape future survey content. Insights from a salary negotiation expert will also be shared.

title: Moving Beyond Pigeonholes and Taking Flight: Advances in Autism at Work Research (Session ID 957)
tracks: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Andrew Lester | Caleb Phillips | Ludmila Praslova | Maira Ezerins | Natalie Longmire | Tiffany Johnson
speaker_affiliations: ISS STOXX · Full-time | London School of Economics and Political Science | Vanguard University of Southern California | University of Central Florida | Tulane University | Georgia Tech
description: Rosado-Solomon, E. H. (Chair), Phillips, C. (Co-Chair), Alaaldin, T., Ezerins, M. E., Johnson James, T. D., Lester, A., Longmire, N. H., Praslova, L., Stillwell, E. (2026). Moving beyond pigeonholes and taking flight: Advances in autism at work research [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Although organizations have launched dedicated programs to support autistic employees, there are still pigeonholes that not only constrain autistic employees’ growth but degrade their work experience and well-being. This IGNITE! session leverages novel research to confront the typecasts, stereotypes, stigmas, neurotypical norms, and barriers faced by autistic employees in organizations. Presenters illuminate opportunities for organizational change in work design, communication practices, talent acquisition, and leadership that enable autistic employees—and their organizations—to thrive.

title: Occupational Health/Work and Family Posters
Partner Showcase: Valence - Beyond Adoption Metrics: ADI's Power User Index for Measuring AI Coaching Effectiveness
speakers: Jennifer Carpenter | Ellie Wildman
speaker_affiliations: Valence
description: Organizations evaluating AI coaching typically measure adoption: logins, session counts, satisfaction. But does usage alone predict impact? This session presents findings from a study of 45,000+ AI coaching sessions at Analog Devices, a global semiconductor company with 26,000 employees across 30+ languages. Researchers developed a Power User Index measuring frequency, breadth of use cases, and conversation quality. Key findings: power users were 28% more likely to move up a performance band; manager usage doubled team adoption; and women were overrepresented among power users by 68%. Jennifer Carpenter (VP Global Head of Talent, ADI) and Ellie Wildman (Valence) will share the methodology and implications.

title: Proactive People Analytics Solutions: Addressing Tomorrow’s Problems Today (Session Id 844)
speakers: Aniqa Hatem | Colby Nesbitt | Danilo Le Sante | Yael Oelbaum | Zachary Rosch
speaker_affiliations: JetBlue Airways | Netflix | Google | JetBlue | The Farmer's Dog
description: Oelbaum, Y. (Chair), Hatem, A., Rosch, Z. I., Le Sante, D. R., & Nesbitt, C. K. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Proactive People Analytics: Proactive People Analytics Solutions: Addressing Tomorrow’s Problems Today [IGNITE] Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As organizations face pressure to do more with less, people analytics must evolve from reactive reporting to proactive problem solving. This IGNITE session showcases how practitioners leverage problem construction skills and predictive analytics to anticipate workforce needs before they arise. Through specific, organizational use cases, authors demonstrate how proactive, user-centered analytics products improve talent acquisition, employee development, and succession planning—delivering impact before problems arise.

title: Recruiting Research and Analytics: Tales From the Field (Session ID 519)
speakers: Nathan Mondragon
speaker_affiliations: ProboTalent
description: Simonet, D., (Chair), Gustavo, J., Mondragon, N., Reburn, Z., & Williams, Z. (2026). Recruiting Research and Analytics: Tales from the Field [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA United States. The goals of the session are to (a) share insightful applied research conducted across the recruiting and analytics space and (b) highlight different ways recruiting-focused research teams are structured and approach work within organizations, with special emphasis on contributions outside of traditional interview and assessment development areas. Each presenter will share a research endeavor that informs new directions for recruiting or selection practices within their organizations and will also describe their teams, the projects they take on, and how they use their research to drive action.

title: Religious Inclusion in the Workplace: Mega Threats, Accommodations, and ERGs (Session ID 853)
speakers: Annie Kato | Helen Chung | Denise Daniels | Humaira Hatif | Josh Nagel
speaker_affiliations: Seattle Pacific University | Wheaton College | George Mason University | New York State Unified Court System
description: Kato, A. E., (Co-Chair), Chung, H. H. (Co-Chair), & Daniels, D. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Religious inclusion in the workplace: Mega-threats, accommodations, & ERGs [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Despite growing employee interest in expressing religious identities at work, religion remains an understudied aspect of workplace diversity. This symposium features 3 papers that contribute to the understanding religious employees’ experiences, including the impact of mega-threats to religious identity, coworker reactions to religious accommodations, and the effects of religious employee resource groups. Collectively, this research offers new insights into how organizations can support religious employees and foster an inclusive environment for a religiously diverse workforce.

title: Righting the Ship: Reorienting Publishing Toward Solving Important Issues (Session ID 675)
speakers: Ryan Grant | Spencer Garcia | Charles Calderwood | Cort Rudolph | Fadel Matta | Lillian Eby | Steven Rogelberg
speaker_affiliations: University of North Carolina at Charlotte | Georgia Institute of Technology | Virginia Tech | Wayne State University | University of Georgia
description: Grant, R. (co-chair), Garcia, S. C. (co-chair), Calderwood, C., Eby, L., Matta, F. K., Rogelberg, S. G. & Rudolph, C. (2026). Righting the ship: Reorienting publishing toward solving important issues [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel addresses ongoing concerns about the state of theory in the field—specifically, the lack of theory aimed at solving real-world problems (Tihanyi, 2020). Authors unpack why theory building often prioritizes novelty and “theory for theory’s sake,” examine factors fueling these trends, and discuss how this limits the field’s practical value. Panelists will outline concrete steps editors and reviewers can take to shift incentives toward theories that both advance science and address pressing organizational challenges.

title: SIOP Special Session: Revenge at Work: The Psychology of Getting Even (Session ID 1447)
speakers: Nathan Bowling | P. Jewel Morrison | Thomas Tripp | Xin Wei Ong | Ashlyn Kurtz
speaker_affiliations: University of Central Florida | New College of Florida | Washington State University | The University of Akron | Clemson University
description: Morrison, P. J. (Co-Chair), Ong, X. W. (Co-Chair), Bowling, N. A., Kurtz, A., & Tripp, T. M. (2026). Revenge at Work: The Psychology of Getting Even [Invited Alternative Presentation]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference. New Orleans, LA, United States. Revenge at work doesn’t always look like a dramatic blow-up. It is distinct from workplace aggression as it involves action to restore justice to another party (Bies & Tripp, 2005). In today’s organizations, it often hides in the shadows: quiet quitting, malicious compliance, withholding information, or subtle sabotage towards the company or coworkers (Alter, 2015; Mahand & Caldwell, 2023). This rotating roundtable session explores how employees attempt to restore justice through retaliatory behaviors—and what that reveals about the “failure of workplace systems to offer meaningful repair."

title: Supporting Targets of Sexual Harassment in the Workplace (Session ID 590)
speakers: Hannah Stockdale | Yijue Liang | Dr. Vicki J. Magley | Megan Korsak | Harshit (Harry) Chaubey
speaker_affiliations: George Mason University | University of Connecticut | University of Texas at Arlington | Women in Work Lab, IU Indianapolis
description: Stockdale, J. (Co-Chair) & Liang Y. (Co-Chair), Magley, V. J. (Discussant). (2026). Supporting targets of sexual harassment in the workplace [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United State Chaubey, H., Stockdale, M. S., Kiebler, J., Kimble-Hill, A.C. (2026). Enhancing sexual harassment training outcomes through empathy training: A comparison of modalities Korsak, M. A., Bernard, L., & Martinez, L. R. (2026). A trauma-informed approach to managing sexual harassment incidents at work Liang, Y., Sun, T., McCauley, R., & Zhu, Z. (2026). Access to collective representation, customer sexual harassment and gig work Sexual harassment in the workplace has detrimental effects on targets’ well-being and work outcomes, yet little research has examined how to mitigate these impacts when harassment occurs. This symposium aims to advance scholarly understanding of how to better support sexual harassment targets. Empirical and conceptual papers highlight different approaches—training programs, organizational trauma-informed approaches, and collective representation—that can provide critical support for targets across various work settings.

title: Telling Your Story: Translating and Communicating I-O Skills (Session ID 958)
speakers: Chanda Murphy | Courtney Keim | Ashley Nixon | David Earnest | Julianne Brown-Meola | Adriane Sanders
speaker_affiliations: Mindful in Memphis | Bellarmine University | Willamette University | Austin Peay State University | Bath & Body Works
description: Keim, A.C. (Co-Chair), Murphy, C. (Co-Chair), Anderson, Z., Brown, J., Earnest, D.R., Nixon, A.E., & Sanders, A.M.F. (2026). Telling your story: Translating & Communicating I-O Skills [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychology professionals and students must overcome a translation barrier between the technical language of SIOP competencies (e.g., psychometrics, job analysis) and the NACE career readiness competencies (e.g., communication, leadership) often used by employers. This panel of academics and practitioners will engage in a lively discussion on how to translate I-O expertise to bridge the gap, ensuring graduates and early career folks are marketable, able to advance through generative AI screening processes, and recognized for their valuable talent.

title: The AI Scorecard 2.0: AI’s Potential Beyond Efficiency (Session ID 977)
speakers: Katherine Gibbard | Andrew Evans | Caitlynn Sendra | Gabriela Burlacu | Lindsay Horst | Dr. Neil Morelli
speaker_affiliations: SAP SuccessFactors | Upwork | Plexus Corp. | Salesforce
description: Gibbard, K. (Chair), Sendra, C., Horst, L., Morelli, N., Burlacu, G., Evans, A. (2026). The AI Scorecard 2.0: AI’s potential beyond Efficiency [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI adoption is accelerating across organizations, however business gains are often underwhelming and only measured as time savings. This narrow measuring of AI impact only in terms of efficiency risks overlooking critical dimensions of organizational health, employee experience, and people sustainability. In this session, applied researchers and practitioners will present practice-based insights into how AI is shaping the workplace and discuss why organizations must measure the people impacts of AI solutions to capture business gains, and I-O psychologists can lead this charge.

title: “So, Are You a Workplace Therapist?”: Marketing I-O to Organizations (Session ID 815)
speakers: Ahmed Kurtom | Brian Lewis | Emilija Kovacevic | Rose Fonseca | Samantha January | Vanessa Gaskins
speaker_affiliations: Design Force | CUNY Graduate Center/Baruch College | Spellman High Voltage Electronics Corporation | DICK'S Sporting Goods | HP Inc.
description: Lewis, B.A (Chair), Kovacevic, E. (Co-Chair), Liu, S.-Y. S. (Co-Chair), Novak, G. (Co-Chair), Dziuba-Liu, S.-N. C. (Co-Chair), Balangue, V. (Panelist), Fonseca, R. (Panelist), Gaskins, V. (Panelist), January, S. (Panelist), & Kurtom, A. (Panelist) (2026). “So, Are You a Workplace Therapist?”: Marketing I-O to Organizations [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychology plays a vital role in improving workplace outcomes, yet the field remains underrecognized in much of the business world. This alternative session explores how I-O practitioners can better communicate the value of the field to organizational leaders. This session highlights real-world strategies, challenges, and lessons learned in marketing I-O within organizations. The session will provide practical guidance for both practitioners and students seeking to increase the visibility and influence of I-O psychology in their work contexts.

title: Midday Break
Book Lunch
description: Bring your lunch and join fellow attendees for a conversation about the books that have inspired, deepened, or transformed your practice. Share your favorite titles—and feel free to include impactful models, memorable diagrams, or even a cheeky podcast or two. It’s a gathering for bibliophiles, big thinkers, and curious minds.

title: SIOP in Space!
speakers: Peter Reiley | Lauren Landon | Sydney Begerowski | Tevin Barker
speaker_affiliations: Leadership Arts & Sciences | NASA (KBR) | NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration · Full-time
description: “SIOP in Space!” explores how I-O psychologists are shaping NASA missions and U.S. military space launch programs. Panelists bring decades of experience across research, HR, and mission operations, applying I-O to astronaut team effectiveness, team dynamics in extreme environments, AI-enabled team science, and mission-critical training. This career showcase session highlights how I-O drives performance in high-stakes space operations and invites you to rethink where your expertise can take you.

title: Assessing the Assessors: Best Practices for Talent Assessment RFPs (Session ID 447)
speakers: Andrew Solomonson | Connor Eichenauer | Eric Brasher | James Scrivani | Anne Scaduto | Sean Robinson | Victoria Stage
speaker_affiliations: Delta Air Lines | GE Aerospace | JMA Wireless | Chevron
description: Eichenauer, C. J. (Co-Chair), Scaduto, A. (Co-Chair), Scrivani, J. A., Solomonson, A., Brasher, E., Robinson, S., Stage, V. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Assessing the Assessors: Best Practices for Talent Assessment RFPs [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In this session, experienced internal talent assessment leaders will each briefly share an interesting case study about a recent request for proposal (RFP) they led for an assessment solution. Presenters will summarize their end-to-end RFP process (e.g., how they searched for vendors, information they sought, how they evaluated proposals and made decisions), share best practices and key lessons learned, and respond to audience questions. In addition to providing value for fellow internal practitioners, this session will also be insightful for those employed by external assessment vendors.

title: Auditing DEI Practices for Effectiveness and Legal Compliance (Session ID 1198)
speakers: Dan Kuang | Brian Marentette | Lynn Clements | Peggy Stockdale
speaker_affiliations: Resolution Economics | Berkshire Associates
description: Kuang, D. (Chair), Clements, L., Marentette, B., Stockdale, M. (2026). Auditing Your DEI Practices for Effectiveness and Legal Compliance. [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel offers critical insights into the evolving legal landscape of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) programs after Executive Order 14173, which emphasizes merit and seeks to eliminate unlawful DEI practices. Panelists will share strategies for evaluating DEI programs through a multidisciplinary lens. I-Os need to understand best practices for qualitative policy reviews that align with current law, quantitative audit techniques that evaluate the implementation of written policies through workforce analytics, and guidance for cultivating a legally defensible DEI culture.

From science to impact—Shaping the future of work through I-O excellence [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This IGNITE! session features recipients of 7 awards who are making contributions to the future of work through I-O psychology.

title: Coaching as a Tool for Thriving: Unlocking Growth, Resilience, and Well-Being at Work (Session ID 313)
speakers: Allie Gabriel | Brodie Riordan | Ali O'Malley, PhD, PCC | Darlene Thompson | Shonna Waters
speaker_affiliations: Purdue University, Mitch Daniels School of Business | Ocular, Georgetown University | Reflexion Group LLC | RiseOn Talent | Fractional Insights
description: Gabriel, A. S. (co-chair and panelist), Riordan, B. G. (co-chair and panelist), O’Malley, A. L. (facilitator), Thompson, D. J. (panelist), & Waters, S. D. (panelist). Coaching as a tool for thriving: Unlocking growth, resilience, and well-being at work. Session submitted to the 2026 SIOP Conference, New Orleans, LA. Employees today must adapt to extraordinary volatility, often pushed to their cognitive, emotional, and physical limits. Surviving in a chronically hypervigilant, activated, or exhausted state is unsustainable for both people and organizations. One high-impact tool for shifting from surviving to thriving is coaching. In this session, academics and practitioners who have studied and practiced coaching and thriving for combined 70+ years convene. This session will illuminate the impact that coaching has for individuals and organizations for promoting thriving, well-being, and also performance.

title: Community of Interest: Designing Neuroinclusive Pathways From Classroom to Workplace (Session ID 1456)
tracks: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility | Special Session
speakers: Juliette (Dr. Nelson) Nelson | Nicholas Smith | Anthony Ruffin | Sophie Kay | Cristina Giannantonio | Larry Martinez | Megan Korsak

description: Nelson, J. (chair), Smith, N. A. (chair), Korsak, M., Hurley-Hanson, A., Martinez, L. R., Kay, S., Ruffin, A., & Giannantonio, C. (2026). Designing neuroinclusive pathways from classroom to workplace. [Community of Interest]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This COI invites educators, practitioners, researchers, and students to share strategies and learn from one another to explore how mentoring and teaching practices in I-O education can prepare neurodivergent students to navigate environments void of psychological safety and thrive in organizational contexts. Attendees will exchange insights and best practices on how to build inclusive environments of support. There will be a small panel to initiate the discussion and then small groups for collaborative learning.

title: Effective and Ineffective Sources of Support for Employees With Disabilities (Session ID 898)
tracks: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Alecia Santuzzi | Andrew Davidson | Lauren Luchetti | Lynette (Herbert) Killian | Melanie Fountaine | Valentina Bruk-Lee
speaker_affiliations: Northern Illinois University | DePaul University | Neuro Consulting Solutions | St. Cloud State University | Florida International University
description: Santuzzi, A. M. (Chair), Bruk-Lee, V., Davidson, A., Fountaine, M., Herbert, L., & Luchetti, L. F. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Effective and Ineffective Sources of Support for Employees with Disabilities. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Employees with disabilities continue to face known and hidden barriers to success in employment. However, limited empirical guidance is available to confidently select support strategies for these employees. This panel will highlight insights into the effective and ineffective sources of support for employees with disabilities. The panel will draw on literature across disciplines and practitioner experiences to identify science–practice gaps and provide direction for priority research endeavors and updates to workplace practices to effectively support employees with disabilities.

title: Executive and Corporate Ethics at the Organizational, Team, and Individual Levels (Session ID 765)
tracks: Organizational Processes/Development/Culture | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Ellie Tran | Kortney Kosak-Koberna | Sampoorna Nandi | Robert Lewis | Wesley Johnson | Kasey Guentert

description: Tran, E. H. (Co-Chair), Kosak-Koberna, K. (Co-Chair), & Nandi, S. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Executive and Corporate Ethics at the Organizational, Team, and Individual Levels [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Johnson, W. N. (2026). Corporate Social Responsibility and Employee Psychological Outcomes: A Review of Experimental Evidence. Tran, E. H., Martín-Raugh, M. P., Watts, L. L. (2026). The Role of Champions, Deviants, and Moral Intensity in Shaping Ethical Decisions in Teams. Lewis, R. E., Guentert, K., Magill, M. (2026). Predicting Ethical Leadership Behavior: Linking Simulation Outcomes with Personality and 360 Data. Ethics unfolds across multiple layers of organizations. This 50-minute symposium brings together 3 papers that illustrate these layers, offering novel insights into how ethics is shaped across various organizational levels. The first synthesizes experiments on how corporate social responsibility shapes employees’ pride, identification, and values; the second examines how ethical role models and moral intensity influence team decisions; and the third investigates leaders’ individual responses to ethical challenges. Together, they highlight practical ways to manage ethics across organizational levels.

title: From Awareness to Action: Tackling Toxic Leadership Through Research and Practice (Session ID 769)
speakers: C. Allen Gorman | Laura Hambley Lovett | Paul McCarthy | Ruoyan (Renee) Pi

From Awareness to Action: Tackling Toxic Leadership Through Research and Practice [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Toxic leadership remains a pervasive and costly issue in today’s workplaces. This IGNITE! Panel of thought leaders and researchers highlights current research and practical strategies for detecting, measuring, and mitigating toxic leadership. Presenters will share findings from North American studies revealing both overt and covert toxic behaviors and demonstrating how evidence-based frameworks and interventions can bridge the gap between research and practice to create healthier, more accountable organizations.

title: From Ivory Tower to Office Tower: Turning I-O Research Into Real-World Impact (Session ID 430)

This Shark Tank-style alternative session will push attendees to practice their scientific communication skills in a competitive, fast-paced, and enriching environment. In this interactive setting, groups of participants will briefly pitch I-O research insights to a panel of expert judges. Attendees will be able to refine their abilities to communicate with various stakeholder audiences. This session is ideal for graduate students, academics, and practitioners at any career stage seeking to improve their scientific communication skills and get real-time feedback in a supportive environment.

title: Is the Future of Strategic Workforce Planning Skills-Based? (Session ID 312)
speakers: Lauren Park | Angela Le Mathon | Aaron Sorensen | Cole Napper | Robert Motion

Is the Future of Strategic Workforce Planning Skills-Based? [Debate]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In an era when organizations have more and better skills data than ever before, questions remain about how such data should be incorporated into strategic workforce planning practices. This debate will highlight 2 perspectives—a skills-based perspective and a skills-included perspective—and seek to uncover future best practices through their discussion.

title: Large Language Models as Methodological Partners in Organizational Research (Session ID 700)
speakers: Hanyi Min | Hannah Walker | Andrew Speer | Joe Bradley | Levi Sassaman

Large Language Models as Methodological Partners in Organizational Research [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The application of artificial intelligence (AI) and large language models (LLMs) in research design has received increased attention. This symposium highlights how LLMs can serve researchers as methodological tools capable of enhancing current practices and techniques while improving the efficiency of otherwise laborious tasks.

title: More Data, Less Insight? Why So Many Leaders Still Lack Self-Awareness (Session Id 536)
speakers: Ryan Rush | Cody Warren | Kyla Holcombe | Martin Lanik
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessment Systems | Hogan Assessments | Visa | Pinsight
description: Rush, R., Warren, C., Malter McLean, K., Lanik, M., & Holcombe, K. (2026). More data, less insight? Why so many leaders still lack self-awareness [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Despite abundant access to feedback tools and assessment data, research indicates leadership self-awareness has not meaningfully improved in 2 decades. Panelists explore personality traits and cognitive patterns that make leaders more likely to develop self-awareness, focusing on factors that can be developed through targeted interventions. Drawing from research-backed best practices and real-world experiences across diverse organizational contexts, the discussion addresses critical questions around feedback receptivity and evidence-based approaches to building leadership self-awareness.

Deskless-first listening strategies for frontline industries [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will explore why contemporary employee listening strategies often leave out deskless workers and how organizations can build systems for employee sensing that are designed with the deskless worker in mind. The moderator will facilitate discussion among senior employee listening research scientists and leaders from organizations including Ford, Target, Boeing, and Albertsons on lessons learned and practical approaches for developing strategies to more effectively capture the day-to-day experience of frontline employees.

title: Organizational Agility and Resilience: A Framework for Thriving in Exponential Change (Session ID 1336)
speakers: Rachel Frautschy DeMuth | Tom Braun | Olya Taran | Roger Cude
