description: Braun, T. J. (Presenter), Cude, R. L. (Presenter), DeMuth, R. L. F. (Presenter), & Taran, O. A. (Presenter). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Organizational Agility and Resilience: A Framework for Thriving in Exponential Change [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This intermediate/advanced session addresses unprecedented organizational transformation—companies now implement 9+ major changes annually versus 2 pre-2020—whereas traditional approaches yield disappointing results. Participants explore a framework positioning agility and resilience as distinct yet interdependent capacities essential for thriving amid perpetual change. Moving beyond episodic models, presenters define core constructs, outline a conceptual model, and discuss tools for application. Participants actively apply the framework to real-world scenarios and share insights.

title: Partner Showcase: CodeSignal - Amplifying I-O with AI: Scaling Simulation Best Practices
speakers: Seterra Riggs | Nathan Hundley
speaker_affiliations: CodeSignal
description: Work simulations are among the strongest predictors of job performance, yet for non-technical roles they have historically been difficult to scale. Unlike coding simulations, which can be delivered and scored automatically, non-technical simulations often rely on assessment center methods such as roleplays, behavioral interviews, written exercises, and human raters. These approaches are job-relevant and effective, but also high-touch, labor-intensive, and typically reserved for executive or other high-value hiring contexts. This presentation explores how AI can serve as a force multiplier for industrial-organizational psychologists, scaling assessment best-practices without abandoning rigor that underpins them. Working in lockstep with I-O expertise, AI agents can be trained as role players, structured interviewers, and rubric-based graders using the same principles I-O practitioners have long applied to human assessors. We will discuss how AI, guided by I-O science, can support realistic, standardized, and scalable simulations for non-technical roles, and share emerging research from CodeSignal’s AI simulation products.

title: Reimagining Enterprise Assessment: Overcoming Barriers to Strategic Alignment (Session ID 840)
speakers: Courtney Deyulio | Alec Campbell | Jan Harbaugh | Julia Walsh | Lauren Lilly | Timothy Bartkoski

Reimagining Enterprise Assessment: Overcoming Barriers to Strategic Alignment [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel brings together internal assessment leaders from Fortune 500 companies and top experts from premier consulting firms to offer a rare, inside look at how the world’s most influential organizations are transforming talent assessment at scale. Panelists will reveal the obstacles they’ve faced in pursuing enterprise-wide alignment, the innovative strategies that helped them overcome those obstacles, and the measurable impact on business performance and workforce potential. Attendees will leave with actionable strategies and real-world lessons not found in existing literature.

This symposium explores how social and science identities serve as foundations for resilience and persistence across educational and workplace contexts. Through longitudinal and qualitative approaches, the 4 papers examine predictors of STEM success, science identity development, and the lived experiences of those who persist despite adversity.

description: Morrison , P. J. (Co-Chair), Vaidya, H. (Co-Chair), DeKay, N., Kurtz, A., Silverman, A. (2026). Well-Being Fatigue: When Employees Stop Believing the Company Cares [Invited Roundtable]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference. New Orleans, LA, United States. Employee burnout has long been a focus of organizational research (Maslach et al., 2001). Well-being programs have been proposed to combat burnout, but employees now report fatigue from both work and wellness (Hart et al., 2018; Beames et al., 2023). Indeed, Gallup shows engagement at a 10-year low despite interventions (Harter, 2025). This session explores well-being fatigue (i.e., the distrust in wellness efforts that overlook workload, equity, and leadership) and introduces terms like carewashing and quiet cracking. Attendees will learn strategies to restore trust and tackle root causes.

title: Say NO to Overwork and YES to I-O Career Sustainability! An Overdue Discussion (Session ID 979)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychologists improve the work life of countless others, but how are they doing in their own lives? Most have experienced pressures to work excessively or impression-manage how busy and stressed they are to keep up with the crowd. This session is about self-reflection and personal resistance to norms of overwork and stress as a badge of honor. Four experts with a passion for confronting overwork culture in their research and practice will facilitate small group discussion about how to say "no" to overwork and "yes" to sustainable careers for themselves and the workers they care about.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Skills-based approaches to talent management have taken hold in the past several years, with companies reorienting how they hire, develop, and place key talent. However, for decades, I-Os have viewed skills as one part of a complement of capabilities required for successful performance alongside requisite knowledge, abilities, and behaviors. I-O expert practitioners will discuss whether skills are sufficient for driving high impact talent management in organizations or if traditional KSAO view needs to be reasserted in organizations to maintain core talent practice integrity.

Participants analyze a client problem, develop evidence-based solutions, and deliver an executive presentation. In this session, participants will share lessons learned, tips for future participants, and reflections on the process.

Panel Discussion. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. GenAI conversations have predominantly centered around usage, adoption, and its inevitability in work and personal lives. Substantially less interest is seen in shortcomings and harms. I-O psychologists need to be aware of the negative impacts on workers and societies, and to actively seek to mitigate harm. This interactive session will engage the audience in discussions of real-world consequences of tools such as GenAI, and the role I-O psychologists should play when encountering these tools in the workplace and personally choosing to use them.

This interactive roundtable explores how to translate research into business-ready guidance without losing integrity. Participants will share strategies for building trust, simplifying without oversimplifying, and using storytelling to make science resonate in complex, real-world contexts.

By combining fresh models with applied examples, the session offers attendees tangible strategies for structuring evaluation efforts in today’s learning environments.

Contemporary popular TV series can serve as powerful case studies for leadership development. This session will analyze several popular series including Succession, Ted Lasso, and The Crown, and show how, by reducing cognitive load and creating an engaging narrative context, learning is enhanced. Excerpts from each series will help participants experience how popular media entertainment can increase the accessibility and integration of complex leadership lessons for leaders who are too often time-pressed and information-saturated.

As organizations expand their use of digital information, from cybervetting job candidates’ social media to monitoring employees through people analytics, questions of fairness, ethics, and equity are increasingly urgent. This Community of Interest will explore the boundaries of digital data use, focusing on individual rights, organizational responsibilities, and best practices. Participants will discuss where transparency ends and intrusion begins, how to minimize bias and misuse, and what policies and practices can sustain organizational effectiveness and trust.

Companies invest in people analytics, yet insights often fail to drive action. By combining technical skills, business acumen, storytelling and executive communication, internal consultants, often named People Analytics Business Partners (PABPs), bridge the gap between technical teams, HRBPs, and leaders to drive real impact. Agentic AI moves beyond single LLMs to goal-directed systems that plan, use tools, and act with bounded autonomy. Despite their popularity, guidance on key design and implementation decisions is scarce. This master tutorial presents a cross-industry framework uniting AI, software development, and I-O best practices for high-stakes workflows. We’ll demo an Agentic AI tool built with this framework and help attendees leave with a use case for their own work, tying design choices to psychometric evidence and implementation guardrails.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The purpose of this introductory tutorial is to highlight the important role that replication studies play in advancing I-O psychology research and offer advice on the best practices for choosing studies to replicate, designing replication studies, and publishing them. Replication studies are those that test the same hypotheses in a previously published article largely following the same methods, but with new data and may include a few constructive modifications. The tutorial will provide participants with a “checklist” on best practices for designing and publishing replication studies.

Across 5 papers, contributors examine mechanisms that shape allyship, leadership, and inclusion—from reframing meritocracy to foster DEI support, to understanding how leaders and followers cocreate allyship, to identifying recognition gaps in microaggressions and inclusion for neurodiverse employees. Together, these studies offer a roadmap for building inclusive, equitable workplaces.

Legitimacy, backlash, and the instability of corporate DEI Despite evidence supporting the significance of DEI, recent antidiversity rhetoric from national leaders calls into question the future of research and practice in this area. This symposium presents research at the forefront of this issue, highlighting the extent of costs associated with resistance to DEI and backlash from reactive organizational policies. Actionable guidance for practice toward improving intervention effectiveness are provided.

Across industries, organizations are beginning to advocate for integrating AI as not just tools but active teammates. This symposium extends core team science concepts to advance understanding of these novel collaborations and how they differ from traditional human teams. Papers examine concepts across team inputs, processes, and outputs: human and AI characteristics, trust, coordination, and performance. Collectively, papers highlight both the promise and limits of applying traditional team models to human–agent teams.

This panel of global assessment leaders from Talogy, HireVue, Saville, Aon, Hogan, and TTS explores generative AI in assessment: preventing misuse, augmenting current practices, and embracing AI as a criterion-relevant skill. Attendees will gain evidence-based strategies, practical innovations, and a forward-looking vision for responsible, inclusive, and impactful talent evaluation and assessment.

title: GenAI and the Death (Or Rebirth) of Management (Session ID 339)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA., United States. Authors prognosticate how the impetus of generative AI will first threaten, then dismantle, and ultimately remake the manager’s role. They discuss implications of forces such as multimodal AI, agents, and context engineering on human-AI teaming, performance evaluation, and a new form of systems thinking overseen by a reconstituted manager profile. They present previously unshared (even to each other!) predictions for GenAI's impact on management over 3, 5, 10, and 25-year time horizons and debate wildly contrasting views stated with unflinching, unwarranted confidence.

title: Good Enough to Be Good: Talent Decisions Under Speed and Scrutiny (Session ID 1137)

Good Enough to Be Good: Talent Decisions Under Speed and Scrutiny [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Work is changing faster than most decision cycles, forcing leaders to act quickly while maintaining fairness and ROI. This panel examines how people leaders are rethinking data use, skill priorities, and motivation while adapting hiring, mobility, and development amid AI, evolving skill requirements, and economic volatility. By sharing candid lessons and linking them to concepts like unlearning and bounded rationality, panelists will show how to question outdated tools, avoid missteps, and make evidence-based, defensible talent decisions under real-world constraints.

title: I-O Psychology in Military Contexts: Reconnecting Fields to Tackle Emerging Challenges (Session ID 1337)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session explores how reconnecting I-O psychology with military and mission-critical contexts can advance theory and practice. Panelists—scholars and practitioners from defense, NATO, and public safety—will examine leadership, team dynamics, inclusion in hierarchies, and the psychological impact of technology. The session aims to foster cross-sector collaboration, spark new research, and build an international community focused on applying psychological science in high-stakes environments.

title: Is ROI Dead? Debating the Value of Its Impact in Outcome Measurement (Session ID 1165)

(Non-Speaking Contributor). (2026, April). Is ROI Dead? Debating the Value of Economic Impact in Outcome Measurement [Debate]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Return on investment (ROI) has long been the business standard for judging the impact of leadership and talent initiatives. Critics argue ROI oversimplifies complex outcomes—such as trust, equity, and team effectiveness—that organizations increasingly prioritize. Supporters contend ROI remains the only universal denominator, because every outcome ultimately shapes costs and benefits. This debate will stage both perspectives, invite audience challenges, and explore whether the future of measurement lies in refining ROI or moving beyond it.

title: Navigating Headwinds to AI in Assessment (Session ID 328)
speakers: John Weiner | Charles Handler | Eric Dunleavy | Marc Weinstein

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools are quickly expanding in workplace and educational settings, including their application in assessment. Although AI can enable efficiencies, scalability, and innovative approaches, users encounter challenges due to regulations and laws, the need for human oversight and governance, and complexity in evaluating the effectiveness and risks associated with AI tools. This session explores considerations for effectively navigating these challenges, drawing from legal precedent, guidelines, and examples in talent assessment and credentialing.

title: Partner Showcase: Valence - From Chatbot to Coach: Designing Systems That Actually Develop People
speakers: Elizabeth Ritterbush | Beth Shipman | Julia Walsh | Ellie Wildman | Rob Lewis
speaker_affiliations: The Home Depot | Corning Incorporated | General Mills | Valence | ADP
description: The rapid proliferation of large language models has created a misleading assumption: that any AI system capable of conversation is capable of coaching. But I-O psychologists know that effective coaching requires context on organizational culture, individual goals, team dynamics, performance frameworks, and continuity across interactions. This panel brings together I-O leaders from Fortune 500 companies who are actively deploying AI coaching in their organizations. Panelists will explore what distinguishes a purpose-built AI coaching system from general-purpose conversational AI and the organizational design implications of scalable coaching. Whether your organization is evaluating AI coaching vendors or building internal capabilities, this session offers a practitioner-grounded framework for asking the right questions.

Participants see what AI does well, where it fails, and where professional judgment remains essential. This expert demonstration is designed for AI-curious practitioners to understand how conversational prompting can accelerate psychometric workflows without sacrificing rigor or ethics.

In particular, the papers in the proposed symposium investigate gender differences in leveraging self-efficacy, the role of internships in shaping entrepreneurial identity, how entrepreneurs and employees reframe demanding work, the double-edged impact of family support, and the mixed effects of entrepreneurial hustle. Together, these studies inform scholars and practitioners on how psychological and contextual factors shape entrepreneurial experiences.

In recent years, various I-O psychologists have raised questions and concerns about the state of the field, with some arguing that I-Os are experiencing a collective identity crisis. In this 50-minute alternative session, 4 members of the I-O community invite attendees to engage in a highly participative conversation about the state of the field. Using a world café format, attendees will have the opportunity to explore a series of reflexive questions via collaborative, facilitated discussions with their peers.

title: Automated Anonymous: Education and Reskilling for I-Os in the Age of Automation (Session ID 964)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Automation of some of the tasks that are currently the bread and butter of I-O careers will necessitate changes both in how I-Os train the next generation and how they retrain themselves. This panel unites academic and applied perspectives to explore, first, what I-O activities are foreseen to be automated in the coming years and, second, how each of formal graduate programs and personal reskilling can equip I-Os to thrive alongside (or when supplanted by) intelligent systems, promoting our personal and professional flourishing in an increasingly automated world.

This introductory tutorial equips participants with practical tools to apply causal inference methods to everyday workplace challenges. Through accessible explanations and example scenarios, authors introduce 3 quasi-experimental analytical approaches not commonly taught in the field of I-O psychology: differences-in-differences, regression discontinuity design, and propensity score matching. Participants will learn when and how to apply each method, as well as the critical assumptions and potential pitfalls associated with each.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Understanding test administration realities helps assessment developers maximize the effectiveness of their tests. Panelists with experience developing and administering assessments will share strategies for maintaining objectivity, ensuring standardization, and responding resiliently to unexpected events while preserving test integrity—lessons applicable across all assessment contexts.

Authors feature a panel of I-O psychologists at various career stages, and panelists will share personal insights from both sides of the internship. Attendees will learn the long-term benefits of developing intern talent and how they can serve as a pipeline for future full-time employees. This session aims to inspire organizations to view internships as strategic tools for building stronger, more agile teams.

This Community of Interest will explore why valuing and recognizing these contributions matters for organizational effectiveness and culture. Discussions will focus on change fatigue, emotional labor and exhaustion, leadership’s role, equitable recognition, and fairness in distributing career implications.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference. It’s no secret change is difficult to implement, especially within large organizations. In fact, research studies have estimated that roughly 60%–70% of change initiatives fail. One of the keys to setting up an effective change management strategy is starting with employee listening. From there, the session will include case studies from 3 organizations where presenters will share their experiences and recommendations for the implementation of effective change. Attendees will gain concrete actionable ideas to drive meaningful change within their organizations.

This session brings together expert researchers in I-O psychology and sport management to discuss the opportunities, challenges, and future research integrating the 2 fields. The goal of this session is to connect those with common interest in sport while providing guidance and inspiration for future work that advances the field of I-O psychology through sport.

Discussion will highlight ways to build trust, ensure legal defensibility, and create consistent, ethical, and engaging experiences from candidate to employee.

Participants at all levels, from introductory to advanced, will benefit from the discussion, receive a capability-mapping tool, and gain insights to strengthen OD’s role as a strategic partner.

This presentation examines how personality relates to early leadership emergence among undergraduate teaching assistants (TAs) in a gateway leadership development course for undergraduate incoming students. Authors argue that personality assessments, when used formatively, can guide leadership development within academic settings. This introductory session is intended for educators and practitioners seeking to identify and support emerging leaders in higher education.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores the current evidence-based practices for reducing the high failure rate of leaders within their first 18 months. Because leader failure follows predictable patterns, panelists will use a chronological framework to examine interventions to transform potential adversarial relationships to productive partnerships. Drawing from industrial-organizational psychology research and real-world implementation experiences, the discussion addresses critical factors that predict new leader success with particular emphasis on change agent leaders who face heightened scrutiny and resistance.

Fireside chat presented at the annual conference of the Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology, New Orleans, LA. This fireside chat brings together Ochsner Health CEO Pete November and Chief People and Culture Officer Tracey Schiro for an intimate conversation about the partnership dynamics that drive organizational performance. The discussion moves beyond typical panel presentations to explore how these leaders actually work together daily. Through candid dialogue and real stories from Louisiana's largest health system, they examine partnership models, trust-building in the C-suite, data-driven decision making, and more, offering practical insights grounded in both research and lived experience.

title: The Dark Side of Meaningful Work: Passion, Burnout, and Organizational Responsibility (Session ID 1300)
speakers: Malissa Clark | Javon Davis | Shelby Davis

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA., United States. Although meaningful work is often positioned as the facilitator of employee well-being and engagement, emerging research highlights troubling consequences: overwork, burnout, and tolerance of poor working conditions. This session challenges previous meaningful work literature by engaging the audience in a discussion on the potential consequences of meaningful work perceptions. Through an Open Fishbowl structure, attendees will connect real-world examples with research on meanings work, callings, and occupational health.

title: Ways to Address Flawed Assumptions in Testing (Session ID 452)
speakers: Dr. Joel P. Wiesen
speaker_affiliations: Applied Personnel Research
description: Wiesen, J. P. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Ways to Address Flawed Assumptions in Testing [Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Testing practices often rely on unnoticed flawed assumptions or misconceptions. For example, high interrater reliability in BARS grading may be artifactual and not support validity, and setting passing points based on overall test performance assumes that strengths compensate for weaknesses and may result in hiring incompetent applicants. Possible remedies for 20 such flaws will be discussed from the viewpoint of a practitioner.

Attendees will learn and practice evidence-based micro-interventions, ranging from designing purposeful meetings to engaging in appreciative inquiry and navigating difficult conversations. Participants will leave with wise techniques they can immediately integrate in teams and organizational development work.

Panelists will share applied research, organizational practices, and emerging approaches, to highlight strategies for balancing innovation, scientific rigor, and candidate experience in a rapidly evolving landscape.

Worker well-being is a global challenge with urgent implications for organizations and employees alike. This dynamic panel brings together leading applied and academic experts to spotlight international perspectives, share cutting-edge insights, and engage the audience in co-creating practical solutions. Adding unique depth, a representative from SOHP’s pre-conference forum will deliver fresh takeaways, ensuring a lively, future-focused discussion that bridges research, practice, and action in today’s rapidly changing world of work.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Coachability—the willingness to accept feedback, reflect, and adapt—is a key predictor of leadership success, yet it is inconsistently measured across assessment methods. This session brings together researchers and practitioners to explore how coachability appears in simulations, psychometrics, and interviews, and what signals reveal a leader’s learning capacity. Panelists will share evidence and practical strategies to assess and develop coachability as a foundation for leadership adaptability and growth.

This symposium highlights research exploring the complex role of identity in authenticity, including race/ethnicity, gender, and sexual orientation.

To address this gap, authors believe open communication from both scientists and practitioners is needed to find a balance between the rigidity of science and the flexibility required by organizations. This alternative session will have speakers address this gap through their experiences with balancing practicality and rigor in training design.

title: Beyond High Touch: AI in Executive Development (Session ID 302)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming learning and development at every level of organizations—personalizing learning, scaling leadership programs, and enhancing coaching. Yet, paradoxically, executive development remains largely untouched, still centered on high-cost, high-touch human interventions. This panel explores whether “high touch” must always mean “human touch,” and how AI can democratize, scale, and reimagine executive learning. Panelists will discuss key use cases and debate the opportunities and risks of applying AI at the top of organizations.

Participants will rotate among expert-facilitated tables to co-create strategies, culminating in a post-conference best-practices toolkit that advances inclusive, ethical, and impactful research.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Much of the conversation around AI in organizations emphasizes its promise and potential, but real-world implementation often reveals serious pitfalls. From hallucinations and bias blind spots to privacy concerns and overreliance on tools, failures can undermine trust, fairness, and outcomes. This Community of Interest session invites participants to candidly share “AI gone wrong” experiences and surface lessons learned. Through discussion, participants will gain insights into recurring challenges and sharpen their awareness of how to navigate AI’s risks responsibly.

title: Demystifying Inclusion: Everyday Inclusion for the Masses (Session ID 1343)

Demystifying inclusion: Clearing the fog around inclusive actions & behaviors [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Inclusion is widely valued but often misunderstood. Many employees want to contribute to inclusive workplaces yet lack clear, practical guidance. This panel, Demystifying Inclusion, brings together practitioners, scholars, and consultants to clarify what inclusion truly means, strip away its politicized framing, and highlight actionable behaviors that foster respect, fairness, and belonging. Through research and real-world examples, panelists will illuminate simple, scalable paths to inclusion.

title: Detecting and Dealing With GenAI-Facilitated Cheating (Session ID 137)

[Contributor] (2026). Detecting and dealing with GenAI-facilitated cheating [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. With the rapid advances in Generative AI (GenAI) capabilities over the last few years, job applicants are using widely available tools like ChatGPT to write resumes and cover letters, and to prepare for interviews. Some also attempt to outsource the assessment completion process to GenAI. This session brings together global perspectives on detecting the use of GenAI to cheat on assessments, as well as dealing with the consequences. Panelists from several multinational organizations will engage in a lively discussion on how to detect and cope with GenAI-facilitated cheating.

title: Does Work Feel Different? A New Psychological Contract for an Evolving World (Session ID 823)

A New Psychological Contract for an Evolving World [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In an era of generative AI and global disruption, the psychological contract between employers and employees is being rewritten. This panel explores how organizations can evolve from static models to dynamic systems that emphasize employability, adaptability, and trust. Panelists will share research and practice insights on sustaining fairness, meaning, and human value amid technological transformation and shifting workforce expectations.

Society of Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Though highly skilled, I-O psychologists often struggle to influence decision-makers. This session teaches Monroe's Motivated Sequence (MMS), a framework used since the 1930s to craft persuasive messages. After last year’s successful session (350+ RSVPs and 100 attendees) this year’s will focus where participants were most engaged: panelist stories and live pitch critiquing. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Many I-O psychologists have jobs that require communicating complex information to diverse audiences including executives, clients, and stakeholders, but formal training in technical writing may not be part of their academic preparation. This master tutorial provides principles and practical procedures for creating effective technical reports that differ from academic writing. Participants will learn to identify target audiences, apply appropriate communication strategies, use formatting techniques, and leverage modern tools including AI to improve writing quality and efficiency.

Together they offer multilevel insights into how people, relationships, and culture create safer organizations.

This interactive panel, featuring experts from Apple, the FBI, The Second City, and other highly regarded organizations, explores how humor strengthens inclusive, engaged, and innovative work cultures. Authors examine how tenets of humor—such as active listening, building on others’ ideas, and embracing uncertainty—enhance resilience, collaboration, creativity and psychological safety in diverse teams. Panelists highlight studies linking humor with employee engagement and belonging among both neurodivergent and neurotypical employees. Attendees gain evidence-based insights and practical approaches.

speakers: Zihao Jia | Tariq Shaban | Mina Son | Virginia Cheng | Xavier Heron

description: Jia, Z. (Co-Chair), Lee, P. (Co-Chair) & Shaban, T. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Novel applications of LLM in measurement [Symposium]. LLM-based Scoring in Text-based Fit Assessments. Large language models are transforming psychological measurements. This symposium features 4 studies exploring LLMs as collaborators in key measurement tasks. The first evaluates LLMs for social desirability ratings in forced-choice item design. The second compares LLM- and human-based content validation across more than 100 organizational scales, offering interpretive benchmarks. The third introduces AutoScale, a fully automated LLM pipeline for scale development with strong psychometric properties. The 4th applies LLMs to score person–organization fit from open-ended responses.

title: SIOP Special Session: Is This In-Role or Extra-Role? Exploring the Gray Areas of Work Through Film (Session ID 1431)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychology distinguishes between “in-role” and “extra-role” behaviors, yet the line between them is often unclear. This interactive session uses film clips from The Devil Wears Prada, Horrible Bosses, and The Intern to explore that ambiguity. Participants will vote on whether actions are in-role or extra-role as scholars discuss how context, expectations, and measurement shape perceptions of what it truly means to go “above and beyond” at work.

title: Smarter Listening, Better Outcomes: Unlocking EX With AI (Session ID 395)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping employee listening by making surveys smarter, feedback more conversational, insights faster, and action more sustainable. From survey design and data collection to analysis and action planning, AI offers new tools to capture richer feedback and drive meaningful change. When paired with behavioral science best practices ensuring rigor, transparency, and human judgment, AI can amplify employee voice, close the gap between insight and action, and transform the employee experience.

title: Still Here! Navigating Remoteness During Graduate School (Session ID 477)

Navigating remoteness during graduate school [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Graduate students often experience various forms of remoteness during their training (e.g., distance from advisor, lab, or program). Yet, there is limited guidance on how students can effectively navigate remoteness. In this session, panelists will discuss their personal experiences with remoteness during graduate training, and offer actionable strategies for how to cope with this common but overlooked element of the student experience. Small group discussions with the audience will follow, where participants will collectively develop a comprehensive list of strategies.

title: Trust But Verify: Assessment Integrity in the Digital Age (Session ID 914)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As remote and virtual assessments become increasingly prevalent, organizations face mounting challenges in maintaining test integrity and deterring candidate misbehavior. This panel brings together assessment owners who have implemented security solutions across different organizational contexts and industries. The discussion will explore how different approaches impact candidate behavior, assessment validity, and the hiring experience. Panelists will share real-world cases of successful implementation strategies, pitfalls to avoid, and emerging trends in assessment security.

title: Wait What!? Disparate Impact Theory and the Current Legal and Regulatory Landscape (Session ID 1278)
speakers: Charles Mcghee | Patrick McNiel | David Morris
speaker_affiliations: Affirmity | Workforce Compliance & Diversity | Affirmity
description: McGhee, C. R., McNiel, P. D., Morris, M. D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Wait What!? - Disparate Impact Theory and the Current Legal and Regulatory Landscape [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Disparate impact theory has been embedded in the legal and regulatory landscape for over 50 years. This legal doctrine has been upheld by court rulings at all levels and has broadened in application over the years. However, disparate impact theory as a guiding civil rights principal is being threatened by recent Supreme Court rulings, executive orders, employer confusion about “illegal DEI,” and other forms of pressure. Panelists will present the history of this theory, recent changes in the legal and regulatory landscape, effects on employers, and outcomes that are evolving as a result.

description: Paustian-Underdahl, S. C. (Chair), Banks, G., Cullen-Lester, K., Frear, K., Loignon, A., & Louie, R. (Panelists). (2026, April). Bridging the divide: How leadership science can better inform leadership development [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Leadership development (LD) is a multi-billion-dollar industry, yet programs often fail to deliver sustained impact. Leadership science has identified the traits and behaviors linked to effectiveness, but these insights are often lost before or during translation into practice. At the same time, research frequently overlooks the realities leaders face. This panel examines the systemic science–practice gap in leadership: why evidence is underutilized, where research lacks relevance, and how collaboration can bridge the divide between scholarship and LD practice.

This event offers insights into making job changes, exploring new directions, and navigating career transitions. Small group discussions will be facilitated by practitioners with diverse career experiences. Learn more about I-O career paths you may be interested in transitioning to or discover a path you didn’t know existed. Designed for I‑O professionals with 2+ years of experience seeking effective strategies for building a thriving I‑O career.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States This panel spotlights chronic illness as a widespread yet overlooked workplace challenge, reshaping how organizations think about inclusion, well-being, and performance. It brings together experts bridging research and practice to explore how chronic physical, mental, and neurodevelopmental conditions affect work experiences, culture, and outcomes. Through a solution-focused dialogue, panelists will share evidence-based strategies to create supportive policies, inclusive work design, and drive organizational change that allows chronically ill employees to thrive at work.

Internships have long been central to graduate education. Yet as people science and analytics roles rapidly emerge within organizations, there is limited guidance on how to structure new internships. This panel bridges the academic–practitioner gap by bringing together key stakeholders from organizations and I-O programs to discuss meaningful internship programs. Authors provide a practical overview of how to advocate for internships, design them to be beneficial for all, and extend their benefits beyond a project. Attendees will leave with guidance from designing to evaluating internship programs.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Balancing work and personal life is becoming increasingly challenging in today’s rapidly evolving work environment. As employees seek fulfillment beyond their jobs, hobbies play an important role in promoting well-being, creativity, and connection. This session explores the intersection of hobbies and work by categorizing various types of hobbies, assessing their relevance in the modern workplace, and discussing the benefits and potential drawbacks of these hobbies for both individuals and organizations.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizations are beginning to recognize the strengths neurodivergent employees bring, yet most efforts stop at providing accommodations. The next challenge is talent development: creating pathways for growth, advancement, and long-term career success. This panel will explore how I-O psychology can shape strategies that move beyond access to focus on cultivating skills, preparing leaders, and fostering career progression for neurodivergent professionals. Attendees will gain practical insights on how organizations can better develop and retain this critical talent.

This panel brings together I-O psychologists who have successfully implemented GenAI solutions at Amazon, Takeda, and Humancore to share how they crossed "the GenAI Divide." Through discussion of research and piloting phases, panelists will provide practical strategies for identifying success metrics, managing organizational friction, and building learning-capable systems. Attendees will learn to avoid pitfalls and implement GenAI solutions that deliver value in areas like hiring, performance management, and employee development.

This panel explores how professionals can ethically and effectively use platforms like LinkedIn and TikTok to expand impact, build community, and legitimize I-O psychology. Attendees will gain strategies, resources, and a clear action plan to elevate their voice and the discipline online.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The rapid deployment of AI, robotics, and algorithmic systems has led to technology-led workforce reductions, reshaping not only headcount, but also how individuals view their futures, how teams function under pressure, and how global narratives on technology evolve. This symposium unites individual, team, and global lenses to explore workers’ experiences of job insecurity and technological change, highlighting evidence-based, theory-informed pathways for responsible deployment, targeted reskilling, and healthier, more ethical organizations in the age of automation.

Participants will then have the opportunity to share their insights with the overall audience.

title: Synthetic Personality: Conceptual and Research Encounters (Session ID 543)
speakers: Georgi Yankov | Anoop Javalagi | Bradley Pitcher | Pengda Wang
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessments | HumRRO | APTMetrics | Rice University
description: Yankov, G. P. (Chair) (2026). Synthetic personality: Conceptual and research encounters [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This symposium investigates whether large language models can emulate human-like personalities consistent with the 5 factor model. It showcases 4 studies examining the stability of LLM-based decision policies, the effects of personality manipulation in workplace negotiations, the validity of LLM-generated personality ratings compared to self-reports, and the fidelity of simulated profiles such as those of U.S. presidents. Findings suggest LLMs can exhibit stable, theoretically coherent, and psychometrically valid “synthetic personalities.”

title: Tails From the Workplace: Human–Animal Interactions in the World of Work (Session ID 620)
speakers: Halle Gold | Ian Hughes | Ivan Hernandez | Daniel Svyantek
speaker_affiliations: Texas A&M University | Texas A&M University - Psychological and Brain Sciences | Virginia Tech
description: Hughes, I. M. (Co-Chair) & Gold, H. (Co-Chair). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Tails from the workplace: Human-animal interactions in the world of work [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Donnelly, L., Svyantek, D., Wadsworth, D., & Waggoner, P. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Paws and partnerships: Characteristics and benefits of canine handlers in safety services. Gold, H. & Hughes, I. M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). How and why quality time with one’s dog at home spills over into the workplace. Hernandez, I. & Busick, C. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The diametric opposition between supporting slaughterhouses and promoting the aims of OHP. Junça-Silva, A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). When inclusion is low: Pet-friendly practices as catalysts for meaningful work and engagement. This intermediate-level symposium explores how human–animal interactions (HAIs) shape employee well-being, motivation, and meaning at work. Across diverse contexts—from pet ownership and canine-assisted teams to animal-inclusive policies and slaughterhouse labor—the 4 papers illustrate both the enriching and distressing aspects of HAIs in work life. Together, they highlight the expanding relevance of animals in organizational science and occupational health psychology.

title: The Creative Catalyst: Personal Experiences That Drive Innovation at Work (Session ID 1263)
speakers: Lily Blocker, PhD | Alexis d'Amato | Joshua Fairchild
speaker_affiliations: Stony Brook University | University of Nebraska, Omaha | Creighton University
description: Blocker, L. (Chair) (2026). The Creative Catalyst: Experiences that Drive Innovation at Work [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Fairchild, J., Hessman, K., Brainard, J, & Deluca, K. (2026). Investigating the Interaction of Expertise and Idea Originality on New Product Evaluation. D’Amato, A., Witkowski, C., Ligon, G., & Hunter, S. T. (2026). Perspective-Taking and Creative Climates in the Intelligence Communities: Insights from Two Case Studies Blocker, L., Salter, N. P., Polavarapu, A., & Forman, J. (2026). Diversity and Disruption: LGBTQ Leaders’ Potential for Creativity. In today's evolving business landscape, innovation is indispensable for maintaining a competitive edge. This symposium presents 3 studies that describe how experiences influence our potential to innovate. These experiences range from evaluating creative ideas based on domain expertise, utilizing perspective taking of adversaries, and the unique insight that stems from being a minority member. Further, strategies and insights that can empower organizations to embrace innovation and thrive in today's competitive landscape will be discussed.

title: The Expanding Horizon: NonHR Data for Proactive People Analytics (Session ID 212)
speakers: Jeremy Sapia | Craig Reddock | Rory O’Gallagher | Sidney Thomas
speaker_affiliations: HP | Amazon | Ikona Analytics | Allstate
description: Sapia, J. (Chair), Thomas, S., O’Gallagher, R., Reddock, C., & Blazek, E. S. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The Expanding Horizon: Non-HR Data for Proactive People Analytics [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel discussion explores the opportunities and challenges of leveraging non-HR data, defined as data existing outside traditional HR information systems, to enhance organizational effectiveness and business outcomes. Four practitioners will present diverse use cases demonstrating how non-HR data sources can help transform people analytics from a reactive service to a proactive strategic function. The presenters will address critical questions about data regulation, ethics, privacy, and the methodological challenges of connecting data sources housed by different business units.

title: The Impact of AI on I-O and Assessment: Futureproofing Our Skills and Identity (Session ID 127)
speakers: Kimberly Adams | Alexander Alonso | Beth Bynum | Liberty Munson | Tara Myers
speaker_affiliations: Human Resources Research Organization (HumRRO) | Society for Human Resource Management (SHRM) | HumRRO | Microsoft | American Nurses Credentialing Center
description: Munson, L. J., Alonso, A., Myers, T. L., Bynum, B. H., & Adams, K. A., (April 30 – May 2). The Impact of AI on I-O Psychology: Futureproofing Our Skills and Identity [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI is transforming assessment and redefining the roles of I-O psychologists. In this session, a panel of assessment experts will explore how AI is shifting professional identity, creating new career paths and opportunities (with the help of new skills), and raising challenges for I-O psychology as AI is used to perform the tasks of those new to the field (deskilling). Panelists will share real-world examples, ethical dilemmas, and actionable strategies to help attendees adapt and thrive as AI reshapes what I-Os do, how they do it, and the skills needed to be successful in our roles.

title: Udderly Controversial: Debating Engagement’s Sacred Cows (Session Id 1099)
speakers: Aaron Kraus | Brandon Jordan | Boris Genzer | Isabelle (Izzy) Ponce-Pore | Taylor Scotese | Rebecca Clancy
speaker_affiliations: Vertex Pharmaceuticals | ForPsyte Talent Assessment | Marriott | Marriott International | WTW | Lodestone People Consulting
description: Genzer, B. (Moderator), Jordan, B. (Panelist), Scotese, T. (Panelist), Kraus, A. (Panelist), Ponce-Pore, I. (Panelist), Clancy, R. (Panelist) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Udderly Controversial: Debating Engagement’s Sacred Cows [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Despite decades of effort, organizational engagement remains stubborn. Are we doing it wrong? This rapid-fire debate challenges sacred assumptions on listening strategy, design, and measurement. Experts will spar over engineering engagement, the utility of continuous listening, and whether AI or benchmarks add noise or clarity. The goal: provide research-anchored takeaways for programs that fit reality.

title: Who Rules? Global Leader Insights From Personality, 360, and Talent Assessment (Session ID 1423)
speakers: Krista Pederson | Kate Bravery | Nicole Dickie | Shovna M | Violeta Lučić | Alise Dabdoub
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessments | Mercer, A Marsh Business | ThreeFish Consulting India | Assessment Systems International
description: Pederson, K.M. (Chair). (2026, April 30 - May 6). Who rules? Global leader insights from personality, 360-feedback, and talent management [Symposium]. Society for Industrial Organizational Psychology, New Orleans, LA, United States. Dickie, N.M., Dabdoub, A., Pederson, K.M. (2026, April 30 – May 6). Global emergent leader study: Distinguishing characteristics of leader personality by country. Bravery, K., and Bailey R. (2026, April 30 – May 6). Readying leaders for success in an interconnected and fragmented world. Matic, S., Tracy, M., Edema-Sillo, E., and Lucic, V. M. (2026, April 30 – May 6). Emergent versus effective leadership in Eastern Europe. Mukhopadhyay, S., Dutt, B., Dabdoub, A., Cruickshank, L., (2026, April 30- May 6). What does 360 feedback data say about Indian leaders? Leadership is critical to the success of organizations. Organizations must understand the nuances of leadership in different countries and contexts to successfully support cross-border business, leader placement and development. This symposium presents 4 papers using various assessment data, including personality, 360s, and talent survey to examine leadership across and within different countries and contexts. Authors contribute to the cross-cultural leadership literature by offering data-based insights on global leadership.

title: AI Goes to College: Practical Tools for Navigating AI in the Classroom (Session ID 887)
speakers: Annie Kato | Cristina Rubino | Loren Naidoo | Satoris Howes | Michael Ekema
speaker_affiliations: Seattle Pacific University | California State University, Northridge | Oregon State University | CSU-Long Beach
description: Kato, A. E. (Chair & Panelist), Ekema, M. L. (Panelist), Howes, S. S. (Panelist), Naidoo, L. J. (Panelist), & Rubino, C. (Panelist). (2026, April 30 – May 2). AI goes to college: Practical tools for navigating AI in the classroom [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. How can faculty harness the power of AI in the classroom while addressing its challenges? This interactive session moves beyond debate to share practical, tested strategies for navigating AI in the classroom. Presenters will showcase diverse approaches, from using AI to co-design active learning and support grading to fostering critical AI literacy and upholding academic integrity. Roundtable discussions will engage participants in sharing experiences and generating actionable insights for effective pedagogy in the age of AI.

speakers: Richard Guzzo | Marcus Dickson | Michael Ford | Paul Bliese

(2026, Apr 30-May 2). Advancing academic–organizational collaboration: Internal research and data sharing. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Dickson, M. Discussant and facilitator. Organizations create and curate large quantities of research-friendly data. For more than a decade the use of this data has been predicted to revolutionize IO science. Is the revolution here? This symposium reports evidence on the impact of such data on IO science, examines 2 recent success stories in acquiring and using organizational data for published research, and closely examines the nature of academic-organization collaboration for data sharing and scientific impact. The symposium combines research findings with experience-based insights.

title: Are You Smarter Than an MBA? An AI-Powered Upskill on Business and Financial Acumen (Session ID1091)
speakers: Aqsa Ali | Kareem Panton
speaker_affiliations: BTS | American Express
description: Ali, A. A. (Co-Chair), Panton, K. (Co-Chair), Aruffo, A. (Co-Chair), (2026, April 30 – May 2). Are You Smarter than an MBA? An AI-Powered Upskill on Business & Financial Acumen [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This interactive session will upskill I-O psychologists on key tenets of business and financial acumen through AI-powered teaching. It will engage attendees in application-style learning on core concepts they can use to be more effective in their work. Following each round, concept applications will be debriefed. Attendees will leave with a deeper understanding of how to operate more effectively with clients and navigate the interests of organizational leaders. The session is intended for all audiences regardless of existing business and financial knowledge.

Zhou, L., Zhang, F., Parker, S., & Wang, M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Age differences in team leadership: A process model. Theory building in organizational psychology and behavior has advanced greatly over the last several decades but still needs to better uncover the processes driving phenomena over time. We argue for the ability to triangulate process knowledge through the combined use of qualitative and quantitative process data, along with computational process theories. Each of these approaches provides unique but meaningful insights into the nature of organizational processes to build better theories and accumulate knowledge. Authors demonstrate this tripartite approach across 5 studies of teamwork processes.

title: From Service to Science: Veterans Navigating the I-O Psychology Pipeline (Session ID 855)
speakers: Abigail Burt | Esther Rodgers | Russell Thomas | Marla J Albertie PhD
speaker_affiliations: OpTech | St. Mary’s University | George Mason University | CTU
description: Burt, A. N. (Co-Chair), Rodgers, E. (Co-Chair), Albertie, M. J., Pearson, A. D., Estrada, A. X., Thomas, R., & Ramsey, R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). From Service to Science: Veterans Navigating the I-O Psychology Pipeline [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel will explore how military-affiliated students and professionals navigate the I-O psychology pipeline, from military service through graduate education to practice. Drawing on personal experiences and applied perspectives, panelists will discuss challenges such as stigma, cultural gaps, and underemployment, as well as the unique strengths and skills veterans bring to the field. This session will offer strategies for educators and organizations to better support this population and strengthen graduate programs and workplace outcomes.

title: Innovation in Skills Assessment Development: Open Space to Build Scalable Strategies (Session ID 832)
speakers: Lauren Park | Marian Pitel-Wali | Ruth Imose
speaker_affiliations: SAP | Workera | Johnson & Johnson
description: Imose, R. (Co-Chair), Lindgren, C. (Co-Chair), McDaniel, M., Park, L., Pitel-Wali, M. (2026). Innovation in Skills Assessment: An Open Space to Brainstorm Scalable Strategies [Alternative Session]. Society of Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This facilitator-led open space session aims to foster small-group discussions focused on developing effective skill assessment strategies and exploring how AI can support scalable assessment methods. Participants will rotate through 4 focused discussion tables, each led by a facilitator, in 15-minute rounds. Facilitators will synthesize insights, capture key takeaways, and at the session’s end, share recommendations across topics. The goal is to generate collaborative, actionable best practices to inform future efforts in measuring skills at scale.

title: Innovations in Public Safety Assessments: Best Practices and Lessons Learned (Session ID 654)
speakers: Julia Haas | Menucha Sperlin | Brandon Flores-Hutton | David Finch | Daniel King | Kristen Hargrave | Mark Frame | Todd Baker
speaker_affiliations: Carl Vinson Institute of Government, University of Georgia | Port Authority of New York New Jersey | IOS, Inc. | Talent Strategies Consulting | Arlington County Government | Middle Tennessee State University | HumRRO
description: Haas, J. B., Sperlin, M., Baker, T. A., Finch, D. M., Flores-Hutton, B., Frame, M., Hargrave, K., & King, D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Innovations in Public Safety Assessments: Best Practices and Lessons Learned [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Public safety agencies are under growing pressure to modernize how they select and promote personnel. This panel of experts will examine how technology, AI, and innovative assessment methods are transforming this traditionally change-resistant field. Panelists will share lessons learned and success stories from agencies that have evolved their practices, offering attendees with practical strategies to improve candidate experience, strengthen stakeholder engagement, and build more effective, future-ready public safety organizations.

title: Learning From Labor: (Re)Connecting Work Psychology With Unions (Session ID 764)
speakers: George Alliger | Leslie Hammer | Jason Phillips | Keaton Fletcher | Lisa Kath | Lauren Kuykendall | Michael Zickar | Peter McEachern | Ryszard Koziel
speaker_affiliations: Consulting Work Psychologist | Oregon Health & Science University | Baruch College & CUNY Graduate Center | Colorado State University | San Diego State University | George Mason University | Bowling Green State University | Saint Louis University School of Medicine
description: McEachern, P. J. (Co-chair), Koziel, R. J. (Co-chair), Alliger, G. M. (Co-chair), Broderick, G., Fletcher, K., Hammer, L., Harrison, R., Kath, L., Kuykendall, L. E., London, M., Phillips, J., Reindel, R., Zickar, M. J., & Catlin, M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). (Re)Connecting occupational health psychology with labor unions [Alternative session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. It is a little-known fact that the field of occupational health psychology was developed in part by I-O psychologists who studied and worked with labor unions. Although most of those connections have faded, interest in their reestablishment is increasing. This session will facilitate that process through roundtable discussions between session attendees, union leaders, and occupational health and safety researcher-activists. They will confront disconnects between I-O/OHP and organized labor, learn about what communities have to offer each other, and lay the groundwork for future collaboration.

title: Nudging Leaders Forward: Interventions to Enhance Organizational Decision-Making (Session ID 868)
speakers: Nandini Mehta | Alyssa Ortega | Amanda Julian | Bradley Brummel | Kaytlynn Griswold | Melissa Regester
speaker_affiliations: University of Houston | Vantage Leadership Consulting | Monark | PepsiCo
description: Mehta, N. M. (Co-Chair), Ortega, A. M. (Co-Chair), Brummel, B. J., Griswold, K. R., Julian, A., Lacerenza, C. N., & Regester, M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Nudging leaders forward: Interventions to enhance organizational decision-making [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The purpose of this panel discussion is to explore the utility of nudge interventions for enhancing leader development, drawing insights across practitioners and academics. Topics addressed include nudge theory and core components of nudging, current organizational applications, proposed applications toward leaders, and potential considerations and challenges in their future adoption. The overarching goal is to spark scientific inquiry into how nudges can be leveraged to enhance organizational decision-making among leaders.

title: Partner Showcase: Aon Talent Solutions - Work Rewired: AI's Impact on the Workforce, Talent Assessment, and the Candidate Experience
speakers: Ernie Paskey | Melissa Champine | Lauren Lilly | Maged Natanios | Scott Reithel
speaker_affiliations: Aon | Aon Talent Assessments | Marriott International
description: The showcase examines how Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping work—and how organizations can respond with intention rather than reaction. The session introduces an AI sensitivity–driven approach to understanding how tasks, skills, and roles are evolving, and how those insights enable job reimagination, targeted skill updates, and more informed workforce decisions. Building on this foundation, presenters explore how AI is transforming talent assessment and the candidate’s experience, shifting from traditional methods to more scalable, immersive, and engaging solutions. The session concludes with a real-world case study illustrating how AI can be used to design next‑generation assessment experiences, including the creation of dynamic video, audio, and 3D content—grounding emerging concepts in practical application for I-O and talent leaders.

title: Pushing the Boundaries of Personality Science in the Workplace (Session ID 525)
tracks: Selection/Assessment/Individual Differences | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Bart Wille | Brian Connelly | Samiat Ishola | Sang Eun Woo | Sofie Dupré | Theresa Leyens

description: Wille, B. (Chair), & Connelly, B. S. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Pushing the boundaries of personality science in the workplace [symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Ishola, S., Hickman, L., & Koutsoumpis, A. (2026). Paraverbal cues and personality: A review of organizational research and demonstration of modern methods. Bonner, C. V., Woo, S. E., & Waters, S. (2026). Validity considerations for narrative-based personality assessment. Leyens, T., Lievens, F., & Hofmans, J. (2026). Behavioral signatures across role-playing assessments in multiple, speeded assessments. Dupré, S., Wille, B., & De Clercq, B. (2026). Volitional personality change for professional growth: A multi-layered investigation via a digital intervention. This symposium presents 4 contributions which each introduce novel perspectives to the science of personality at work. These innovations involve (a) the use of paraverbal cues as relevant sources of information, (b) the value of life narratives for personality assessment in organizations, (c) the analysis and validity of within-person variability in personality characteristics across assessments, and finally (d) the effectiveness of an intervention aimed at intentionally changing aspects of personality in the context of professional development.

title: Recent Advances in Research on Careless Responding to Surveys and Tests (Session ID 888)
speakers: David Sexton | Mark Ramsey | Rusty Wilson | Cory Moore
speaker_affiliations: University of Central Florida | Research Scientist | Hirevue

Examining the Interplay between Item Difficulty and Insufficient Effort Responding. This symposium presents recent discoveries in research regarding careless responding to surveys and cognitive ability tests. Specifically, this proposal contains 4 papers, including: (a) a construct validation of a new response time-based measure of carelessness to surveys, (b) a construct validation of an eye-tracking based measures of carelessness to surveys, (c) a meta-analysis on the prevalence of carelessness within different samples, and (d) a paper examining the effects of carelessness on data quality.

title: Rethinking Talent Management: Challenging Conventional Assumptions (Session Id 294)
speakers: Janine Waclawski | Allan Church | Gina Seaton | Seymour Adler | Steven Rogelberg
speaker_affiliations: Maestro Consulting LLC | Symbotic | Spencer Stuart | University of North Carolina at Charlotte
description: Waclawski, J. (Moderator), Adler, S., Church, A.H., Rogelberg, S.G. & Seaton, G. (2026). Rethinking talent management: Challenging conventional assumptions. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans LA, United States In today's fast-paced business landscape, where agility is paramount, organizations must regularly reassess their traditional views on how best to manage talent. Several of the core assumptions that have long guided talent management practices may no longer hold true. This session re-examines some key assumptions that organizations often make concerning talent management, drawing from findings emerging from I-O science and insights of leading scientist/practitioners. By reconsidering these assumptions, organizations can start to cultivate alternative, more effective, ways of managing talent.

title: SIOP Executive Board: AI in the Workplace: Reflections and Guidance From EEO Experts (Session ID 1459)
speakers: Charlotte Burrows | Emily Black | Jenny Yang | Christopher Nye
speaker_affiliations: New York University School of Law | New York University | Michigan State University
description: Nye, Christopher (Chair), Burrows, C., Yang, J., & Black, E. (2026). AI in the Workplace: Reflections and Guidance from EEO Experts [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly changing the workplace. For this session, Charlotte Burrows, former Chair of the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC), and Jenny Yang, former Chair of the EEOC and former Director of the Office of Federal Contract Compliance Programs (OFCCP), will discuss their views of the current legal landscape surrounding AI in hiring and how I-O psychology can contribute to this discussion. Then, along with a colleague, they will discuss approaches that computer scientists use to help prevent bias in AI models and the legal implications of these methods.

title: Secret Agents of DEI: Incorporating Best Practices Into Organizations (Session ID 967)
speakers: Alexandra Zelin | Kaitlin Ward | Nick Salter | Vivian Woo | Cindy Dziuba-Liu
speaker_affiliations: Wellstar Health System | Google | Hofstra University | Spellman High Voltage Electronics Corporation
description: Zelin, A. I. (Co-Chair), Salter, N. P. (Co-Chair), Dziuba-Liu, S.-N. C., Garland, E., Ward, K. P, & Woo, V. A. (2026). Secret agents of DEI: Incorporating best practices into organizations [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Amid anti-DEI backlash, organizations are reducing DEI efforts and devoting fewer resources to this area. However, there is still a need to advance this work. Authors propose that employees can be “secret agents” of DEI: individuals without formal DEI job titles who promote equity in various policies, procedures, and practices through their work. The session will feature practitioners who incorporate DEI into their daily jobs, followed by an academic discussing how scholars can conduct research to aid the work of DEI secret agents.

title: Stress as Offense to Self: New Understanding of Illegitimacy at Work (Session ID 1215)

description: Fila, M. J. (Co-Chair), & Rodriguez, W. A. (Co-Chair). (2026, April 30-May 2). Stress As Offense-to-Self: New Understanding of Illegitimacy at Work [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Illegitimate tasks are professional identity threatening stressors that violate normative prescriptions of what can be expected in a given role or position. By signaling a lack of respect towards employees and their roles, they leave employees feeling like “I should not have to do this!” Research on illegitimate tasks spans 17 years. However, new outcomes and boundary conditions continuing to be examined. Therefore, the purpose of this symposium is to highlight 5 novel, new findings in this research; including the first meta-analytic review of this construct, and a study of a new and related construct: the illegitimate absence of tasks.

title: Taking Action on Survey Data With a Deskless Workforce (Session ID 202)
speakers: Belinda Williams | Brian Jefferis | Drew Lam | Mallory Wright | Casey Witherspoon

description: Jefferis, B., Lam, A., Williams, B., Witherspoon, C., & Wright, M. (2026, April). Taking action on survey data with a deskless workforce [Alternative session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA. The purpose of this session is to learn from practitioners who work with deskless workers to take action on survey data to improve the employee experience. Although deskless workers make up the majority of the global workforce and are often included in employee listening surveys, this group is often overlooked in the postsurvey phase of taking action. This session will provide a platform for the panelists to share realistic learnings and solutions to real-world challenges. The audience will have opportunities to help us prioritize the direction of the conversation and ask direct questions.

title: Talent Hackathon AI Edition: Group Brainstorming for Talent Challenges in An AI World (Session ID 1207)

Society for Industrial and Organization Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Many I-O practitioners face similar challenges within their organizations. Through audience participation, this alternative session will crowdsource solutions to pressing AI-related organizational issues, from onboarding to talent strategy. Senior HR Leaders from 8 companies will present an AI talent challenge they are experiencing and then lead roundtable solutioning discussions.

Communication is central to team functioning, shaping not only performance but also adaptation, well-being, and recovery. This symposium features 5 papers that explore diverse drivers of communication (e.g., goals, leadership, work location), as well as its wide-ranging effects. These studies use innovative perspectives to deepen understanding about how team communication patterns emerge, and the critical role they play in team functioning. The results from this symposium provide direction for future research and practical guidance for organizations.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Emotions have become a central aspect of leadership in today’s evolving workplace. This symposium offers multidisciplinary perspectives on leadership as a nuanced emotional process. Four papers examine antecedents of leaders’ emotional influence, follower interpretations, relational dynamics, and the emotional costs leaders face when handling others’ emotions. Session concludes with a discussion of the future of leadership and emotion research in a rapidly changing work environment.

Papers span themes of accident reporting, safety attitudes, and predictive modeling. Designed for an intermediate audience, this session highlights diverse, data-driven methodologies that advance both the science and practice of occupational safety.

title: What Is Your Dangerous Idea? Reimagining the Future of Employee Surveys (Session ID 507)

(2026, April 30 – May 2). What is your dangerous idea? Reimagining the future of employee surveys [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Are I-Os too comfortable with the conventional employee survey playbook? This session brings together survey practitioners from diverse organizations to explore “dangerous ideas” that challenge long-held assumptions in employee surveying. These ideas provoke new ways of thinking and the unsettling possibility that they might be true. In this modified panel format, panelists will explore topics such as the role of surveys in the age of AI, the (over)emphasis on engagement metrics, and how scorecard incentives can distort data. This session invites attendees to reimagine the future of employee listening.

title: Will AI Save or Replace Assessment Centers? A Debate on the Future (Session ID 393)
speakers: Brad Chambers | Christina Norris-Watts | Doug Reynolds | Lynn Collins, Ph.D. | Michael Bazigos | Suzanne Tsacoumis

[Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming assessment centers, promising scalability, consistency, and predictive power. But can algorithms ever replace what makes ACs unique: observing live behavior, its consequences, and the powerful feedback and organizational learning that flow from it? In this debate, 4 panelists will take strong, opposing stances on motions such as “AI is more valid than human assessors” and “AI will render assessment centers obsolete.” Expect sparks to fly as authors confront validity, fairness, ethics, and the future relevance of assessment centers.

title: Withdrawal From Work: Contemporary Manifestations and Personality Predictors (Session ID 770)
speakers: Michael Hazboun | Deniz Ones | Brittany Mercado | Ziyu Ren | Stephan Dilchert

Withdrawal for Modern Work: Contemporary Manifestations and Personality Predictors [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Hazboun, M. K. A., & Ones, D. S. (2026). Affective and Self-Evaluative Personality Traits Predict Withdrawal: A Meta-Analysis . Dilchert, S., & Stanek, K. C. (2026). Beyond Disengagement: A Multidimensional Framework for Understanding Quiet Quitting Mercado, B. K. & Dilchert, S. (2026). Cyberloafing: A Distinct Manifestation of Withdrawal Ren, Z., Ones, D. S., & Giordano, C. (2026). Emotional Intelligence and Employee Disengagement: A Meta-Analysis. When withdrawal was conceptualized, employees were co-located and physically present meant psychologically engaged. Not anymore. Authors introduce an expanded framework encompassing contemporary forms such as quiet quitting, cyberloafing, disengagement, particularly invisible in remote/hybrid work. This symposium features 4 studies exploring withdrawal as theoretically distinct from other counterproductive behaviors and highlighting its personality nomological net. Findings from the meta-analyses and primary studies in this session are essential for anyone addressing engagement and retention at work.

title: AI in Employee Selection Update III: State, Federal, and Litigation Updates (Session ID 462)
speakers: Bre Timko | Cliff Haimann | David Schmidt | Niloy Ray

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The past year has brought major federal deregulatory-focused changes, and states have responded with new AI laws. AI-focused litigation continues, exposing both vendors and users to legal risk. Navigating these evolving regulations and challenges in AI-based employee selection remains difficult. This session will provide updates on federal, state, and international regulations and recent case law. This is the third iteration of this session at SIOP. Attendees will leave with a thorough understanding of the legal landscape, with a focus on updates between the SIOP 2025 and 2026 conferences.

title: Agentic AI at Work: Augmenting the Nature and Future of Work (Session ID 534)
speakers: Sean Noble | Yizhen Egyn Zhu | Eric Sydell | Louis Hickman | Ben Porr | Weiwen Nie
