Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA. This expert-led session examines psychological and organizational dynamics as AI systems take on autonomous, decision-making roles at work. Panelists will explore how organizations prepare employees to collaborate with agentic AI, where to draw boundaries around decision-making authority, and how human–AI collaboration may evolve as autonomy increases. Authors will discuss potential psychological impacts of daily interaction with highly autonomous systems and offer strategies for maintaining human agency while leveraging AI capabilities.

title: Beyond Average: Advances in Team Composition and Approaches to Multilevel Data (Session ID 1188)

Nguyen, Q., Nguyen, J., Rousseau, M., Grossman, R., & Feitosa, J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Temporal and dyadic perspectives on diversity, trust, and performance. Despite decades of research on team composition, I-Os still lack consistent answers about how to assemble people into effective teams. This symposium goes beyond traditional approaches. Authors examine how personality dispersion influences conflict over time and whether training interventions help, explore temporal dynamics showing how surface and deep-level diversity operate differently than previously thought, and introduce teammate expectations as a missing link between traits and outcomes. These studies offer fresh perspectives on team composition with implications for research and practice.

title: Beyond Engagement: Tackling Organizational Disconnection in Modern Work Psychology (Session ID 1129)
speakers: John Cooper | Autumn Krauss | Adrian Furnham | John Weiner | Kira Van Niekerk | Stephen Cuppello

Beyond engagement: Tackling organizational disconnection in modern work psychology [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Despite digital connectivity and AI tools, employees report rising loneliness and disengagement. This panel explores why engagement surveys fail to address the root cause, organizational disconnection, and examines its impact on culture, performance, and well-being. Drawing on research in work psychology, hybrid work, and AI-mediated sociality, authors propose connection as a fundamental part of organizations’ employee value proposition (EVP).

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Occupational Health Psychology (OHP) is an interdisciplinary research area within I-O that draws from insights across multiple disciplines. In this IGNITE session, presenters will discuss how OHP, in combination with engineering and computer science, human factors, psychoneuroimmunology, counseling psychology, sport management, and economics can innovatively address issues concerning employee health, safety, and well-being. The session aims to strengthen research and practice by encouraging audience members to consider a broader interdisciplinary lens in their approaches to worker health and safety.

title: Candidate Feedback Now, Next, and Beyond: Building a Better Applicant Experience (Session ID 388)

This panel will explore practical and innovative approaches to make feedback more impactful for both candidates and organizations. Authors discuss strategies for choosing the right feedback content and method of presentation as well as how to best incorporate technology (especially generative AI) to enhance feedback offerings while still maintaining authenticity and trust. Join us to learn practical tips for creating a valuable and supportive candidate feedback experience that resonates on a personal level.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As the I-O field grows and evolves, graduate training must prepare students to meet workforce demands. Yet, gaps remain in areas like people analytics, workforce development, and consulting skills. This alternative session combines panel presentations and roundtable discussions to explore these blind spots and equip students to proactively address them. Aimed at an introductory to intermediate audience, this session offers practical insights for students, early-career practitioners, and educators.

Closeness at a distance: Creating and maintaining social connections while working remotely. Remote work has grown exponentially over the past few years. The present session brings together emerging research and theory on remote worker well-being, including social well-being, job stress, sleep, and health behaviors, that illustrate a nuanced understanding of remote worker well-being and provides practical suggestions for improvement.

Topics include navigating a challenging job market, breaking into the field, experiences unique to new professionals, and adjusting from grad school to a career. Students, educators, mentors, and those looking for diverse perspectives on early career experiences are well-suited to this introductory discussion on the realities of navigating I-O careers post-covid.

title: High Impact HiPo Programs in Large Enterprises: Evidence, Practice, and Future Trends (Session ID 420)

High Impact HiPo programs in Large Enterprises: Evidence, Practice, & Future Trends. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. High-potential (HiPo) programs remain one of the most widely used and debated talent strategies in large organizations. They promise to secure leadership pipelines, accelerate business impact, and strengthen engagement. Yet, the evidence shows that programs are often inconsistently defined, unevenly applied, and insufficiently evaluated. This panel explores current and future applications of HiPo programs from seasoned practitioners in enterprise companies while also sharing results from a global 2025 HiPo study.

This symposium examines how employees manage invisible disabilities and the associated costs and benefits. Papers explore (a) daily masking among women with ADHD, (b) recovery as a buffer for ASD/ADHD employees, and (c) boundary management in chronic illness. Together, authors offer theory, methods, and practices for more inclusive workplaces.

Me, myself, and AI: How I-Os are using AI for personal development [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial intelligence isn’t just coming: It’s already here and reshaping everything. The I-O field can’t afford to sit on the sidelines. Yet, few SIOP presentations have explored how AI can fuel personal growth for practitioners themselves. In this session, you’ll hear how I-Os are experimenting with AI to supercharge their own learning, streamline their work, and expand their impact. In IGNITE format, presenters will share what sparked their curiosity, how they discovered AI’s potential, and the tangible ways these skills have transformed them both personally and professionally.

title: Measuring Creativity: Exploring Assessment Challenges and Innovations (Session ID 55)
speakers: Jackie Martin Kowal | Cary Stothart | Roni Reiter-Palmon

Exploring Assessment Challenges and Innovations [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel will explore the opportunities and challenges of measuring creativity and innovative thinking within organizations. Panelists will represent academic, applied, and government perspectives, addressing questions of validity, fairness, and practical application. Discussion will highlight current evidence, emerging technologies, and contexts in which creativity measures may or may not be appropriate.

title: Modeling Mindsets for Modern Organizations: Cultivating Statistical Thinking in I-O (Session ID 941)
speakers: Denvar Summers | Karyssa Courey

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Statistical training remains central to I-O psychology, yet most curricula still rely on null hypothesis significance testing (NHST). This session advocates a model-based approach, positioning the general linear model as the conceptual foundation for teaching, research, and practice. Participants will explore how this framework unifies statistical methods, fosters deeper reasoning, and aligns with the data-rich demands of modern organizations. An interactive CPD exercise will guide attendees in developing actionable plans to enhance model-based fluency.

This symposium highlights 3 such areas: egg freezing, fertility treatment, and pregnancy loss. Across qualitative and quantitative studies, the papers examine how employees navigate disclosure, emotions, and organizational support when facing these experiences. Together, they reveal critical gaps in workplace policies and current literature, and with the discussant, the symposium seeks to integrate these contributions, pointing toward the need for more inclusive practices and a broader research agenda.

title: Partner Showcase: Pinsight - AI is Ready. Are your Leaders? What 14,000 Leadership Assessments Reveal About Why AI Adoption Stalls
speakers: Martin Lanik | Amy Huber
speaker_affiliations: Pinsight
description: Organizations are investing heavily in Artificial Intelligence (AI), yet many struggle to move beyond pilots and demonstrations to achieve enterprise-scale impact. Most conversations focus on technology and data readiness, but our research suggests a different constraint: leadership capability. Drawing on a global sample of 14,000 leaders assessed through behavioral simulations and personality measures, this session examines how leadership readiness for AI varies across senior executives, mid-level leaders, and frontline managers. The findings reveal a consistent pattern: organizations can define AI strategy but struggle to translate it into coordinated execution at each level. Senior executives often face challenges leading large-scale transformation, mid-level leaders struggle to translate AI strategy into operational change, and frontline managers lack the skills to guide workforce adoption. By highlighting patterns across industries and regions, attendees will leave with a data-driven view of what leadership capabilities must shift to turn AI investment into sustained business impact.

Impact of Reframed Instructions and Practice Testing on Test Performance of Diverse Candidate Groups Preparing for employment tests is a growing area of research that recognizes test performance depends not only on ability, but also on preparation and context. Practice can enhance performance by reducing anxiety and increasing familiarity. Yet preparation is influenced by test characteristics and individual differences, raising important questions about fairness and validity. Understanding how, when, and for whom practice helps remains essential to equitable assessment and ensuring valid hiring decisions are made.

title: Practicing What We Preach: Integrating “I” and “O” Psychology in Applied Fieldwork (Session ID 559)

Practicing What We Preach: Integrating “I” & “O” Psychology in Applied Fieldwork [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will address the traditionally distinct “I” and “O” sides of the field, in which the I-side has historically focused on the assessment and measurement of KSAOs, and the O-side has focused on the context of assessment and measurement in relation to the social, emotional, and health consequences of individuals. Presenters will provide applied examples of how the traditionally “distinct” sides of our field are heavily integrated through their work. This IGNITE! session highlights the interconnection between the “I” and “O” in practice, showing how insights from both sides are used by practitioners to address workplace challenges.

title: Recommitting to Coaching in the Era of AI (Session ID 125)
speakers: Gina Thoebes | Marc Sokol | Jonathan Reitz
speaker_affiliations: Arizona Cardinals | Sage Consulting Resources | FLUXIFY

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will bring together a panel with expertise in coaching and AI in human development for an interactive discussion on the evolving practice of coaching in this era of AI. Topics will include discussion of best practices on how to incorporate AI into coaching to drive impact for clients, without casting aside our core capabilities. Authors will cover lessons learned and how coaches can create and measure their impact, plus a comparison of coaching interventions with AI compared to other talent development approaches.

title: Simple, Seriously: Applying the Power of Simplicity to Employee Listening (Session ID 759)
speakers: Boris Genzer | Zachary Warman | Christopher Adair | Yael Oelbaum | Brandy Parker

Listening programs often grow complex—long surveys, crowded cadences, heavy action plans, and dense reports. This practitioner panel shows how 4 global organizations simplified their listening strategies to boost participation, data quality, and action. Drawing on research and lived experience, panelists share what worked, what didn’t, and practical guidance for organizations starting or redesigning their engagement programs.

title: The Career Carousel: Insights Into the Job Hopping Phenomenon (Session ID 793)
speakers: Aarti Polavarapu | Sabrina Yu | Asha Isaacs | Andy Kemp | Garett Foster | Jenna-Lyn Roman | Margaret Brooks

The Career Carousel: Insights into the Job Hopping Phenomenon [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The aim of this panel is to explore the evolving phenomenon of job hopping, focusing on its motivations, perceptions, and implications for employees and organizations. Panelists will discuss what defines job hopping, how it has evolved over time, gender, generational, and industry differences, perceptions surrounding it, and its impact on careers. This panel aims to offer a comprehensive understanding of job hoping by blending research on this topic with personal experiences of job hopping.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will provide an update on the work that has been completed in the creation of a certification in I-O psychology. This work includes endorsement from SIOP, the results of a Job Task Analysis survey, and the progress of an item writing committee. Additional discussion will include how the certification exam content aligns with SIOP’s Education and Training Guidelines, as well as the extent that those practicing I-O psychology may harm the public. Finally, future steps in the completion of the I-O psychology certification exam, along with an expected launch date, will be presented.

title: The Personal and Organizational Costs of Unhelpful and Imbalanced Support at Work (Session ID 1127)

(2026, April 30 – May 2). When helping hurts: The behavioral impact of support imbalance. Social support is intuitively beneficial, but the research around support in the workplace is complex and inconsistent. Social support that is actually unhelpful (e.g., undermining confidence, unwanted) or imbalanced can even have negative effects. Three papers highlight nuances of how unhelpful social support can affect workers, increasing feelings of loneliness, depriving workers of core psychological needs, and reducing engagement. In addition, authors present how imbalanced support relates to fewer organizational citizenship behaviors and more counterproductive workplace behaviors.

title: Unpacking Creativity With AI: A Phase-Based Exploration of Creative Problem-Solving Activities (Session ID 982)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The goal of this IGNITE! session is twofold. First, authors attempt to challenge the notion that AI is strictly used for idea generation. Although several presentations in the session focus on idea generation, they explore the capabilities AI serve as a tool for prompt engineering, idea evaluation, and implementation planning. Second, they focus on AI being used in the malevolently creative process by using a custom built, unconstrained model.

title: When Engagement Becomes a Performance Metric: Ensuring Fair Use in Evaluations (Session ID 382)

Organizations often include engagement scores as part of manager performance criteria to drive accountability and improve team outcomes. However, this can place undue pressure on managers, as many factors affecting engagement lie beyond their control. This raises a key question: How can organizations ensure fair use of engagement scores in evaluations? In this panel, experts in employee listening will explore diverse perspectives on the benefits and challenges of this approach and share best practices to balance fairness and accountability.

title: Midday Break
I-O Psychology for Great Good

Whether it’s an ESG initiative, a community pantry, or volunteer work, this lunchtime meetup invites attendees to celebrate impact and explore how we can support one another in making an even greater difference, both within the profession and beyond.

Advancing the Science of Team Assessment: Tools and Insights for High-Stakes Teams [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Teams are critical to success in military and other high-stakes environments, yet assessing their performance requires methods that capture both strengths and weaknesses without disrupting the mission. This symposium presents 4 complementary efforts advancing reliable and contextually relevant team assessment. Presentations highlight tools for assessing teamwork behaviors in real time, capturing the emergence of collective cognition, identifying team dysfunction, and leveraging digital platforms to integrate team and individual assessments to support and improve team performance.

title: Board Characteristics' Diverse Impacts on Sustainability (Session ID 987)
speakers: Stephan Dilchert | Phoebe Hessen | Sydney Higley | Yagizhan Yazar
speaker_affiliations: Zicklin School of Business | University of Minnesota | College of Liberal Arts, University of Minnesota Twin Cities | Tapestry Inc.
description: Dilchert, S. (Co-Chair & Discussant), Ren, Z. (Non-Speaking Co-Chair), & Ones, D.S. (Non-Speaking Co-Chair). (2026, April 30 - May 2). Board Characteristics' Diverse Impacts on Sustainability [Sympoisum]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conferene, New Orleans, LA, United States. Four studies analyzing several thousand organizations worldwide indicate that board gender diversity enhances resource conservation and stakeholder engagement but not transformative innovation or harm prevention. Furthermore, these effects reverse across cultures: board gender diversity predicts greater sustainability in Asia and Anglo countries but reduced sustainability in Latin Europe. Larger boards enhance change-oriented initiatives but not compliance behaviors. CEO duality shows negligible effects. These findings provide guidance for organizations designing boards to embed social and environmental sustainability.

title: Cognitive Ability in Personnel Selection: What Is Its Place Today? (Session ID 479)

Cognitive Ability in Personnel Selection: What Is Its Place Today? [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Recent meta-analytic research examining cognitive ability and job performance, focused on data from contemporary studies, has produced validity estimates far lower than the traditionally accepted value of .51 produced by Schmidt and Hunter (1998). Researchers agree, however, that regardless of one’s perspective on the magnitude of the estimate, cognitive ability continues to play an important role in the overall world of personnel selection. Panelists will discuss perspectives from research and practice regarding when and how cognitive ability may best be utilized in selection systems today.

title: Consulting Gumbo: Stirring Up Clarity on Internal and External Consulting Roles (Session ID 567)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Industrial-organizational (I-O) practitioners often refer to themselves broadly as an external or an internal consultant, but the true nature of these roles varies significantly depending on many factors. In this alternative session, panelists with broad applied experiences will shed light on internal and external I-O consulting by sharing their experiences and cover career topics rarely covered in graduate school. Following a panel question and answer segment, the audience will be invited to join breakout groups to learn more about consulting career paths they are interested in exploring.

This symposium comprises 4 timely empirical papers that seek to contribute to current understanding of how workers and job seekers are engaging in work-relevant self-directed learning inside and outside organizations. Using a diverse set of quantitative and qualitative methodological approaches, presenters will discuss how educational, technological, and social contextual factors influence work-relevant self-directed learning outcomes across the career span. The session includes an expert discussant in the field of workplace learning synthesizing takeaways for research and practice.

title: Fear and Loathing in Leadership Research (Session ID 681)

(2026, April 30 – May 2). Fear and Loathing in Leadership Research: Where We Are Now and Where We Need to Go [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Leadership research is currently undergoing a period of fear and loathing, similar to that described in Hunter Thompson’s classic 1970s novel. In recent years, leadership research has been widely critiqued for its lack of theoretical cohesion and methodological shortcomings. Yet, although many have raised problems with leadership research, the path forward is unclear. This session addresses these concerns by bringing together some of the field's most accomplished leadership scholars to summarize the current problems with leadership research and provide a vision for how they can be addressed.

title: From Art to Science: Rethinking the Discipline of Organizational Design (Session ID 1173)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizational design has long been treated as an art rather than a science. This panel examines how I-O psychology can enhance the rigor of organizational design—linking structure, roles, and decision-making rights to behavioral outcomes and business performance. Through research and real-world redesigns, panelists will demonstrate how data-driven, human-centered design can align strategy, culture, and capabilities to build agile, high-performing organizations and bridge the micro-meso-macro gap, as called for in the literature and currently missing in the I-O community discourse.

title: From Disruption to Direction: Charting the Future of Organizational Practices (Session ID 478)

From Disruption to Direction: Charting the Future of Organizational Practices. [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. With the ever-evolving landscapes of economics, geopolitics, and technology, the organizations that are flexible and agile remain ahead of the rest. Organizations can develop resilience through strategic enhancements to recruitment and selection, leadership development, talent management, employee listening, and people analytics. This IGNITE session offers both data- and research-driven approaches to implementing these practices, with an emphasis on how evolving practices in these areas foster resilience, maintain stability, and allow organizations to succeed amid external challenges.

title: From Insights to Impact: Building Dashboard Suites That HRBPs Actually Use (Session ID 1320)

In today’s data-driven organizations, HR dashboards often fall short of driving action. This 50-minute cross-company panel brings together experts from HP Inc., DICK’S Sporting Goods, Comcast, and AbbVie to explore why dashboards go unused—and what to do about it. Through real-world examples and interactive discussion, panelists share proven strategies to build dashboard suites that HR Business Partners actually use to make informed, impactful decisions.

title: Generative Artificial Intelligence in Workplace Assessment Development: Pros and Cons (Session ID 1254)

Generative artificial intelligence in workplace assessment development: Pros and cons [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology, New Orleans, LA, United States. Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) holds promise for many research and practical applications in workplace settings, but remains understudied in many important fields such as workplace psychological assessment. In this symposium, we demonstrate the use of GenAI to develop and categorize multiple item types for 6 psychological constructs, discuss the advantages and limitations of GenAI in developing and validating psychological assessment items for the workplace, potential next steps and, in lieu of a discussant, reserve ample time for audience discussion.

This symposium focuses on the roles of holistic and precise career assessments, meaningful career feedback, and social and relational considerations in guiding career decisions. Together, these studies highlight ways to support choices that are both informed and personally aligned.

title: Motivation, Attitudes, and Emotions Posters
My Worst I-O Day Ever: Turning Nightmares Into Wisdom (Session ID 190)

My Worst I-O Day Ever: Turning Nightmares into Wisdom [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Setbacks are unavoidable in organizations, from failed initiatives to major crises. However, adversity can also spark growth, innovation, and deeper engagement. In this impactful panel, experienced practitioners will share their “worst I-O day ever,” including unexpected disasters, leadership shifts, and personal setbacks—the tough challenges that grow us. Attendees will hear honest stories, lessons learned, and practical strategies for turning setbacks into opportunities that help individuals and organizations to not just recover but also learn and move forward.

In a year of regulatory whiplash, organizations face major shifts in civil rights enforcement. From the revocation of EO 11246 to OFCCP’s funding rollercoaster, and new EEOC-DOJ guidance on “illegal DEI,” compliance professionals must adapt quickly. This panel offers updates on EEOC and OFCCP enforcement, pay equity, AI regulation, and election-related impacts. Legal experts, practitioners, and employers will share insights on navigating the science-law intersection amid political uncertainty and preparing for what’s next.

title: Partner Showcase: Deloitte - Work Design in the Age of Humans x Machines: AI Value Realization
speakers: Sarah Szpaichler Stough
speaker_affiliations: Deloitte Consulting
description: Organizations are increasing AI investment, yet many aren’t seeing expected returns because technology-first strategies are under-emphasizing the human advantage, a key driver of competitive differentiation. This session explores an approach to Humans × Machines collaboration including how to orchestrate unique human skills and machine capability to deliver exponential outcomes. Grounded in tipping points highlighted in Deloitte’s 2026 Global Human Capital Trends , we’ll share activities that accelerate value realization through Work Design Engineering: clear outcomes, trustworthy decisioning, data/process readiness, adoption by design, and intentional work design. We’ll include a brief visualization of Deloitte’s Work Design Canvas to illustrate how teams re-balance work across automation, augmentation, and uniquely human strengths.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Best practices are more accessible than ever, but adoption remains one of the I-O psychologist’s greatest challenges. Four practitioners with experience in various talent and analytics disciplines will share practical recommendations (e.g., collaboration with HR business partners, actionable design) for driving adoption of programs such as goal-setting, engagement surveys, and people analytics. Following these presentations, the session will shift to a panel discussion and audience questions.

Four speakers will describe how they use lessons from movement, mindfulness, and mirth to breathe life into organizational practices. Using both the IGNITE presentation and small group discussion formats, audience members will hear examples of restorative practices and reflect on how they can use similar practices in their applied work.

Reimagining meetings: Sharing research and practical lessons to inspire organizational interventions [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Employees spend a lot of time in meetings, with organizations questioning whether they drive value or drain productivity. Scholars have highlighted the costs of poor quality meetings, whereas organizations like Shopify have gone so far as to cancel meetings to reset norms, with new technology like AI expanding the possibilities. This session will bring together meetings experts and I-O practitioners to share research and real-world examples of interventions that improved meeting quality, followed by small-group discussions where participants will propose their own interventions.

title: Strategies for Building An AI-Literate Workforce (Session ID 150)

(Panelist), Prabhu, N. (Panelist), & Theys, E. R. (Panelist) (2026) Strategies for Building an AI-Literate Workforce [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial intelligence (AI) is a rapidly emerging technology that is quickly changing how work is done in the 21st century. Due to its increasingly vital role, it is essential that employees understand how AI works, how it is most effectively applied, and how to leverage it responsibly. This session gathers accomplished I-O psychologists to discuss how their organizations are building AI literacy. The conversation will focus on creating an AI-literate workforce through training, development, and personnel selection, offering attendees practical insights for navigating the evolving workplace.

Bowling, N. A., Lyons, B. D, Sexton, D., Mullaney, K., & Wood, D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Developing and Validating a Context-Specific CWB Scale in the U.S. Military. Mercado, B. Unique features of the academic counterproductive work behavior ecosystem. Counterproductive work behavior (CWB) research has often treated context (e.g., occupation or industry) as incidental. This symposium addresses that limitation by examining CWB across diverse settings, including the military, law enforcement, nursing, and academia. Presenters will illustrate how context shapes the nature, antecedents, and consequences of CWB, revealing both commonalities and unique patterns. Collectively, these insights highlight the theoretical and practical value of situating CWB within the work environment.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Industrial-organizational (I-O) psychologists are well-positioned to close the gap between analysis and action in People Analytics. This session highlights projects where insights were translated into structural change: redesigning succession planning to emphasize readiness and accountability, using predictive models to guide stock-based retention, rebuilding leadership frameworks with behavioral clarity, tailoring dashboards to decision-making, and standardizing quality-of-hire metrics. Together, these examples show how I-O psychology ensures analytics is not just descriptive but actionable.

This master tutorial focuses on the use of data visualization to properly communicate information and persuade public audiences. Authors will discuss best practices in data visualization and potential pitfalls. They will also conduct hands-on demonstrations of Tableau, a powerful software used for data visualization and dashboards but rarely taught in psychology programs. Participants will leave the workshop with enough experience to build basic static and dynamic data visualizations using a sample dataset from the organizational sciences.

title: When Science Meets Software: Applying I-O Skills in An HR Tech World (Session ID 587)
speakers: Anna Hulett | Colby Nesbitt | Allie Wehling | Marian Pitel-Wali

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The scientist–practitioner model is colliding with HR tech. Most tools weren’t built by I-Os—or for the strategies I-Os champion—yet they must still make them work. How do you uphold I-O principles when the tech isn’t a perfect fit? This panel convenes I-Os working inside HR tech companies —in product, customer-facing, and internal people roles—in a conversation moderated by an HR tech customer. This panel session equips I-Os to overcome real-world limitations, influence roadmaps, and navigate vendor positioning—while keeping scientific integrity and business impact front and center.

These employer-imposed involuntary nonstandard work schedules significantly impact workers’ well-being and work attitudes. This symposium brings together 3 papers that examine the burdens, underlying mechanisms, and potential interventions associated with organizationally mandated nonstandard schedules across diverse occupational contexts.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI and emerging tech are reshaping collaboration across academia, industry, and government. Yet, differing views on rigor, relevance, and success pose challenges and opportunities. Panelists will share insights on building trust, transparency, and innovation, offering practical strategies for sustainable, high-impact partnerships in an AI-driven world.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Intersectionality is often invoked but rarely practiced with rigor. This 80-minute interactive alternative session moves beyond definitions to applied skill-building and provides participants with practical post-session guidance, prompts, and references via a 1-page toolkit. Focusing on the core tenets of intersectionality, authors will guide a live identity mapping activity and facilitate a scenario lab with multiple cases that require participants to diagnose intersectional blind spots and choose evidence-based actions to resolve them.

title: Beyond Stress and Burnout: Advancing Moral Injury in Organizational Settings (Session ID 1225)

Crayne, M.P., Shortland III, N.D., & Griffith, J.A. (2026). Conceptualizing Antecedents and Outcomes of Workplace Moral Injury. Crayne, M.P., Fletcher, K.A., & Cheung, H.K. (2026). Cortez, J. & Fletcher, K.A. (2026). Organizational Dehumanization Differentially Impacts Exchange Quality with All Levels of the Organization. This symposium examines moral injury as a values-based trauma mechanism linking organizational failures to employee harm. Authors show that moral transgressions and organizational dehumanization function as potentially morally injurious events. These events elicit destructive moral emotions (e.g., shame), which, when amplified by psychological proximity or an antiwork orientation, accelerate strain and reduce organizational commitment. These studies provide novel, evidence-based mechanisms for researchers and practitioners to address moral hazards embedded in contemporary work life.

Navigating cleft habitus: A grounded theory study of upward class transitioners in professional workplaces. Traditional leadership frameworks have perpetuated a narrowly defined leader prototype (typically White, male, higher social class), resulting in the exclusion and undervaluation of the unique strengths held by leaders with marginalized identities. This symposium challenges such restrictive narratives about who is an ideal leader by first examining the cognitive and relational mechanisms through which these biases manifest in organizational settings, and then reorienting the conversation around the inherent value, agency, and unique strengths that arise from experiences navigating the margins.

During the session, two Workera experts will build assessments live using audience-generated prompts, allowing attendees to see how different skill domains, audiences, and use cases can be translated into high-quality, job-relevant assessments. The session is intentionally participatory and educational, providing a clear view into agentic AI assessment design without the feel of a traditional sales presentation.

title: Practical Applications of Artificial Intelligence for Learning and Development (Session ID 1172)
speakers: Amy DuVernet | Alyssa Kaszycki | Sarah Bienkowski

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session explores the practical and ethical use of artificial intelligence (AI) in learning and development (L&D). Participants will engage with real-world examples of how AI can support content creation, analyze course evaluation data, and generate improvement recommendations. The session also addresses common concerns around data quality, bias, and responsible use, equipping attendees with tools and insights to apply AI thoughtfully in their own L&D work.

title: Reimagining Family-Supportive Workplaces: Emerging Models, Populations, and Patterns (Session ID 680)

Supervisor support, work-family conflict, and citizenship behavior: A test of competing theoretical perspectives. Warner, B. M., & Barratt, C. L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The Impact of Giving and Receiving Support on the Work-Family Domain. Work–family support remains a pressing issue for organizations hoping to protect and retain employees and, therefore, also for organizational researchers working to describe and explain it. Across 4 diverse projects (i.e., curvilinear effects, competing longitudinal mediations, social network considerations, a review of research on a special population) integrated by an expert discussant (Dr. Julie Wayne), the symposium reframes support as more than a simple direct predictor or buffer of work–family outcomes—instead as a complex, exchange-based, and sometimes demanding phenomenon. Authors will discuss new insights for measuring, theorizing, and intervening when supporting work–family issues.

speakers: Jackie Sahm | Jocelyn Hays | Julianne Brown-Meola | Laura Heaton

Talent Malpractice: The “Dark Arts” That Erode Our Profession [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This alternative-format Fishbowl Tribunal will transform the audience into co-conspirators in exposing the "dark arts" of talent management. An expert panel will share de-identified stories of "talent malpractice"—real cases of HR pseudoscience—followed by live discussion, audience participation, and a collective "verdict." Attendees will experience the tension between evidence and politics, science and myth, and draft guidelines for reform. This fast-paced session combines storytelling, improvisation, and audience engagement to elevate I-O professional accountability for all.

Collectively, these presentations move from examining applicant and evaluator reactions to AI in asynchronous interviews to unpacking the nonverbal communication challenges and structural inequities these technologies can introduce. Authors will conclude with a discussion on how employers can responsibly leverage technological innovations to attract and select top talent.

Examining the Efficacy of Large Language Models for Thematic Content Analysis. Although capturing conversations within teams offer a fruitful avenue for understanding team dynamics, this type of data collection strategy yields large, complex data sets that can be difficult and time-consuming to manage. Recent advancements in AI have created opportunities for different analytic techniques as well as removing barriers to managing larger qualitative data sets. This symposium features 4 projects that highlight strategies for using AI in analysis of teams data. Importantly, authors end with a discussion with the former JOB editor on ethical considerations of using AI in research.

A Cross-Cultural and Dyadic Examination of Leader-Member Exchange Measurements Equivalence Leadership remains a central yet poorly understood topic, partly due to methodological limitations. Most studies rely on static perceptions of leadership styles rather than actual behaviors, overlooking the dynamic, interpersonal processes between leaders and followers. Data are often collected from a single source and analyzed unidirectionally, neglecting mutual influence. This symposium addresses these gaps through innovative methods that advance leadership research as a dynamic, dyadic, behavioral phenomenon.

title: Unpacking Team Coordination: A Multimethod Dynamic Approach (Session Id 808)

Adaptive Team Coordination Patterns: The Role of Leadership and Event Disruptiveness in Shaping Team Coordination Coordination is a dynamic, adaptive process essential for teamwork, yet much research relies on static, cross-sectional approaches. This symposium presents 5 papers that move beyond snapshots to capture coordination as it unfolds. Contributions include qualitative insights from adversarial contexts, a 2-dimensional framework for planning vs real-time coordination, neural network modeling of multimodal data, validation of objective trace metrics, and studies on leadership and disruptive events. Together, these papers advance theory, methods, and assessment strategies for team coordination.

Collectively, the presentations span from micro (time use) to macro (state policy) perspectives, integrating multiple theoretical lenses (e.g., conservation of resources, social comparison), and demonstrating diverse methodological approaches (archival data, stratified random sampling, vignette, time use data) to advance benefit practices.

title: lavaan Will Fit It, But Should We? Rethinking Fit, Judgment and Responsibility in SEM (Session ID 1237)
speakers: Kalifa Thomas | Navrose Bajwa | Bo Zhang | Jose Cortina | Kaylee Litson | Mel Simone

description: Thomas, K. N. (Co-Chair), Bajwa, N. (Co-Chair), Cortina, J., Litson, K., Simone, M. & Zhang, B. (2026, April 30 – May 2). lavaan Will Fit It, But Should We? Rethinking Fit, Judgment, and Responsibility in SEM [Panel] Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Structural equation modeling (SEM) is a common tool in organizational science because it unites theory, latent variables, and complex designs in one framework. Yet its popularity can come with recurring problems: weak reporting, overfit “pretty” models, cross-sectional designs interpreted casually and thin handling of missing data and power. This panel brings methodologists and applied researchers together to discuss (a) where SEM uniquely advances organizational theory (b) common missteps and pragmatic fixes and (c) additional design and argument clarification for causal claims and replication.

Sexual harassment has been addressed in company training and policies, but the focus has often been on adult workers, whereas sexual harassment targeted towards young workers has been a hidden risk that needs to be evaluated. This symposium focuses on the psychological, physical, and behavioral effects on young victims. Presenters will share empirical, theoretical, and practical results using a variety of methodologies (i.e., surveys, interviews, videos), settings (i.e., high schools, universities, businesses) and countries (i.e., America, Australia, UK). A discussant will review the papers.

A Panel About Panels: New Trends in Crowdsourced Research Panels [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Crowdsourced research platforms have gained prominence in organizational research. However, both the research landscape and technologies are continually evolving, which may in various ways impact how I-Os should use these platforms to ensure data quality. A panel of 5 researchers—representing academia, industry, and internal platform work—is convened to discuss what new opportunities and challenges may emerge with crowdsourcing platforms. In doing so, the session will contribute to continued, creative, and rigorous research using these platforms.

title: Applications of Different Modifications of the Angoff Approach for Cut Score Setting (Session ID 448)

Lin, Y., Beazley, C., & DeKoekkoek, P. (2026). From Complex Models to Clear Decisions: Proficiency Banding in IRT-Based Assessments of Behavioral Skills. With an increased focus on skills, criterion-referenced cut scores set using the Angoff approach offer a way for organizations to make hiring decisions based on whether a candidate has a skill. The Angoff approach has limitations. This symposium discusses modifications to the Angoff approach to address different limitations like unexplained variance in ratings and SMEs underestimating the difficulty of items. The approach can also be modified for soft skills. This session will be informative to individuals unfamiliar with criterion-referenced cut score setting and experienced practitioners.

title: Beyond AI Angst: Early I-O Adopters Share Experiences and Perspectives (Session ID 455)
speakers: Julian Allen | Eric Sydell | Jürgen Bank | Jared Weintraub | Riley Morrison

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, CO, United States of America As of October 2025, 94% of US workers use AI to get work done, and yet 77% of them suffer from AI angst - broad concerns over job security, privacy, lack of confidence, and loss of control - which stifles the potential value of GenAI. The Panelists - self-identified GenAI Early adopters - will share their experiences across large, medium, and small enterprises, discussing practical strategies and experiences across industries. The session aims to inspire curiosity, encouragement and confidence for using GenAI and to equip I-Os to lead effective and responsible implementations in the workplace.

Artificial intelligence (AI) tools are widely adopted across academia and practice to assist with qualitative data analysis. Experienced research practitioners in industrial-organizational and educational psychology will discuss project, personnel, and social considerations driving their decisions to incorporate AI into their qualitative analysis workflow. Attendees will gain insights into where panelists have benefited from using AI as well as unexpected challenges. This session will offer guidance for researchers navigating the evolving landscape of AI-assisted qualitative analysis.

title: Fact or Fiction? Testing What You Know About Training Evaluation (Session ID 1391)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. How much do you really know about evaluating learning and development? In this interactive, game-show-style session, participants will test their knowledge of training evaluation myths. Using live polling, we’ll challenge common misconceptions about training evaluation methods, models and metrics, and share research that refutes them. Participants will have the opportunity to benchmark against lay L&D professionals’ knowledge of training evaluation and will walk away with evidence-based strategies to enhance their own evaluation practices and better demonstrate L&D impact.

title: Future-Ready Leaders: Build Them or Buy Them? (Session ID 733)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As businesses navigate accelerated change, the concept of "future-ready leadership" has emerged as a critical capability. This panel explores a fundamental question facing talent management professionals: Can future-ready leadership competencies be developed through targeted interventions, or do they represent relatively stable individual differences that organizations must identify and select for? Panelists will draw from research on leadership development, personality psychology, and change management to reach a conclusion on whether future-ready leadership should be bought or built.

title: Immigrants in the Workplace: New Directions for Research and Practice (Session ID 858)

Immigrants in the Workplace: New Directions for Research and Practice [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Immigrants remain an overlooked population in I-O psychology, despite being vital to the global workforce. This session brings together experts studying immigrant, refugee, and undocumented worker experiences to discuss research on barriers to employment and equity. After brief presentations on the state of the science, attendees will participate in breakout discussions to generate research questions for advancing immigrant inclusion in organizations. This session will serve as an introduction to immigrant research and a space for scholars to generate new ideas and develop new collaborations.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States of America. This panel will present research and applied experience on how artificial intelligence currently impacts disability inclusion in the workplace. Exploration of its interaction will include selection, accommodations/accessibility, and job engagement. Topics will also include the future of AI and its impacts, organizational implementation, and potential dangers of AI. This panel will examine how mental/cognitive disabilities, as well as physical disabilities, are affected, provide practical guidance, and discuss current research to assess the current state of interaction and its direction.

There’s an emerging trend in HR and People Analytics teams: we’re seeing tighter connections between these functions and IT. This practitioner-focused panel brings together professionals who are navigating these new, closer relationships - through accountability that spans these functions in a variety of ways. Each panelist will briefly share how IT and HR have come together in their organizations, followed by a group discussion of trends, successes and pitfalls.

Authors discuss a new measure of corporate quality of hire developed and validated using publicly available data on new hire outcomes and corporate financial performance. They hear from 3 organizations with exceptionally high scores on this metric. Marriot International, Delta Airlines and Expedia Group discuss aspects of their company culture, job design, recruiting practices, and onboarding methods that contribute to their exceptional quality of hire outcomes. The panel concludes with insights from a leading researcher on methods for capturing new hire value.

Participants will join small group table discussions and will be able to move freely between tables to learn about multiple topics. This event is designed for I-O practitioners with 5+ years of full-time experience.

description: Vaidya, H. (Chair), Dziuba-Liu, S.-N., C (Co-Chair), Ong, X. W. (Co-Chair), Nasrallah, V. (Co-Chair), Dray, K., Caylor, J., Gaskins, V., Hegan, E., Baker, N. (2026). Pragmatic best practices: Balancing practicality & rigor in applied qualitative work [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Qualitative methods are innovative and underused tools for I-O psychologists to address organizational challenges in both research and practice. They are especially valuable for capturing employee experiences and uncovering key processes, though their use often involves trade-offs due to organizational constraints. This session brings together 6 I-O psychologists with practitioner and research experience for a combined panel and roundtable discussion on balancing rigor and practicality when applying these methods in real-world settings.

title: Product Showcase: Deloitte - Agentic Change: Using AI to Personalize Adoption and Elevate Human Leadership
speakers: Chloe Domergue
speaker_affiliations: Deloitte
description: Organizations invest in transformation, yet adoption stalls because change approaches are static and disconnected from how people work. Domergue’s work introduces a “new change paradigm” and an agentic change model that embeds AI agents into the flow of work to deliver personalized nudges and adoption interventions grounded in behavioral science. These agent networks detect friction points, tailor support to motivation and context, and adapt based on response. The goal is not to replace change leaders, but to augment them. By automating behavioral interventions at scale, agentic systems free practitioners to focus on higher-value work: aligning leaders, strengthening relationship capital, shaping employee experience, and making context-sensitive decisions. Use cases will be woven into the dialogue, showing application in regulated environments while preserving trust, engagement, and psychological safety. Rooted in I-O principles such as reinforcement and job design, this approach reframes change as a human–AI partnership designed to build adaptive organizations.

title: SIOP Executive Board: Practitioner Needs Workshop: Cocreating SIOP Support for Practitioners (Session ID 1442)
tracks: I-O Psychologist Career Development/Teaching | Special Session
speakers: Amanda Valone | Heather Flattery | Jackie Martin Kowal | Kelsey Byerly | Dr. William Shepherd
speaker_affiliations: PDRI by Pearson | PDRI | Elevation Leadership, LLC
description: Martin Kowal, J. (Chair), Shepard, W., Byerly, K. R., Valone, A, & Flattery, H. (2025). Practitioner needs workshop: Co-creating SIOP support for practitioners [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This interactive working session invites attendees to directly shape how SIOP supports its practitioner community, with a critical focus on early career practitioners who are known to be at a higher risk of attrition. The session will begin with a brief overview of the current efforts from SIOP’s Professional Practice Committee. The remaining time will be dedicated to small group discussions where attendees are invited to share their suggestions on what they need and want from SIOP. Insights gathered will be synthesized and shared with the Executive Board to directly inform future strategy.

title: Shaping Work in the Modern Era: Expanding Perspectives on Job Crafting Across Context (Session ID 901)
speakers: Dr. Lucille S. Headrick | Zhonghao Wang | Cort Rudolph
speaker_affiliations: San Jose State University | University of Houston-Downtown | Wayne State University
description: Headrick, L. (Co-Chair), Wang, Z. (Co-Chair) & Rudolph, C.W. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Shaping work in the modern era: Expanding perspectives on job crafting across contexts. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As contemporary work is transformed by technological innovation, evolving identities, and heightened performance demands, employees increasingly engage in job crafting to align their work with personal and organizational needs. This symposium integrates 3 papers examining generative AI use in job crafting, relational crafting amid stigmatized identity management, and demands crafting under accountability. Collectively, these studies advance theoretical understanding of job crafting as a mechanism of proactive adaptation, self-regulation, and meaningful work construction in dynamic contexts.

title: Standardizing Excellence: Change Strategies for High-Skill Environments (Session ID 42)
tracks: Organizational Processes/Development/Culture | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Cheryl Jackson | Robert Ulrich
speaker_affiliations: GP Strategies | Gibson, Inc.
description: Jackson, C.L. & Ulrich, R.M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Standardizing Excellence: Change Strategies for High-Skill Environments [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In companies where craftsmanship, pride, and autonomy are deeply embedded in the culture, introducing standard work creates a paradox: driving consistency without losing the artistry that defines the work. This Master Tutorial, for intermediate to advanced audiences, explores that tension through Gibson Guitars, a legacy manufacturer known for artisanal quality and long-standing tradition of excellence. Attendees will gain a people-centered framework for aligning operational excellence with culture to drive behavior change and consistency in technically complex, high-skill environments.

title: Talent Assessment in the Age of AI: Research, Challenges, and Solutions (Session ID 634)
speakers: Matthew Bennett | Nidhal Mazza | McKenzie Specht | Yashna Shah
speaker_affiliations: SHL | Texas A&M University | Amazon

Talent Assessment in the Age of AI: Research, Challenges, and Solutions [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session brings together multiple perspectives on the implications of Generative AI (GenAI) for talent assessment, offering both research insights and practical guidance. Speakers will share lessons from recent studies and applied projects examining how AI interacts with assessment content, scoring models, and candidate behaviors. By integrating evidence from both research and applied practice, this session equips I-O practitioners and assessment professionals with actionable insights for maintaining effectiveness in assessments in an AI-driven landscape.

title: The Elephant (and Donkey) in the Room: National Politics and the World of Work (Session ID 641)
speakers: Ian Hughes | Nathan Bowling | Denise Vesper | Lindsay Denecker | Xiao Xiao

(2026, April 30 – May 2). The work-related consequences of employees’ political media consumption. Vesper, D. & Zacher, H. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Uncertain jobs, certain votes? Examining the relations of job insecurity, union attitudes and political behavior. Denecker, L. & Zickar, M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Speaking up or staying silent? The role of political ideology fit in employee voice behavior. Xiao, X., Arena Jr., D. F., Landay, K., & Smith, S. G. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Political ideology dissimilarity, conflicts, and incivility in organizations. This intermediate-level symposium examines how national politics and work intersect, highlighting both politics? Work and work? Politics spillover effects. Across diverse samples and methods, the papers explore how political media, job insecurity, and ideological (mis)fit shape employee attitudes and behaviors. Together, they underscore politics as a persistent, ambient force that influences well-being, workplace dynamics, and organizational life.

title: The Future of Management Development (Session ID 171)
speakers: Mariangela Battista | Anna Tavis | Gena Cox | Jane Wu | Meisha-Ann Martin | Sheri Feinzig
speaker_affiliations: Sirena Consulting LLC | NYU | Feels Human, LLC | IBM | Workhuman | Marsh McLennan
description: Battista, M., Martin, M., Wu, J., Tavis, A., Cox, G., Feinzig, S. (2026, April 30-May 2). The Future of Management Development. Panel Discussion. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The world of work has greatly shifted and with that so has the role of a manager. It is no longer enough to manage workflow, achieve results through others, gather resources, and develop team members. Today managers also need to navigate complex challenges like cost pressures, dwindling team morale and the relentless need for organizational growth. And yet this still does not fully describe what managers face on a daily basis. This panel will discuss what the future of management development will entail and end with a call to action for researchers and practitioners.

title: The Impact of GenAI on Interviewing Programs: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly (Session ID 816)
speakers: Jamie Winter | Kate LaPort | Nathan Mondragon | Nick Koenig | Tyler Mirando
speaker_affiliations: APTMetrics | Amazon | ProboTalent | Walmart | Citizens Bank
description: Winter, J. (Chair), Koenig, N., LaPort, K., Mirando, T., Mondragon, N., Reburn, Z., (2026). The Impact of GenAI on Interviewing Programs: The good, the bad and the ugly [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The impact of GenAI on talent processes is a hot topic. A common target of AI transformation is interviewing programs. Join the panel of experts with extensive experience developing and implementing interviewing programs for an exciting discussion of the most recent applications of GenAI on interviewing programs, where NOT to apply GenAI, best practices leveraging GenAI to enhance your interviewing process; and critical research questions I-O psychologists need to address regarding GenAI and interviewing.

title: Understanding Alcohol's Impact on Work and Well-Being (Session ID 595)
speakers: Charles Calderwood | Spencer Garcia | Michael Ford | Daniel Nguyen | Qiying (Sakura) Du | Shalene Allen
speaker_affiliations: Virginia Tech | Georgia Institute of Technology | University of Alabama | Aptima · Full-time | University of Bath | Kansas State University
description: Garcia, S. C. (Co-Chair), Calderwood, C. (Co-Chair), & Ford, M. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Understanding alcohol's impact on work and well-being [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Garcia, S. C., Calderwood, C., Wiese, C. W. & Sarkar, N. (2026). Work recovery and employee alcohol use: A theoretical synthesis and systematic review. Nguyen, D., Figueroa-Negrón, A. S., Paradiso, E., Klinefleter, Z., & Key-Roberts, M. (2026). Leader behaviors and unit climates as preventative levers for alcohol abuse in the army. Allen, S. J. & Swanberg, J. E. (2026). Mitigating the impact of perpetrator threats at work: Coworker empowerment buffers threat-driven coping with alcohol and drugs. Du, Q. (2026). The paradox of workplace drinking: how newcomer alcohol use builds and undermines supervisory trust. Employee alcohol use is a persistent and complex topic within organizational science. This symposium presents new theoretical and empirical research that advances understanding of alcohol's multifaceted role in the workplace. The papers explore alcohol's intersection with work recovery processes, how leadership and climate can mitigate alcohol abuse in high-stress contexts, the buffering effect of coworker support on maladaptive coping, and the paradoxical function of social drinking in newcomer socialization and trust formation.

title: Walking a Tightrope: Navigating the Line Between Personal Expression and Professional (Session ID 1176)
speakers: Luiza Flores | Shelby Davis | Arturia Melson-Silimon | Silvia Bonaccio | Christine Nittrouer
speaker_affiliations: Colorado State University | Virginia Tech | University of Ottawa | Texas Tech University
description: Flores, L. (Co-Chair), Davis, S. N. (Co-chair), French, K. (Co-Chair), Melson-Silimon, M., Nittrouer, C., Chawla, N., Bonaccio, S. (2026). Walking a Tightrope: Navigating the line between personal expression and professionalism. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores how employees navigate the tension between personal expression and professionalism in increasingly diverse workplaces. Panelists will address strategies such as code-switching and masking, the influence of organizational norms like professionalism and the “ideal worker,” and the boundaries employees draw between personal and professional life. Together, the discussion highlights the need to challenge existing norms, broaden boundary research beyond work–family, and identify future directions for inclusive organizational practices.

title: Whose Classroom Is It Anyway? Reflecting on Dos and Don’ts of Experiential Learning (Session ID 318)
speakers: Kristie Campana | Amanda Barnes | Bree Cox | Jacob Drexler | Paulina Lopez | Zachary Baker-Salmon
speaker_affiliations: Minnesota State University, Mankato | University of Maryland, Baltimore | JupitHer Consulting · Self-employed | Schaefer Center for Public Policy, University of Baltimore | University of Baltimore | City of Baltimore Department of Human Resources
description: King, K., Lopez, P., Baker-Salmon, Z., Barnes, A. N., Cox, B., Drexler, J. & Campana, K.L. (Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Whose Classroom Is It Anyway? Reflecting on Do’s and Don’ts of Experiential Learning [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores the Classroom-as-Organization model, an experiential learning approach in which courses are structured to function like organizations. Student panelists who participated in a graduate-level course will share first-hand reflections on the benefits, challenges, and lessons learned from this method. By discussing both “do’s” and “don’ts,” the session provides practical guidance, highlights common pitfalls, and encourages faculty to consider how this approach can enrich teaching and learning.

title: Words at Work: Applications and Best Practices of Archival Text Data in I-O Psychology (Session ID 946)
speakers: Michael Campion | Hanyi Min | Juan Madera | Lauren Landon | Lia Crowley
speaker_affiliations: Purdue University | University of Houston | NASA (KBR) | Baruch College/The Graduate Center
description: Crowley, L. M. (Chair), Fleyshmakher, D. (Co-Chair), Aronson, S. (Co-Chair), Cohen-Charash, Y. (Co-Chair), Campion, M., Dwivedi, P., Landon, L. B., Madera, J. M., Min, H., & Pyram, R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Words at Work: Applications and Best Practices of Archival Text Data in I-O Psychology [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. A data explosion in the past decade has created opportunities for I-O psychologists to leverage archival text as a data source for research. Archival text research draws on data originally collected for reasons other than answering a researcher's current research question. In I-O psychology, such sources range from job reviews to work emails to social media posts. These sources offer unique strengths and weaknesses. In this session, authors will showcase how I-Os have used these sources to study work, highlighting opportunities for future research and providing guidance for navigating limitations.

title: A Question of Conscience: Clarifying Moral Harms at Work (Session ID 603)
speakers: Spencer Garcia | Joel Lefkowitz | Logan Watts | Rodney L. Lowman | Dr. Shane Connelly
speaker_affiliations: Georgia Institute of Technology | Baruch College and the Graduate Center, CUNY | University of Texas at Arlington | Lowman & Richardson/Consulting Psychologists, PC | University of Oklahoma

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Moral and ethical challenges are a prevalent source of workplace stress that harm well-being, negatively impact job attitudes, and predict employee attrition. This panel of experts will discuss what moral harm is, its effects on employees, and how organizations and I-O psychologists can address related challenges. The goal is to build the audience's understanding of the nature and dynamics of these challenges in the workplace and how they may be addressed.

title: Awards: Experience to Evidence: Reflections on a Work–Family Research Career (Session ID 1470)
tracks: Occupational Health/Work and Family | Special Session
speakers: Tammy Allen
speaker_affiliations: University of South Florida
description: My early experiences as a first-generation college student and graduate student parent offered a lens that shaped a research program spanning work-family issues, mentoring/career development, and workplace flexibility. This talk reflects on how experience and evidence informed one another and what these insights mean for the future of creating supportive, inclusive work systems.

title: Being Significant With Nonsignificant Data (Session ID 1151)
speakers: Leann Caudill | Melanie Standish | Ron Landis
speaker_affiliations: Coastal Carolina University | Lenovo | Wilbur O. and Ann Powers College of Business at Clemson University · Full-time
description: Caudill, L. E. (Co-Chair), Standish, M. P. (Co-Chair), & Landis R. S. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Being significant with nonsignificant data. [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-Os spend countless hours theorizing and designing research studies in hopes of shedding light on interesting workplace phenomena. But what happens when results are “nonsignificant?” I-Os might need to think outside the box because contributions can still be significant. This alternative session will provide numerous examples of how to move forward with nonsignificant data. Attendees will organize into small discussion groups for most of the session to share experiences with null results to find paths forward, in hopes of fostering collaboration and accountability.

title: Beyond the Episode: Considering Enduring Forms of Emotions at Work (Session ID 413)
speakers: Ami Shah | Manuel Gonzalez | Nikitha Mohan | Paul Spector | Heewon Jang | Renee Leger | Sara Stavely
speaker_affiliations: Montclair State University | Tampa General Hospital | George Mason University | Baruch College / Graduate Center | The Port Authority of New York & New Jersey
description: Gonzalez, M. F. (Co-Chair), Mohan, N. S. (Co-Chair), Shah, A. (Co-Chair), & Spector, P. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Beyond the episode: Considering enduring forms of emotions at work [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Jang, H., Kaplan, S., Kuykendall, L., Liao, X., & Maguire, L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). When the Weekend Ends: How Sunday Affective Thoughts Shape Monday Work. Stavely, S. M., & Gray, E. C. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Grief at Work: A Systematic Review of Bereavement and Organizational Support. Leger, R., & Cohen-Charash, Y. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Forgiving and Forgetting? Easier Said than Done: Hate as a Sentiment at Work Research on emotions has continued to be prominent over the last few decades; however, most of it focuses on episodic emotions, moods, and affective traits. This symposium highlights 3 research papers investigating affect, extending beyond brief episodes and dispositional traits. The papers explore emotional spillover to new contexts, transformation of short-term emotions into chronic affective patterns, and the conceptualization of emotions as sentiments. Collectively, the current symposium illustrates exciting new research avenues for studying affect in organizations.

title: Creativity Meets Large Language Models: Generative AI in Creativity Research (Session ID1061)
speakers: Alisha Silkey | Bao Ho | Melissa Keith | Alexis d'Amato | Ares Boira Lopez | Austin Swanson
speaker_affiliations: Bowling Green State University | McKinsey and Company | University of Nebraska, Omaha | University of Nebraska, National Counterterrorism Innovation, Technology and Education (NCITE) Center | University of Nebraska at Omaha
description: Ho, B. G. (Co-Chair), Silkey, A. J. (Co-Chair), & Keith, M. G. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Creativity meets Large Language Models: Generative AI in creativity research [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Silkey, A. J., Ho, B. G., & Keith, M. G. (2026, April 30 – May 2). An interpretable AI approach to human vs. AI-generated responses to creativity tasks. Swanson, A., Linnell, A., Laverghetta Jr., A., Luchini, S., Beaty, R., & Reiter-Palmon, R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Old tests, new tricks: Using LLMs to expand creativity assessment items Boira-Lopez, A., Theobald E., D’Amato, A., Hunter S., Rygg, J., & Elson, J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). When Generative AI helps and when it hurts: Sequencing and levels of analysis in creative problem solving. d’Amato, A. L., Theobald, E., Rygg, J., Elson, J. S., & Hunter, S. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Outside the guardrails: A dual-study exploration of AI-enhanced malevolent creativity. Creativity researchers are currently contending with whether and how large language models (LLMs) inform what I-Os know about human creativity. This symposium moves beyond the question of whether generative AI can be creative. Across 4 studies, the authors examine questions of whether human and AI creativity can be differentiated, how to leverage LLMs to assess creativity and enhance creative problem solving, and what guardrails should be in place considering the potential for malevolent creativity.

title: Demonstrating the Use of Escape Rooms as a Novel Research Method (Session ID 391)
speakers: Takudzwa Chawota | Nissa Gongora | Steven Zhou
speaker_affiliations: Wayne State University | Claremont McKenna College
description: Zhou, S., Chawota, T. A., Gongora, N., & Dickson, M. W. (2026, April 29 – May 2). Demonstrating the use of escape rooms as a novel research method [Alternative session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This alternative session provides a live, interactive demonstration of an escape room used as a novel research method for data collection on topics related to leadership, teams, motivation, and strategic planning. Escape rooms, which became popular in recent years, are real-life adventure games where teams of participants work together to solve puzzles in order to “escape” a simulated game. Authors argue that escape rooms offer a controlled environment to test I-O-related theories while maximizing participant engagement in a way that will attract people to participate in research studies.

title: Feedback Anonymity: Institutionalized Gossip or Useful Information? (Session ID 203)
speakers: Eric Marcus | Paul Levy | Laura Finfer | Nicole Baker | Steve Walker

description: Levy, P. (Co-Chair), Marcus, E. (Co-Chair), Baker, N., Finfer, L., & Walker, S. (2027, April 30 – May 2). Feedback Anonymity: Institutional Gossip or Useful Information? [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Practitioners with internal and external consulting experience will be joined by subject matter experts from academia to review the tradeoffs of implementing anonymous and authored feedback mechanisms in the workplace. Use cases, best practices, and lessons learned will be shared by the panelists as a way to stimulate discussion with the audience. Relevant research will be discussed and presented where appropriate. The focus will be on what the current issues are as identified by the audience – this should be a lively, interactive, and fast-paced session.

title: From Chaos to Clarity: Navigating Leadership During Crisis (Session ID 64)
speakers: Sarah Synder | Tina Madani | Dan Fisher | Eileen Linnabery | Ginevra Drinka | Thor Legvold | Thaddeus Rada-Bayne

(Co-chair), Drinka, G., Fisher, D., Legvold, T., Linnabery, E., & Rada-Bayne, T. (2026, April 30 - May 2). From chaos to clarity: Navigating leadership during crisis [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel brings together practitioners and researchers to explore evidence-based strategies for effective leadership during times of crisis. This will include topics such as fostering psychological safety, transparent communication, and adaptive decision making. Attendees will gain actionable insights to buffer stress, promote resilience, and strategies to guide teams successfully through periods of uncertainty and instability.

title: From Potential to Performance: Individual Differences That Enable AI Readiness (Session ID 126)
speakers: James Meaden | Julia King | Josh Allen | Julia Walsh | Kristin Allen | Tara McClure
speaker_affiliations: Founder @ The Alive Institute | SHL | Walmart | General Mills | Aon
