From Potential to Performance: Individual Differences that Enable AI Readiness [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session brings together researchers and practitioners to explore the individual differences that shape readiness for thriving in the era of AI-enabled work. Drawing on evidence from academic research and applied studies, presenters will discuss what differentiates employees who excel in AI-enabled environments. A discussant will facilitate thought-provoking conversation, focused on practical implications for organizations. By bridging science and practice, this session aims to describe how to leverage research to unlock potential and drive performance in the AI-powered future of work.

title: Invisible Strains, Real Consequences: Breaking Barriers to Support Vulnerable Workers (Session ID 416)
speakers: Jacquelyn Brady | Shalene Allen | Hoda Vaziri | Jazmin Argueta-Rivera | Kaushik Ram | Marian Hernandez
speaker_affiliations: San Jose State University | Kansas State University | University of North Texas | Rice University
description: Allen, S. J. (Co-Chair), Brady, J. M. (Co-Chair), & Smith, N. A. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Invisible strains, real consequences: Breaking barriers to support vulnerable workers [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Workers experiencing vulnerability often face “invisible strains” rooted in stigma, marginalization, and personal hardship, leading to tangible consequences. This symposium examines how diverse forms of vulnerability influence workers’ experiences and how organizations can either mitigate or exacerbate harm. Together, these papers deepen understanding of how strains emerge and highlight the need to foster inclusive, safe, and supportive workplaces through theoretical and practical insights focused on creating a more equitable workforce.

title: LLMs-Based Methods for More Reliable and Efficient Structured Interviews (Session ID 744)
speakers: Georgi Yankov | Josh Liff | David Mayers | Isaac Thompson | Zihan Yan
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessments | HireVue | Knockri | Amazon | University of illinois
description: Yankov, G. P. (Chair) (2026). LLMs-based methods for more reliable and efficient structured interviews [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Structured interviews are strong predictors of job performance but exhibit substantial variability in validity. This symposium examines how large language models (LLMs) can reduce this variability by enhancing score reliability, optimizing interview length, and ensuring raters provide accurate, evidence-based feedback. The 4 studies address sources of rater noise, the relationship between interview length and its reliability, an IRT-inspired method for shortening interviews, and automated tools for verifying interviewer debriefs.

title: Leadership and the Human Capital Advantage in Private Equity (Session ID 932)
speakers: Robert Kaiser | Kevin Impelman | Matthew Lemming | Ryne Sherman
speaker_affiliations: Kaiser Leadership Solutions | Summit Leadership Partners | Hogan Assessments
description: Kaiser, R. B. (Chair) (2026). Leadership and the Human Capital Advantage in Private Equity [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Private equity (PE) is a huge and growing industry with over $15 trillion in assets under management globally. PE firms buy and grow companies to increase their value and sell them for a profit. It is an intensely competitive industry, with few ways for firms to differentiate themselves. However, one source of competitive advantage concerns human capital and talent management. This session features presentations that illustrate the application of the science of talent management and development, focused on leadership, in PE-backed companies.

title: Lightning Tutorial: How to (Actually) Influence and Drive Decisions With Data (Session ID 386)
speakers: Alyssa Marshall | Caitie Jacobson Mikulis | Morgan Depenbusch | Elisabeth Stocking

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This Lightning Tutorial equips I-O professionals with practical methods to move beyond “letting the data speak for itself” and truly influence decisions. Panelists will share research-grounded strategies and case studies on persuasion, credibility, storytelling, and networking, while demonstrating how generative AI can be used to practice these skills. Attendees will leave with actionable tools to translate insights into impact and drive organizational change.

Multimodal LLM assessment of personality and job performance from social media. Li, W., McNeney, D., Barr, R., Prabhu, N., Zhang, C., Capman, J. F., & Boyce, A. S. (2026). Identifying requisition-specific knowledge, skills, and abilities with large language models. Wang, P., Hou, D., Sun, T., & Tay, L. (2026). Investigating the psychometric properties of multimodal large language model-based performance evaluation. Stevenor, B., Anyanwu, C., & McBride, N. L. (2026). Predicting item difficulty with AI. Nie, W. & Dabdoub, A. (2026). Less is more? Testing single-item and AI-based predictors of personality in short video interviews. The emergence of large language models (LLMs) presents many opportunities for industrial-organizational psychology research and practice. However, empirical research is limited regarding the capacity of LLMs to conduct various assessment-related tasks. This symposium presents 5 papers that research and apply LLMs to core problems of scoring constructs and scale development. The papers provide nuanced validity evidence for using LLMs to assess personality and vocational interests, extract competencies from job requisitions, and predict item difficulty.

title: Not Taught in Grad School: Realities of Internal and External Consulting (Session ID 1187)
speakers: Alyssa Green | Mounica Reddy | Jessica Zalewski | Samantha Getzen | Sydney Begerowski | Lauren Zervos
speaker_affiliations: Lotis Blue Consulting | PepsiCo | DICK'S Sporting Goods | APTMetrics | NASA (KBR) | CIBC
description: Reddy, M. (Co-Chair), Green, A. (Co-Chair), Zervos, L., Zalewski, J., Begerowski, S., & Getzen, S. (2026). Not taught in grad school: Realities of internal and external consulting [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This fast-paced panel aims to provide a “realistic job preview” of consulting careers for I-O psychologists, combining panelists’ experiences with evidence from professional practice. Panelists will contrast internal and external roles, reflect on what was learned on-the-job versus during graduate school, and share strategies for conveying the value of I-O psychology. This session is intended to provide graduate students and early-career professionals with insights to better navigate and shape their careers.

title: Pressure-Tested Engagement: What Keeps Manufacturing Workers Connected (Session Id 44)
speakers: Ashlyn Elick | Robert Ulrich
speaker_affiliations: GP Strategies Corporation | Gibson, Inc.
description: Jackson, C.L., Elick, J. A., McManus, A., Ulrich, R.M. (2026, April 30 - May2). Pressure-Tested Engagement: What Keeps Manufacturing Workers Connected [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Manufacturing workforces face unique engagement challenges, particularly when under pressure. This tutorial shares findings from GP Strategies’ engagement data and a Gibson Guitars case study to reveal what drives and sustains engagement when stress peaks. Intermediate-level attendees will explore key drivers of engagement and resilience, discover scalable strategies for front-line workers, and examine how embedded, institutionalized systems reinforce performance within an industrial workforce. The session bridges data, practice, and narrative psychology to drive lasting impact.

title: Rethinking Recovery: Novel Approaches to Recovery Experiences, Antecedents, Outcomes (Session ID 233)
speakers: Didar Zeytun | Ryan Grant | Allie Gabriel | Robert Ridder | Ze Zhu
speaker_affiliations: Penn State University | University of North Carolina at Charlotte | Purdue University, Mitch Daniels School of Business | Baylor University | Texas A&M University
description: Zeytun, D. (Co-Chair), Grant, R. (Co-Chair) & Gabriel, A. (Discussant). (2026). Rethinking Recovery: Novel approaches to recovery experiences, antecedents, and outcomes. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Recovery from work stressors has attracted increasing attention from both scientists and practitioners. This symposium presents novel approaches to understanding recovery processes by introducing new methods for their operationalization and objectively measuring both antecedents and outcomes.

title: Rethinking Retirement: HRM Retention Strategies for Sustaining Work Ability (Session ID 404)
speakers: Alexander Jackson | Donald Truxillo | Gigi Petery | Katrina Burch | Susanne Bruyere
speaker_affiliations: Middle Tennessee State University (MTSU) | Kemmy Business School, University of Limerick | Western Kentucky University | Cornell University
description: Petery, G. A. (Chair). (2026). Rethinking Retirement: HRM Retention Strategies for Sustaining Work Ability [IGNITE+ Panel Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session explores how longer lifespans and shifting demographics are reshaping retirement. Presenters examine outdated “cliff” retirement models, phased retirement policies, retention strategies for older workers, and work ability as a key late-career metric. Together, they highlight how organizations can design flexible policies and practices that retain talent, support well-being, and promote successful transitions to retirement.

title: Saving Time While Maintaining Technical Rigor: New Uses of AI/ML for People Analytics (Session ID 718)
speakers: Anoop Javalagi | Felix Wu | Fred Oswald | Charles Matthews | Nathaniel Voss
speaker_affiliations: HumRRO | University of California, Irvine | Appalachian State University | Human Resources Research Organization (HumRRO)
description: Wu, F. Y. (Co-Chair), & Javalagi, A. A. (Co-Chair) (2026). Saving Time while Maintaining Technical Rigor: New Uses of AI/ML for People Analytics [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This symposium examines artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) methods for optimizing personnel selection, measurement validation, and taxonomy development. Three studies showcase how these technologies deliver reliable and valid results with greater efficiency and scalability compared to conventional applied statistical approaches. The session features cutting-edge examples of empirically grounded and ethically responsible human-in-the-loop AI/ML integration in people analytics for intermediate to advanced audiences.

title: Situational Judgment Test Innovations by and for Humans: No AI Required (Session ID 556)
speakers: Harrison Kell | Michelle Martin-Raugh | Jeffrey Conway | Thomas Rockstuhl
speaker_affiliations: The University of Texas at Arlington | Roblox | Nanyang Business School, NTU
description: Kell, H. J. (Co-Chair), & Martin-Raugh, M. P. (Co-Chair). (2025). Situational judgment test innovations by and for humans: No AI required [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Amid growing enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) in assessment, this symposium highlights 5 advances in situational judgment testing that demonstrate the enduring value of human expertise. Each presentation illustrates how theory-driven design, empirical evidence, and psychological insight continue to advance assessment quality. The papers address construct fidelity, contextual validity, faking mitigation, candidate experience, and large-scale implementation, all without relying on AI. Together they show that meaningful innovation arises from thoughtful science, not automation.

title: Text to Talent: Practical Applications of LLMs for Evaluating Constructed Responses (Session ID 211)
speakers: Cameron Beazley | Gerianne De Klerk - van Someren | Kevin Rutherford | Matthew Bennett | Michael Fetzer | Ben Porr | Yin Lin
speaker_affiliations: SHL | OccPsyence Ltd. | Welliba | Aon | Harver
description: Beazley, C. (Chair), Bennett, M., de Klerk, G., M., Fetzer, Lin, Y., Porr, B., Rutherford, K. (2026, April 30 – May 2) Text to talent– Practical applications of LLMs for evaluating constructed responses [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Although the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) has exploded across industries, using LLMs for evaluating responses is a novel approach in talent assessment and talent development. There are open questions around the accuracy, fairness, and quality of the approach. This introductory IGNITE session showcases practical applications of using LLMs for evaluating a range of constructed response types. Presenters will outline real world case studies that investigate the accuracy, reliability, explainability, security, and fairness of LLM evaluation.

title: The I-O Edge: Supercharge Your Non-I-O Job With Applied Strategies (Session ID 356)
speakers: Breanna Thomas | Andre Hennig | Anthony Auriemmo | Xinyu (Judy) Hu | Tatana Olson
speaker_affiliations: SHL | National Basketball Association | Northern Trust | United States Navy
description: Thomas, B., Henning, A., Hu, J., Auriemmo, A., Olson, T. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The I-O Edge: Supercharge Your Non-I-O Job with Applied Strategies. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. During this exploratory session, panelists will share experiences, practical strategies, and expert perspectives on leveraging I-O psychology knowledge and skills in roles beyond the traditional I-O domain. The discussion will include insights into how to prepare for non-conventional I-O positions, exploring the mindset adjustments necessary when working in a non-traditional or I-O adjacent role, and examining how the value of the I-O skillsets may evolve in response to ongoing technological advancements within the industry.

title: The I-O Practitioner’s Playbook: Insights From Work in Elite Sports (Session ID1041)
speakers: Charles Scherbaum, Ph.D. | Gina Thoebes | Harold Goldstein | Isabel Bilotta | Meredith Damore | Nadine Maliakkal
speaker_affiliations: Baruch College | Arizona Cardinals | The Graduate Center & Baruch College, CUNY | Deutser | Elevate | Hogan Assessment Systems
description: Scherbaum, C. (Chair), Maliakkal, N. T., Thoebes, G. P., Goldstein, H., Bilotta, I., & Damore, M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The I-O practitioner’s playbook: Insights from work in elite sports [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will highlight the application of industrial/organizational (I-O) psychology to elite sports organizations. Panelists will discuss the unique context of working in elite sports, covering topics that range from working with elite athletes and coaches “on the field” to working “in the business” with front-office and other organizational staff. Panelists will emphasize the use of psychometrically sound assessments, share compelling research findings, and shed light on the evolving role of I-O psychology in this highly competitive, pressurized environment.

title: The Mind Behind the Machine: I-O Psychologists as Architects of AI Tools (Session ID 451)
speakers: Carolyn Kalafut | Evan Theys | Justin D. Purl | Kate LaPort | Nathan Hundley | Nicholas Martin
speaker_affiliations: Microsoft | Palo Alto Networks | Archer | Amazon | CodeSignal | Meta
description: Kalafut, C., Hundley, N., LaPort, K., Martin, N., Purl, J., & Theys, E. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The Mind Behind the Machine: I-O Psychologists as Architects of AI Tools [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As AI transforms people analytics, the role of I-O psychologists is increasingly complex and critical. This panel features experts who’ve led AI initiatives across organizations, discussing how I-O psychologists should be embedded in AI development to protect data integrity, uphold ethics, and ensure models reflect human behavior. Real-world examples will highlight successful collaborations with technical teams and the value of cross-disciplinary fluency.

title: Diversity & Inclusion Reception
location: Hilton NOLA Riverside: Grand Ballroom B
tracks: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility | Events & Receptions
description: Join us for a special reception honoring our Diversity & Inclusion Committee volunteers and celebrating a year of meaningful impact, mentorship, and partnership. This gathering is an opportunity to connect, reflect, and recognize the collective efforts that continue to strengthen and advance our community. We are also proud to feature poster presentations from the Diversifying I-O Psychology (DIP) Program Scholars and warmly welcome their mentors to join us in supporting and celebrating their work.

title: Practitioner Reception
location: Hilton NOLA Riverside: Grand Ballroom A
description: We invite you to a New Orleans–inspired meet-and-greet reception designed for practitioners to connect and celebrate. Honor this year’s practitioner award recipients while building relationships and engaging with colleagues in a relaxed, welcoming setting.

title: Workscapes 2.0
description: After a well-received inaugural exhibit at SIOP 2025, Workscapes is back! Workscapes, an art exhibition curated by I-Os and for I-Os, was designed to give attendees a restorative space to pause, recharge, and connect through creative expression amid the demands of conference week. Workscapes 2.0 builds on last year's event with more viewing time, greater visibility, and enhanced interactivity, deepening its commitment to Gentle SIOP values and the belief that celebrating the whole person is what makes our SIOP community stronger.

title: SIOP Talent Showcase
description: An evening celebrating creativity within our I-O community, featuring stand-up comedy, music, dance, poetry, and more. Whether you want to perform or simply enjoy the show, you’ll be part of a joyful celebration of the many talents that enrich our field. It’s not just a talent show, it’s a space for expression, connection, and remembering that I-O psychologists are creative humans too.

title: Continental Breakfast

Academic Assessments in An Era of Evolving Technology: Panel and Workshop (Session ID 956)

speaker_affiliations: Zayed University | George Mason University | University of South Florida | Colorado State University | University of Central Florida
description: Henderson, A. A. (Chair), Ahmad, A. S., Prasad, J., Smith, C. E., & Su, S. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Assessments in an Era of AI and Evolving Technology: Panel and Workshop [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In an era of evolving technology, educators face a challenge to ensure learning and integrity while preparing students for technology integrated workplaces. This session brings together faculty and academic leaders to discuss how technologies are changing traditional assessment approaches followed by an interactive workshop in which participants will collaborate to design I-O assessments that both leverage and safeguard against technology. Attendees will leave with actionable strategies and access to a shared repository of ideas that can be used across academic and organizational settings.

title: Approaches to Conducting Leadership Research in the Military (Session ID 574)
speakers: Ann-Louise Holten | Benjamin Elliott | Ingrid Covington | Ivana Igic
speaker_affiliations: Forsvarsakademiet · Full-time | University of Minnesota | K U Leuven | Military Academy ETH Zurich
description: Euwema, M. C. (Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Approaches to Conducting Leadership Research in the Military [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Covington, I. K. & Igboanusi, G. (2026) From Isolation to Community: Inclusive Leadership in NATO. Holten, A.L, & O. H., Rahbek (2026) Political communication . Morf, M., Igic, I., Avolio, B & Inceoglu, I (2026) What Makes a Moment Matter? Rethinking Leadership Development Through the Eyes of Military Leaders. Elliott, B., Kuncel., N, (2026) Muddied Boots: Rater Source Effects in Military Academy Recommendation Letters. This symposium, Leadership Research in the Military, presents 4 complementary studies exploring contemporary leadership within complex defense contexts. Covington and Igboanusi examine an inclusive program at NATO SHAPE enhancing belonging and resilience through social identity and psychological safety. Holten and Rahbek analyze leadership as linguistic performance shaping legitimacy and collective identity. Morf, Igic, Avolio, and Inceoglu identify pivotal experiences driving leader development and adaptability. Elliott and Kuncel assess validity across leadership raters, revealing context-dependent predictors. Together, these studies redefine military leadership as adaptive, inclusive, and socially constructed.

title: Assessment Consulting Challenges: What Would You Do? (Session Id 955)
speakers: Emily Solberg | Jill May | Joseph Stewart | Kimberly Wrenn
speaker_affiliations: SHL | City of Chicago | The Home Depot

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will present challenging early career consulting scenarios regarding the use of assessments. Small groups will brainstorm and engage in group discussion as to how best to address the challenges. A panel of experienced consultants will then provide their advice and insight on each scenario. Topic areas discussed will include setting oneself up for success, getting results, and navigating challenges. This session is geared towards graduate students and others considering the consulting path, as well early career internal and external practitioners.

title: Building Assessment Center Stimuli With the Help of AI (Session ID 1264)
speakers: Dr. Gregory J. Pool | Gabriel Reyes
speaker_affiliations: St. Mary's University | St. Mary’s University

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This interactive tutorial builds participant knowledge for integrating AI and Big Data into Assessment Center methodology using "vibe coding" approaches. Through hands-on demonstrations, participants learn to create browser-based simulations, collect behavioral traces using JavaScript event listeners, and integrate chat APIs. This accessible method enables I-O practitioners without programming backgrounds to modernize assessment practices.

title: Community of Interest: Evaluating Wellness at Work—Evidence, Privacy, and Care (Session ID 1454)
tracks: Occupational Health/Work and Family | Special Session
speakers: Jenna McChesney | Lauren Moran | Dr. Jacob Seybert | Sarah Haidar
speaker_affiliations: Meredith College | Georgia Institute of Technology | Roblox
description: Haidar, S., McChesney, J., Moran, L., & Seybert, J. ( 2026). Community of Interest: Evaluating Wellness at Work: Balancing Evidence, Privacy, and Care. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizations increasingly offer wellness benefits, ranging from yoga and mindfulness to nutrition programs, as a way to boost employee well-being and organizational outcomes. Yet evaluating the effectiveness of these initiatives raises sensitive challenges around data privacy, ethics, and cultural considerations. This Community of Interest session invites practitioners and academics to share experiences, frameworks, and strategies for responsibly assessing wellness programs while balancing rigorous evidence with employee trust and care.

title: Comparing Modern Qualitative Analysis Methods for Insights in Industry and Research (Session ID 1103)
tracks: Research Methods/Measurement/Statistics | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Sabina Samipour-Biel | Angie Benda | Mounica Reddy | Natalie Luna | Trevor Fry
speaker_affiliations: West Chester University | University of Nebraska at Omaha | PepsiCo | Microsoft · Full-time | Pinterest
description: Samipour-Biel, S. (Chair), Benda, A., Brenner, J., Fry, T., Klema, K., Luna, N., Reddy, M., Roberts, A., & Vinjamuri, S. (2026, April 29 – May 2). Comparing modern qualitative analysis methods for insights in industry and research [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will contrast modern methods for securing insights from qualitative open-ended response data, including thematic analysis, content analysis, natural language process (NLP), semantic analysis, large language model generative artificial intelligence (LLM genAI), and a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) AI tool. Panelists from industry and academia will present use cases and discuss benefits and drawbacks of various approaches, as well as decisions made to balance efficiency and accuracy. Panel discussion and audience Q&A will take place after brief presentations of use cases.

title: DIY KSAOs 3.0: Career Exploration for I-O Graduate Students (Session ID 379)
tracks: I-O Psychologist Career Development/Teaching | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Amy Huber | Briana Squires | Megan Barker | Brittany Lynner | Courtney Shelby | Caelan Kaylegian | Chris Henle | Chase Winterberg | Heather Prather | Jaron Holmes | Kaitlin Ward | Kyle Inselman | M. description: Lynner, B. (Co-Chair), Inselman, K. (Co-Chair), Barker, M., Bryant Shelby, C., González-Morales, M. G., Henle, C., Holmes, J., Huber, A. F., Islam, S., Kaylegian, C., Ward, K. P., Prather, H., Price, N., Squires, B., Stelman, S., & Winterberg, C. (2026, April 30 – May 2). DIY KSAOs 3.0: Career Exploration for I-O Graduate Students [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Back by popular demand, this alternative session will address the challenge of selecting an I-O career path by facilitating micro-mentoring conversations between experienced I-O professionals and graduate students. Specifically, the session will empower attendees to explore the necessary knowledge, skills, abilities, and other characteristics (KSAOs) for various I-O careers, identify their interests, develop strategies for skill development, and seek guidance from experienced professionals.

title: Do Great Teams SYNC Alike? Understanding Collective Constructs in Teams (Session ID 1429)
speakers: Janet Okoroji | Tyree Mitchell | Mikhail Wolfson | Caroline Deal | Nale Lehmann-Willenbrock
speaker_affiliations: Louisiana State University | University of Kentucky | University of South Florida | University of Hamburg
description: Okoroji, J. C. (Co-Chair), Mitchell, T. D. (Co-Chair), & Wolfson, M. (Discussant). (2026). Do Great Teams SYNC alike? Understanding Collective Constructs in Teams [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Much of the team and leadership research has relied on limited static methods to measure team interactions; however, this limits how I-Os understand the temporal development and measurement of collective constructs (e.g., affect, coordination, synchrony, and leadership). This symposium brings together 3 papers that explore understanding of team interactions and leadership through alternative approaches in methodology and theory, which can provide deeper insights on teams. The discussant will synthesize insights and provide recommendations for research and practice.

title: Examining Workplace Ostracism, Microaggression, and Pathways to Inclusiveness (Session ID 1141)
speakers: Cong Liu | Jun Michelle Yang | Huiwen Lian | Lindsay Pyc | Oyindamola Adebo | Vi Phung
speaker_affiliations: Rutgers University · Full-time | UNC at Greensboro | Texas A&M University | McKinsey & Company | Rutgers university
description: Liu, C. (Co-Chair), Yang, J. (Co-Chair) & Lian, H. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Examining workplace ostracism, microaggression, and pathways to inclusiveness [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Most research on workplace ostracism and microaggression has been conducted by academic scholars. However, these subtle forms of workplace mistreatment remain pressing issues that affect organizational health and productivity. This symposium, designed for both scientists and practitioners, brings together 4 presentations from academic and industry professionals to explore subtle forms of workplace mistreatment (e.g., task ostracism, ostracism toward managers, microaggression toward female physicians) and to highlight how organizations address exclusion and subtle forms of mistreatment, and to foster company-wide inclusiveness through global employee attitude surveys.

title: From Misuse to Best Use: Rethinking Assessments for Inclusive Talent Management (Session ID 937)
speakers: Damion Jones | Terence Bostic
speaker_affiliations: CMA Global, Inc. | CMA Global
description: Bostic, T. J., Gfeller, J. D., Jones D. S.., & Webster, I. D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). From misuse to best use: Rethinking assessments for inclusive talent management [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This intermediate-level tutorial examines common pitfalls in applying assessment data to talent management and offers evidence-based alternatives grounded in contemporary research. Authors highlight how misuse of assessments can unintentionally disadvantage historically marginalized groups and constrain organizational outcomes. Participants will gain practical strategies for leveraging assessments to advance equity, enhance selection decisions, and support the development of diverse talent pipelines.

title: Groups/Teams; Legal/Ethical/Current Events/Humanitarian/Pro-Social; Global/International/Cross-Cultural Issues; Organizational Processes/Development/Culture Posters

title: Into the Dark: Zombie Leadership, False Weapons, and You: Our Only Hope (Session ID 1420)
speakers: Alex Haslam | Jackie Sahm | Jocelyn Hays | Robert Kaiser
speaker_affiliations: University of Queensland | Hogan Assessments | Kaiser Leadership Solutions
description: Hays, J. (Chair), Haslam, A. (Panelist), Kaiser, R. (Panelist), Sahm, J. (Panelist), & Viswesvaran, V. (Panelist). (2026, April 30–May 2). Zombie leadership: Dead ideas that still walk among us [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will transport the audience through a cinematic journey of realization that people have been living in a zombie apocalypse. In Act I, Haslam unmasks the undead—executives who look, walk, and talk like leaders but are animated by infected practices. In Act II, Sahm cuts the lights: Executive performance data are nearly meaningless, leaving people in total darkness. Act III brings Kaiser with a plot twist— flimsy weapons are useless against the enemy. In Act IV, the audience joins the fight in a race against time to save the world from the zombie invasion.

title: LLMs for I-Os 2.0: A Features and Applications Masterclass (Session ID 207)
speakers: William Camden | Yizhen Egyn Zhu | Eva Riddervold Sandberg | Bao Ho | Karim Badr | Matthew Bennett
speaker_affiliations: SHL | Amazon | Aon | McKinsey and Company
description: Bennett, M. (Co-Chair), Badr, K. (Co-Chair), Camden, L., Ho, B., Riddervold Sandberg., E., & Zhu, Y.E. (2026, April 30 – May 2). LLMs for I-Os 2.0: A Features and Applications Masterclass [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The field of large language models (LLMs) is evolving rapidly, and many I-Os are still learning how to integrate these tools into their jobs and fully harness their potential. Many do not know where to get started on this journey. Join us for a tutorial to demystify LLMs and learn practical LLM skills. This session offers valuable insights and demonstrations for beginners and intermediates. We'll start with basics and cover many topics, including prompt engineering, using tools and Agent Mode, LLM automation, and even RAG. By the end, you'll be well equipped to integrate LLMs into your work.

title: Moral Injury at Work: Translating a Clinical Construct for I-O Psychology (Session ID 566)
speakers: Ho Kwan Cheung | Matt Crayne | Deborah DiazGranados | Ashley Durbin | Dr. Maureen E. McCusker | Trevin Glasgow
speaker_affiliations: University of Calgary | University at Albany, SUNY | Virginia Commonwealth University | The Crane & The Bull, Corp | Marriott International | University of Virginia
description: Crayne, M.P. (Chair), DiazGranados, D., Glasgow, T., Stephens, A., McCusker, M.E., & Cheung, H.K. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Moral injury at work: Translating a clinical construct for I-O psychology [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel session introduces moral injury to I-O psychology, clarifying definitions and boundaries, translating clinical insights, and illustrating how potentially morally injurious events arise in everyday work. Panelists will discuss theory, measurement, and practice, highlighting prevention, moral repair, and research directions that distinguish value-driven harm from resource-driven strain.

title: No Experiment, No Problem? Causal Inference in Applied Quasi-Experimental Settings (Session ID 830)
speakers: Ludek Stehlik | Marissa Post
speaker_affiliations: Sanofi
description: Post, M. L., Laurentsyeva, N., Stehlik, L., & Ushakov, I. (2026, April 30 – May 2). No Experiment, No Problem? Causal Inference in Applied Quasi-Experimental Settings [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Evaluating organizational programs without experiments (A/B tests) can make causal inference seem out of reach. This practice-oriented tutorial designed for a beginner to intermediate technical audience highlights usage of quasi-experimental methods and econometric/ML techniques - Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs), Generalized Estimating Equations (GEE), Coarsened Exact Matching (CEM), and Double Machine Learning (DML) - to estimate impact when randomization is infeasible. Using R and Python, participants learn to design, analyze, and communicate credible causal evidence in applied settings.

title: Pathways to Successful Aging: Self-Regulation, Knowledge Sharing, and Work–Life Balance (Session ID1004)

description: Schirle, G. (Co-Chair), Choi, Y. (Co-Chair), & Zhan, Y. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Pathways to successful aging: Self-regulation, knowledge sharing, & work-life balance [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Die, K., Baker, C., Schoemann, A., Bowler, M. (2026). Crafting a Path to Retirement: Subjective Age, Job Crafting, and Age-Inclusive HR Practices. Pfrombeck, J., Chen, A., Hon, K.W.H., Li, J.M.H. (2026). The Multidimensional Nature of Knowledge Flows from Older to Younger Employees. Lapp, G., Peng, Y., Taneva, S.K., Chakrabarti, A., Krissel, V., Fujisawa, R., Li, Z. (2026). Retirement Adjustment: Subjective and Social Well-Being of Retirees in Response to The Preretirement Work-Nonwork Interface. Supporting an age-diverse workforce and facilitating retirement transitions are becoming more important for organizations. This symposium advances understanding of successful aging at work and into retirement by examining subjective age, job crafting, age-inclusive HR practices, knowledge sharing, leader uncertainty regulation behaviors, and the work–nonwork interface. Together, these studies illustrate the roles of both individual factors and organizational practices in fostering engagement, motivation, and adjustment across the later career and post-retirement journey.

Minimum qualifications, such as degrees, licenses and certifications, and prior experience, are intended to safeguard competence and quality. Yet these requirements may also restrict access to employment and reduce workforce diversity. This panel brings together experts from research, policy, and practice to examine the role of minimum qualifications across industries. Discussion will focus on when such requirements are essential, when they act as barriers, and how they can be aligned with skills-based approaches to hiring and workforce development.

Drawing on real-world experience, panelists will share insights on implementing AI in deliverables, addressing successes and challenges. Panel Discussion at the 2026 Conference for the Society of Industrial-Organizational Psychology. This panel explores why performance management (PM) and the pay-for-performance reward systems tied to it continue to fail after 70 years of research and reform. Despite constant redesign, most organizations and employees remain deeply dissatisfied with these processes. Despite this dissatisfaction, organizations have no plans to abandon their processes. Panelists will examine the flawed paradigms and practices behind PM, the forces reshaping organizations and HR practices, and how to re-imagine PM as a process that enables individual, team, and organizational performance.

In an effort to guide future flow research and practice, this panel synthesizes and highlights major developments in theory, methodology, and practice from the past decade, aiming to ensure that flow becomes a vital component of our work experience.

Discussion will emphasize building organizational trust through appropriate use case selection, addressing GenAI's limitations, and achieving scalable integration while managing cultural change.

Decent work was first advocated by the International Labor Organization and is now a part of the UN’s sustainable Development Goals. Decent work is productive, delivers fair income, security, social protection, chances for development, freedom of expression, the right to organize, and equal opportunity for women and men. It is congruent with the I-O principles but is rarely in textbooks or taught to students.

The goal is to walk away with (1) a more nuanced understanding of where critical disruption is – or is not – needed alongside (2) practical ideas for utilizing the best of both traditional and emerging methods.

To extend research, authors present papers on elite athletes, emphasizing typical yet critical I-O constructs for predicting behavior and performance on the job.

This panel unites people analytics professionals across career stages, with panelists just starting their people analytics career, transitioning industries, and seasoned leaders, for a cross-career dialogue on how the field’s realities align with its promises. Early-career panelists will share how expectations compare to experience, mid-career experts discuss navigating growth and change, and senior leaders reflect on long-term trajectories. Attendees will gain diverse insights and 4 practical tools to guide their own people analytics career journey.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference. New Orleans, LA, United States. Remote and hybrid work are often framed as liberating — free from commutes, rigid schedules, and in-person oversight. Yet, without clear boundaries, work often bleeds into personal life. Employees can face assumptions from others that they have endless flexibility or “aren’t really working.” During this panel, experts and practitioners will discuss remote work through self-regulation, gendered stereotypes, and work–life boundary management. Participants will leave with insights and practical tools to help themselves and their organizations navigate hidden constraints of working from home.

Organizations are embracing skills-based talent management, but many find themselves “stuck in the middle,” navigating the messy realities of operationalizing, validating, and integrating skills into talent systems. This session presents case studies from leading companies, illustrating how they address these challenges and emphasizing the critical role I-O psychologists play in bridging emerging trends with evidence-based practice.

For each pillar, 2 speakers will offer rapid opposing perspectives: 5 minutes, 20 slides, auto-advancing. The session will conclude with an interactive Q&A.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States This panel examines the application of artificial intelligence (AI) to streamline Job Task Analysis (JTA) processes. AI can cluster job roles based on JTA data, facilitate skills-based competency modeling, and inform various practices. Additionally, AI-generated drafts of knowledge, skills, and abilities (KSAs) enable subject matter experts to validate and refine competencies efficiently. Implications for reducing administrative burden and advancing evidence-based talent management are discussed.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, L.A., United States. The focus on "star" performers is challenged by research showing performance is cyclical and context-dependent. This exposes a "knowing-doing gap": research has shown many ingredients for a high-performance culture, but practitioners still struggle with workable recipes. This panel confronts this by exploring & discussing 3 practitioner dilemmas: the complexity of integration, the nuances of measurement, and the challenge of sustainability. The session provides actionable strategies for building performance systems that are not only effective but truly enduring.

Holland’s theory of vocational choice (1959, 1997) and its corresponding RIASEC model have provided the foundation for research into and understanding of vocational interests for more than 60 years. This symposium comprises 3 papers describing how basic interests—more elemental dimensions subsumed by each of the RIASEC dimensions (Su et al., 2019)—are being used to refine and expand understanding of vocational interests.

title: Beyond the Glass Ceiling: Dominance Stereotypes and Spillover Barriers for Women Lead (Session ID 1344)

description: Deepak, P (Chair), (2026, April 30 – May 2). Beyond the Glass Ceiling: Dominance Stereotypes and Spillover Barriers for Women Leaders [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Women face unique barriers after reaching leadership. This symposium integrates 4 studies examining how dominance stereotypes, stigma spillover, and identity threats restrict women's progress in leadership roles. Authors investigate which stereotype dimensions constrain representation, how individual failures produce group penalties, and which organizational interventions work. Findings reveal dominance (not competence) predicts bias, one woman's failure affects others' opportunities, public failures trigger guilt-by-association, and visible male allyship outperforms diversity statements.

Using Google’s validated design sprint methodology, participants will develop strategies for advancing personal growth and SIOP's strategic priorities.

The discussion highlights timely, evidence-based guidance for leaders navigating hybrid work.

speakers: Allison Besl | Steven Hunt | Gabi Danna | Hannah Markell-Goldstein | Isaac Bazian
speaker_affiliations: Amazon | Crosschq | Dropbox | University of Minnesota
description: Besl, A. N. (Chair) & Hunt, S. (Discussant). (2026). The Science of New Hire Surveys: Enhancing Performance Measurement Approaches [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizations increasingly rely on new hire evaluation surveys to assess quality of hire but face challenges in obtaining meaningful differentiation while maintaining high response rates. This symposium presents recent research on improving these critical measurement tools. Papers examine rating scale optimization, competency selection, completion rates, and timing considerations across organizational contexts. Results demonstrate how thoughtful survey design can address measurement limitations while maintaining practical utility.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LO, United States. Get ready to toss, catch, and act! In this interactive session, participants will use a beach ball toss activity to surface real frustrations with employee surveys, then turn those frustrations into concrete design adaptations to improve the employee experience. You’ll leave with fresh ideas, tweet-sized takeaways, and the momentum to test solutions back at work.

Validity at the Crossroads: Navigating AI's Impact on Theory and Practice [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session examines both the theoretical implications and practical challenges of AI's impact on validity theory and assessment practice. Panelists will discuss how current frameworks need evolution, explore emerging theoretical approaches, and provide practical guidance. The session balances deep conceptual discussion with actionable insights, making it valuable for both researchers interested in theoretical advancement and practitioners facing immediate AI challenges. Attendees will understand why traditional approaches may be insufficient and what they can do about it today.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In this session the editors of the Journal of Applied Psychology, Industrial and Organizational Psychology, Organizational Research Review, and Human Performance will provide advice regarding how to successfully submit a manuscript not only to the journals represented in the session but also advice relevant to I-O psychology related journals in general. The editors will discuss not only how to avoid having your manuscript desk rejected but also what they wish that all authors knew about submitting manuscripts to their journals to increase the likelihood of acceptance.

title: Where AI Stops and Human Responsibility Starts in Employee Experience (Session ID 1328)
speakers: Kamela Lupino | Ali Badibanga | Cat Yaris | Elizabeth Conjar | Jes Beck | Matthew Evans | Nikola Hartling
speaker_affiliations: Mercer | Nike | Capital One | Amazon | Children's Minnesota | Qualtrics | TD Bank Group
description: Lupino, K., Evans, M., O’Kain, A., Hartling, N., Badibanga, A., Conjar, E., Beck, J., Hartling, N., Yaris, C. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Where AI Stops and Human Responsibility Starts in Employee Experience [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Although AI has enabled employee experience (EX) insights to be delivered faster and at scale, AI alone does not create a consistent EX nor its desired outcomes (productivity, retention, etc.). In fact, handled poorly, AI has the ability to harm the quality and impact of actioning. Geared toward intermediate practitioners and drawing on the latest research, this panel, which includes real-world application from organization EX leaders, will discuss how and where to harness the capabilities AI offers, and where human touch remains essential.

title: Workplace Misconduct: IOPs’ Role in Theory, Practice, and Litigation Support (Session ID 1286)
speakers: Dan Kuang | Meghan Lowery | Peggy Stockdale | Dr. Vicki J. Magley
speaker_affiliations: Resolution Economics | Korn Ferry | University of Connecticut
description: Kuang, D., Lowery, M., Magley, V., & Stockdale, M.S. (2026). Workplace Misconduct: IOPs’ Role in Theory, Practice, and Litigation Support [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual conference, New Orleans, LA, United States This panel examines workplace misconduct—behaviors such as bullying, incivility, and abusive supervision—through legal, theoretical, and applied I-O psychology lenses. Panelists discuss construct clarity, measurement, analytics, and expert testimony in advancing psychosocial safety, supporting emerging legislation, and guiding organizational accountability to reduce misconduct and foster respectful, psychologically safe workplaces.

title: “Meaning In” Work or Life Within the Work Stress Framework (Session ID 607)
speakers: Marcus Fila | Sharon Glazer | Brent Reed | Norbert Semmer | Peter McEachern
speaker_affiliations: Hope College | Rutgers, The State University of NJ | University of North Carolina at Charlotte | University of Bern, Switzerland | George Mason University
description: Fila, M. J. (Co-Chair), & Glazer, S. (Co-Chair). (2026, April 30-May 2). ‘Meaning in’ work or life within the work stress framework [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. There is increasing attention in the media and industry reports on the notion of purpose and meaning. Despite the topic not being new, research within the work domain remains limited, particularly with respect to work stress. In response, this symposium showcases new empirical and theoretical research on the role of meaning in work and life in the experience of stressors and strains at work. In particular, the presentations explore meaning as an appraisal orientation or a process variable that has protective qualities to thwart stressors and strains. This session offers relevant, fresh perspectives for scholars and practitioners alike.

AI in Leadership: Impacts on Employee Performance and Well-Being [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will present research-based insights on the growing use of AI in leadership roles and its impact on employee performance, motivation, and psychological health. Panelists will share findings from recent studies along with practical guidance for organizations navigating the shift to AI-enhanced leadership. Topics will include trust in AI systems, effects on feedback and decision-making, risks to well-being, and strategies for ethical and effective implementation.

title: Blurring Boundaries: Advances in Theory and Research on the Work–Home Interface (Session ID 284)
speakers: Arnold Bakker | Evangelia Demerouti | Alyssa Lezcano
speaker_affiliations: Erasmus University Rotterdam | Eindhoven University of Technology | University of South Florida
description: Bakker, A. B., & Demerouti, E. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Blurring boundaries: Advances in theory and research on the work–home interface [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As work and home boundaries blur, understanding their interplay is crucial for sustainable well-being and performance. This symposium presents new insights into the work–home interface. Hammer et al. show that sustained supervisor and coworker support reduces work–family conflict. Lezcano et al. reveal how dual-earner couples jointly manage boundaries under remote work. Demerouti et al. find that family–work facilitation enhances engagement and creativity, whereas job crafting buffers conflict effects. Bakker et al. demonstrate that leisure crafting replenishes energy when family demands are high.

title: Climbers Versus Performers: Disentangling Executive Confidence From Competence (Session ID 249)
speakers: Reece Akhtar | Ted Hayes | Mina Morris
speaker_affiliations: Deeper Signals | Consulting Psychology Journal: Practice and Research | Accenture
description: Akhtar, R., Morris, M., & Hayes, T. (2026, April 30–May 2). Climbers vs. performers: Disentangling executive confidence from competence [Panel discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Many executives labeled “high potential” rise quickly due to confidence and ambition—traits that predict emergence but not long-term success. This panel examines the bias favoring “climbers” over true “performers” with data from global employers, showing how judgment and learning agility better predict effectiveness. Authors share evidence-based assessment and coaching practices, including AI-enabled approaches, to help organizations reward competence rather than confidence.

title: Cutting Through the Jangle: Methods for Addressing Construct Proliferation (Session ID 426)
tracks: Research Methods/Measurement/Statistics | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Valerie Sessa | Jonathan Shaffer | Shiyang Su | Susan Simkins (formerly Mohammed) | Frederic Vinal
speaker_affiliations: Montclair State University | West Texas A&M University | University of Central Florida | Pennsylvania State University
description: Sessa, V.I. (Co-Chair) & Su, S. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Cutting through the jangle: A review of methods for addressing construct proliferation [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Construct proliferation is a common problem across many fields, including industrial and organizational psychology. Although construct proliferation is a recognized problem, relatively few methods have been applied to assess it comprehensively. The purpose of this session is to showcase a variety of traditional and innovative methods that can be used to determine if 2 or more constructs are redundant. Although the presenters draw on their own research topics (team cognitions, job attitudes, leadership, and workplace aggression), these will serve as the “ground” while the focus will be on the methods used.

title: Dark Traits in Leadership: From Situational Triggers to Follower Outcomes (Session ID 181)
speakers: Jasmine Vergauwe | Peter Harms | Greg Haudek | Louisa Vofrei
speaker_affiliations: Vrije Universiteit Brussel | The University of Alabama | Hogan Assessments
description: Vergauwe, J. (Chair) & Harms, P. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Dark Traits in Leadership: From Situational Triggers to Follower Outcomes [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Dark traits such as narcissism, Machiavellianism, and psychopathy are a common cause of derailment in leaders, but their effects are not uniformly destructive. This symposium showcases research that moves beyond static views on leaders’ dark traits to examine their dynamic expression, situational triggers, and effects on follower and team outcomes. Spanning within-person, dyadic, and team-level analyses, the papers reveal when and how dark traits harm or help. An expert discussant integrates insights, offering a more nuanced perspective on dark leadership.

title: Deter, Detect, or Dampen: Practical Interventions for Counterproductive Work Behavior (Session ID 309)
tracks: Job Performance/Withdrawal/Misbehavior | Content Area Theme Track

Deter, detect, or dampen: Practical interventions for counterproductive work behavior [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will emphasize interventions aimed at deterring and detecting the occurrence of, plus dampening the post-occurrence fallout from, counterproductive work behavior. Expert panelists will summarize their academic and applied work in this area, share their vision for “ideal” interventions, articulate important future research needs, and discuss controversial intervention topics, such as restorative justice and electronic performance monitoring. Ample opportunities will be provided for audience members to engage by sharing their own expertise, asking questions, and answering polls.

title: Disruptive Leadership: How Dyads, AI and Shock Tactics Are Changing the Way We Lead (Session ID 1317)
speakers: Tessa Dehring | Annabelle Kerr

How Dyads, AI and shock tactics are changing the way we lead. [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As leadership challenges intensify and technology reshapes the world, traditional models are no longer enough. This fast-paced Mashup session introduces 3 provocative approaches to modern leadership: the dyad model for cross-institutional collaboration and shared authority, smart shocking to rapidly build leadership capabilities and disrupt inertia, and AI-supported leadership for enhanced decision-making. Through real-world examples, tools, and lessons learned, 3 dynamic speakers will spark fresh thinking and practical insights.

Dark Personality in the Workplace: Impulsively Behaving Badly? Pavicic, J., & Benson, A., (2026, April 30 – May 2). Who is in Conflict with Whom? How Personality Traits Relate to Relationship Conflict Between Team Members Li, Z., & Woodley, H. (2026, April 30 – May 2). (2026, April 30 – May 2) Daily Leadership: Coping Methods and the Role of Regret and Happiness A recent report estimated that workplace mistreatment costs organizations over $914 billion in the United States alone. Although workplace mistreatment includes bullying, harassment, incivility, and abusive supervision, scholars argue that workplace mistreatment cannot be understood through isolated perspectives. Thus, getting at the nuances of workplace mistreatment requires various perspectives. This symposium addresses this call by presenting research that relies on different methods—individual differences, dyadic relationships, team dynamics, and intrapersonal diaries—to broaden understanding of the negative outcomes associated with workplace mistreatment.

title: Eldercare in Organizations: Employee, Couple, and Caregiver Perspectives (Session ID 611)
speakers: William Luse | Margaret Beier | David Taullahu | Eunae Cho | Gracie Schirle
speaker_affiliations: University of La Verne | Rice University | University of South Florida | National Chengchi University | The George Washington University
description: Luse, W. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Eldercare in Organizations: Employee, Couple, and Caregiver Perspectives. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Schirle, G., Peng, Y., Zhan, J., & Zhang, W. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Reframing Eldercare: The Role of Challenge Appraisals in Supporting Thriving at Work Cho, E., Allen, T., & Cheng, H.-L. G. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Couple Efficacy to Balance Work and Eldercare. Taullahu, D.B., Smith, C.E., Allen, T.D., & Carrizalez, D.D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). A Meta-Ethnography of Stress in the Caregiving Workforce: A Challenge-Hinderance Perspective. This symposium brings together 3 distinct but complementary perspectives on how employees navigate these demands: (a) employees providing informal care to aging relatives, (b) dual-earner couples managing eldercare responsibilities, and (c) formal caregivers employed in paid eldercare roles. Across these works, authors highlight both the challenges and opportunities of eldercare, examining how individual, relational, and organizational factors shape outcomes such as thriving, relationship quality, and well-being. Together, the papers underscore the importance of understanding eldercare as a multifaceted work–family issue with significant implications for organizations, employees, and families.

title: From Harm to Dignity: Ethical Foundations for Autism at Work Research and Application (Session ID 1194)
speakers: Debra Comer | Ludmila Praslova
speaker_affiliations: Hofstra University | Vanguard University of Southern California
description: Praslova, L., & Comer, D. R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). From Harm to Dignity: Ethical Foundations for Autism at Work Research and Application [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychologists are uniquely positioned to do tremendous good in their autism at work research and practice. However, traditional autism frameworks rooted in clinical paradigms can cause unintended harm when applied without critical examination. This tutorial provides a practical framework for designing research and applications that honor autistic people's worth, voice, and expertise. Through case analysis examining both what goes wrong and what works well, participants would develop skills to recognize dignity risks and create work environments that enable autistic people to thrive.

Five leaders from 6 industries who built people/organizational analytics functions or practices from scratch will share their stories, insights, and lessons learned. If you’ve ever thought of doing this and wondered, “Is there a blueprint?” or “What are the watchouts?”, this session is for you. After IGNITE! introductions (20 slides per speaker advancing automatically every 15 seconds over 5 minutes), the format shifts to a comedic interactive format: questions for the panel are answered then rewarded with questions from the panel in a humorous game show format. Appropriate for all levels.

Across 4 studies, authors will discuss stereotypes about the Millennial generation in China and the United States, differences in normative commitment between generations, generational differences in beliefs about sexual harassment, and similarities and differences between generations in perceptions of career frictions.

Drawing on data from major multinational companies, authors examine the magnitude of cultural differences, whether they reflect genuine behavioral variations or biases, and discuss best practices for equitable comparisons through audience participation. Expert panelists will discuss practical solutions and engage attendees on strategies that balance ideal approaches with real-world applications.

title: Hands-On With Agentic AI: A Practical Guide for I-O Professionals (Session ID 791)

As organizations grapple with AI technology, I-O professionals face a critical need to develop practical AI competencies or risk being left behind. This master tutorial addresses this challenge by providing hands-on experience with Agentic AI implementation. Participants will learn distinctions between Generative and Agentic AI, set up essential tools, and complete practical exercises. The tutorial bridges the technical skills gap among I-O psychologists, enabling them to effectively leverage these technologies while addressing common implementation barriers.

(Moderator), & Acosta, N (Moderator). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Problem Solving Across Industries as an I-O Practitioner [Alternative - Single Paper]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychologists span diverse industries yet share a common educational foundation. This session shows how practitioners can adapt core skills to tackle similar challenges across sectors. In breakout groups, attendees from fields like military, healthcare, government, and technology will explore cross-industry problem-solving. The goal is to highlight shared competencies, debunk role misconceptions, and empower I-O professionals to lead and thrive across contexts through influence, expertise, and strategic impact.

title: Responsible AI in I-O Research and Practice: Examples and Lessons (Session ID 436)
speakers: Yizhen Egyn Zhu | Yi Wang | Michael Campion | Hanna Hlebasko | Jingyi Li | Yuyun Huang

description: Wang, Y., & Zhu, Y. E. (Co-Chairs) (2026). Responsible AI in IO research and practice: Examples and lessons. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As I-O psychologists increasingly adopt Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Large Language Models (LLM), the stakes have never been higher for the responsible use of AI in I-O research and practices. This symposium shares examples and lessons for implementing responsible AI in I-O research and practices, including scoring narrative responses, automated interviewer debrief preparation process, and competency modeling. The studies use a variety of methods to enhance responsible AI usage, such as Chain-of-Thoughts prompting, input and output sanitization, LLM-as-Judge, and human-in-the-loop.

Interest in nature contact and biophilic design at work (strategically bringing nature into the built workplace environment) is growing rapidly across industries and applied research disciplines. This inaugural “Biophilia Effect @” symposium brings expertise from I-O psychology into the broader conversation on nature at work. Featuring 4 presentations at the cutting edge of this field, and providing dedicated time for networking among the presenters and attendees, this session holds relevance for multiple audiences (academic, research, and practitioner).

title: The Fate of Our Work Selves: Employment-Related Personality Change and Dynamics (Session ID 345)

Person-Team (PT) Fit: Dynamic Processing of Inter-personal Behavior and Job Performance. In organizational research, personality has been predominantly approached from a dispositional perspective, treating personality as a static and relatively stable predictor of work-related outcomes. However, recent research positions personality as more dynamic and changes in tandem with work-related experiences. This symposium presents 4 studies that examine personality change and dynamics within organizational contexts, offering several insights on personality change related to underemployment, job characteristics, turnover, and person-team fit.

Labeling the data for training and testing purposes (as in supervised machine learning) may not be feasible in some cases. In this tutorial, I will discuss unsupervised machine learning techniques that do not require labeled data and result in an automated classification of observations or grouping of variables for further analysis. I will focus on a type of density estimation technique called anomaly detection. I will illustrate the technique through a publicly available or a valid AI-generated Python code and synthetic/simulated dataset with numeric (not text) variables.

Authors hope attendees will come with open minds and critical questions to help chart a new path for the future of diversity training.

Society of Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Floyd, T.M., Woehler, M., Cullen-Lester, K., Yang, S.W., & Yu, S. (2026, April 30-May 2). Networking Up: How Targeting Superiors vs. Peers Affects Observers’ Inferences about Networkers’ Motives. Woehler, M., Cullen-Lester, K.L., Porter, C.M., & Gerbasi, A. (2026, April 30-May 2). Should I Reach Up? Why and When Higher-Ranking Contacts Differentially Benefit Men's and Women’s Performance Evaluations. Lee, A.K. (2026, April 30-May 2). Perceived Relational Energy Comparison: Reframing Leader Energy as a Socially Constructed Signal. Choudhury, A. & Maupin, C.K. (2026, April 30-May 2). Signal Consistency in Shaping Team Processes: The Roles of LMX Differentiation and Reward Interdependence. Employees are often encouraged to “reach up” to higher-ranking colleagues, yet such ties send powerful social signals that shape how others interpret motives, fairness, and status. This symposium examines how upward relationships are perceived and their outcomes. Four papers explore networking motives, gendered interpretations of higher-ranking contacts, comparative perceptions of leader energy, and the alignment of leader–member exchange with reward systems. Together, they show that upward ties are not neutral resources but visible cues shaping meaning, inclusion, and cooperation at work.

title: Standing on Business: Young Professionals’ Approach to Solving Leadership Development (Session ID 866)

Standing on Business: Young Professionals’ Approaches to Solving the Leadership Development Problem [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. A panel of young professionals from different backgrounds discuss their approaches to solving the leadership development problem and how the “right” approach changes depending on your vantage point: being embedded in an organization, coming in as an external consultant, or contributing to the research as an academic. Panelists will be comprised of internal organizational development practitioners, external leadership consultants, and current PhD graduate students. The session will start with a hybrid panel-roundtable, then shift into a section for audience engagement.

title: The Leaders We Have Versus the Leaders We Want: Expectations for Effectiveness (Session ID 131)

The Leaders We Have Versus the Leaders We Want: Expectations for Effectiveness [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will present research and expert recommendations on increasing effective leadership in 4 focal countries: the UK, Mexico, Hungary, and Mainland China. The panelists will discuss 2 sources of leadership personality data, including data on the personality trends current leaders in their country have and what personality trends the people in their country say they want in a leader. The panelists will share their expertise on how to bridge these gaps so leaders can engage employees and effectively lead high-performing teams.

title: The Work–Life Balance Myth: Why Static Approaches Fail Across Career Stages (Session Id 449)
speakers: Kimberly French | Malory Bostwick | Heather Prather | Kyle Inselman | Meisha-Ann Martin | Ryan Duffy

(2026, April 30 – May 2). The work-life balance myth: Why static approaches fail across career stages [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will present research-grounded advice to I-O researchers and practitioners to help move beyond static work-life balance approaches toward dynamic, individual-centered frameworks. Topics will include literature on work-life integration across career stages, the problems with current cultural messaging around "balance," and practical tools from experienced researchers and career coaches for implementing personalized, values-based approaches to work-life integration.

title: When the Apology Never Comes: Restoration and Resistance Through Dignity (Session ID 1232)
speakers: Helen Chung | Mariko Harman | Nicole Dekay | Scott Campanario
speaker_affiliations: Seattle Pacific University | Scontrino-Powell | ORS Impact
description: Campanario, S. (Panelist), Chung, H. H. (Co-Chair And Panelist), DeKay, N. (Panelist), Harman, M. A. (Co-Chair And Moderator), Iverson, N. (Panelist), Scott, W. (Non-Speaking Contributor). (2026, April 30 - May 2). When The Apology Never Comes: Restoration and Resistance through Dignity [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. When an employee’s dignity is assaulted in the workplace and accountability never comes, how do they carry on, when leaving isn’t an option? This session explores how individuals resist and restore as acts of maintaining and strengthening dignity, reclaiming agency and meaning in the face of silence or disregard. The interdisciplinary panel of scholars and practitioners blends research and practice to illuminate how people rise after dignity assaults and practical approaches to supporting these employees.

title: AI, Fairness, and the Future of Personality Testing (Session ID 961)
speakers: Tariq Shaban | Deniz Ones | Heather Myers | Kayden Stockdale
speaker_affiliations: Podium | University of Minnesota · Full-time | Traitify by Crosschq | Virginia Tech
description: Shaban, T. (Chair), (2026, April 30 – May 2). AI, Fairness, and the Future of Personality Testing: Balancing Fairness, Engagement, and Validity in Personality Testing [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and computational psychometrics are transforming personality assessment to be more adaptive, fair, and engaging. This symposium highlights 3 innovations: image-based assessments that enhance accessibility and candidate experience; large language models (LLMs) that audit fairness by detecting semantic and cultural bias; and AI-driven adaptive testing using non-linear, forced-choice models to reduce faking and improve validity. A closing commentary examines the ethical and theoretical implications of AI-enabled assessment for transparency and responsible practice. Together, these advances show how AI can enhance psychometric rigor and fairness in modern testing.

title: AI-Driven Work Analysis: Balancing Automation and Human Insight in Talent Management (Session ID 1140)
speakers: Ren Nygren | Dr. Ashley A. Walvoord | Julie Lyon | Melanie Standish | Yuyan (Zoe) Zhang
speaker_affiliations: APTMetrics | Talent and AI Executive Leader | LinkedIn | Lenovo
description: Nygren, R. (Chair), Lyon, J., Standish, M., Walvoord, A., & Zhang, Y. (2026). AI-driven work analysis: Balancing automation and human insight in talent management [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel examines AI-driven work analysis tools that promise to streamline work analysis by parsing job descriptions, postings, and internal data. Drawing on field research and case studies, panelists compare outputs generated by AI alone versus AI reviewed by human experts, assessing their accuracy and usefulness across different talent management processes. Authors discuss best practices, risks, and guidelines for integrating AI with human expertise in work analysis.

title: Age-Inclusive Work: Challenges and Opportunities for HRM Policies and Practices (Session ID 402)
speakers: Dave Cadiz | Gigi Petery | Justin Marcus | Meghan Davenport | Shelly Rauvola

description: Petery, G. A. (Chair). (2026). Age-Inclusive Work: Challenges and Opportunities for HRM Policies and Practices [IGNITE+ Panel Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Advancing age-inclusive work through HRM policies and practices poses many challenges and opportunities organizations globally, including (un)conscious ageism and tackling training and work design needs. This IGNITE+ Panel session features 5 thought provoking presentations from workforce aging scholars that highlight mechanisms driving inequity, strategies to support employees across the lifespan, and international efforts to bridge science and practice. After, the panel will engage with the audience offering insights and practical solutions for building inclusive, age-diverse workplaces.

title: Assessment in the Flow of Work: Charting a Course for the Future of Assessment (Session ID 749)
speakers: Tracy Kantrowitz | Caribay Garcia
speaker_affiliations: PDRI Pearson | Microsoft
description: Demko, B., Kane, L., & Kantrowitz, T. (2026). Assessment in the Flow of Work: Charting a Course for the Future of Assessment [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Enterprises are shifting toward skills-based talent management, creating demand for scalable, objective skill assessment. Traditional testing cannot support the vast number of skills required. Emerging GenAI approaches offer potential to extract skill signals from everyday work artifacts, yet their validity and fairness remain untested. This session explores research using large language models to evaluate real work products, comparing AI and human ratings to assess feasibility, reliability, and implications for the future of assessment.

Rains Wallace Dissertation Award, the Schmidt-Hunter Meta-Analysis Award, and the Joyce & Robert Hogan Award for Personality & Work Performance. This practitioner-focused session brings together leaders from organizations that have moved beyond baseline compliance to build supportive family and medical leave programs that strengthen retention, equity, and engagement. Panelists will share their organization’s policy choices, impactful practices, and lessons learned, followed by a moderated discussion to highlight common themes and unique solutions. Live polls will surface comparative insights, and participants will leave with practical, actionable takeaways they can apply immediately within their own organizations.

title: Big Feedback Energy: Bold Coaching for Bold Personalities (Session ID 465)
speakers: Alise Dabdoub | Erin Eason | Kristy Busija | Marina Mery | Mark Shoemaker | Marlene Dunne

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel offers practical strategies for coaching leaders with emotional volatility or overconfidence traits that can distort self-awareness and hinder growth. Drawing on research linking personality traits to 360-feedback gaps, panelists will share evidence-based techniques for delivering tough feedback, fostering emotional regulation, building humility, and sustaining change. Attendees will gain tools to support bold leaders in becoming more self-aware, resilient, and effective.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, Louisiana, United States. This panel explores the evolving nature of leadership, focusing on the role of talent data in generating leadership insights, and how AI is transforming the landscape of leadership “intelligence.” What data matters most, and how can it be used responsibly and effectively? Finally, authors discuss the evolving partnership between human practitioners and AI as well as the role that I-O psychology science should play in shaping the future of modern leadership development.

title: Challenges to Physical Performance Assessment in 2026 (Session ID 1386)
