Society of Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Dulla, J. J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Physiological testing trends: Demographic, fitness, and technological challenges. Gebhardt, D. L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Fit for physical work or fit for fairness? Job simulations versus normed scoring. Baker, T. A. ((2026, April 30 – May 2). Physical Testing Litigation, Remote Testing, and the Role of AI. Physical performance assessment in 2026 has become more challenging due to demographic shifts and the varied court rulings. This symposium addresses age- and sex-normed scoring for basic ability tests and implications for hiring qualified individuals. With the increase in gender identities, difficulties related to normed scoring tend to be addressed using a job simulation approach. Finally, advances in smartphone technology and AI in the testing process are discussed, along with how they can support an applicant mentor process.

title: Childhood adversity and adult work lives: Implications for I-O research and practice (Session ID 813)
speakers: Andrea Bazzoli | Deepshikha Chatterjee | Baylor Graham | Larry Martinez | Mindy Bergman

Childhood adversity and adult work lives: Implications for I-O research and practice [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) have lasting effects on health, development, and life outcomes, yet their influence in work settings remains understudied in I-O psychology. This panel brings together experts to examine how early-life adversity can shape adult work lives, well-being, and career trajectories. Panelists will discuss conceptual and applied challenges, highlighting the importance of ACEs research in a workplace context, and how trauma-informed and life-course perspectives can play a hidden role in various workplace outcomes.

Connecting the dots: Network perspectives on organizational attitudes and behavior [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This symposium highlights how network analysis offers a unifying framework for understanding organizational attitudes and relationships. Using both psychometric and egocentric approaches, 3 studies examine organizational commitment, newcomer socialization, and training evaluation through the lens of interconnected systems. Together, they demonstrate how mapping these networks reveals the central links that shape behavior, engagement, and organizational outcomes.

title: Cultural Humility: The Courage to Stay Curious (Session ID 1273)
speakers: Kristine Reeves | Alyssa Lopez | Scott Campanario

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As workplaces become more diverse and interconnected, cultural humility (Tervalon & Murray-García, 1998; Foronda, 2020) offers a framework for inclusive leadership grounded in self-reflection, openness, and lifelong learning. This panel explores theory, evidence, and applications of cultural humility across individual, team, and organizational levels. Panelists will bridge research and practice to highlight how humility fosters authentic leadership, equity, and organizational cultures rooted in curiosity, empathy, and accountability.

This panel showcases practitioners embedding neuroinclusion and equity into organizational systems. Panelists will share case studies illustrating how neuroinclusive practices are integrated into organizational effectiveness efforts. Attendees will gain practitioner insights, frameworks, and strategies for shifting from individual accommodations to systemic program design that fosters belonging and performance.

Through live polling, audience voting, and playful debate, participants will gain practical examples and creative tools to explain I-O concepts through relatable, real-world analogies. Leave with fresh ways to connect the science to everyday work, confidently explain what I-Os do beyond our discipline, and inspire others to see and teach I-O in new ways. Cast your vote for the presenter with the best real-world portrayal of I-O psychology in popular culture!

This symposium highlights the nuanced experiences and challenges unique to LGBTQ+ employees navigating professional environments. Authors begin with experiences that affect the entire LGBTQ+ community before narrowing in on the trans and nonbinary community.

This panel will share findings from the 2026 Future Storming workshop, where talent leaders and I-O psychologists engaged in future storming and explored scenarios on future trends. In 2023 authors ran a similar workshop and forecasted 5 shifts that have since begun to reshape talent and leadership, demonstrating the value of future-back thinking. Panelists will highlight fresh insights, explore implications for I-O psychology, and invite audience engagement. This session will spark dialogue on how the field will evolve to stay ahead of change.

title: Has GenAI Taken My Job Yet? I-O’s Role in the GenAI Workplace (Session ID 698)
speakers: Robert Brusso | Ann Kwak | Daniel Schmerling | Anne Scaduto
speaker_affiliations: Amazon | Capital One | GE Aerospace

What’s changed for I-Os with the emergence of GenAI in the workplace [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As GenAI continues to reshape the workplace, I-O psychologists face pressing questions about their professional future. This panel brings together industry experts to demonstrate how GenAI serves as a powerful tool to enhance rather than replace I-O capabilities. Through real-world examples, panelists will showcase the critical intersection of human expertise and AI capabilities, highlighting areas where I-O psychology expertise remains irreplaceable. Attendees will gain practical insights into effectively leveraging GenAI while maintaining core I-O competencies.

title: Leveraging I-O Psychology During Times of Uncertainty and Ambiguity (Session ID 160)
speakers: Mariangela Battista | Elizabeth Kolmstetter | Gena Cox | Mathian Mat Osicki | Sara Weiner | Seth Kamen | Dr. William Shepherd

Roundtable. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA. The current global work environment is presenting challenges for I-O psychologists. I-Os are asking questions and looking for guidance as everyone is trying to navigate this new world. I-Os are living in unprecedented times. How can I-O psychologists manage through all this uncertainty? How can they continue to leverage the science/evidence-based research to do work as I-O psychologists? This roundtable provides a forum for SIOP members to discuss various topics impacting I-O psychologists today whether they are in-house practitioners, consultants, or academics.

title: The Future Called—It Wants Its Ethics Back: Rebooting Ethics in the Tech-Forward Work (Session ID 1013)
speakers: Emi Barresi | Payton Stewart

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As technology automation reshapes the workforce, organizations face a growing cost of ethical oversight: loss of trust, empathy, and accountability in tech-mediated systems. Grounded in socio-technical and human-AI teaming research, this session introduces the HEARTH model (Human Ethics Approach for Remote and Tech-Hybrid) for ethical leadership. Through a micro-case and interactive activity, participants explore how to preserve human ethics and adaptability while collaborating with intelligent systems to lead with integrity in the digital age.
